License: CC BY 4.0
arXiv:2604.04556v1 [math-ph] 06 Apr 2026

From BV-BFV Quantization to
Reshetikhin–Turaev Invariants

Nima Moshayedi Institut für Mathematik
Universität Zürich
Winterthurerstrasse 190 CH-8057 Zürich nima.moshayedi@math.uzh.ch
Abstract.

We propose a program for bridging the gap between the perturbative BV-BFV quantization of Chern–Simons theory and the non-perturbative Reshetikhin–Turaev (RT) invariants of 3-manifolds, passing through factorization homology of 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebras and the derived algebraic geometry of character stacks. We conjecture that the modular tensor category underlying the RT construction arises as the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category from BV-BFV quantization of Chern–Simons theory on the disk, with the derived character stack LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) and its shifted symplectic structure mediating the proposed identification. We formulate seven conjectures, including a main conjecture asserting natural equivalence of the BV-BFV and RT constructions as (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended topological quantum field theories, develop a proof strategy via deformation quantization of shifted symplectic stacks, and clarify the role of 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-Koszul duality in translating between perturbative and non-perturbative data. Supporting evidence is examined in the abelian, low-genus, and Seifert fibered cases. Connections to resurgence, categorification, and the geometric Langlands program are discussed as further motivation, though significant technical gaps remain open.

This paper was prepared with the assistance of AI tools for writing, mathematical exposition, literature search, and typesetting. All content has been verified by the author to the best of his ability. The author takes full responsibility for all mathematical content, including the formulation of conjectures, the correctness of attributed results, and any errors.

1. Introduction

1.1. Historical context and motivation

The Reshetikhin–Turaev (RT) invariants [59] constitute one of the central achievements of quantum topology. Starting from a modular tensor category 𝒞\mathscr{C} (prototypically the semisimple quotient of representations of a quantum group Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}) at a root of unity), one constructs a (2+1)(2{+}1)-dimensional topological quantum field theory (TQFT) assigning vector spaces to closed surfaces and linear maps to cobordisms. Applied to a closed 3-manifold presented via surgery on a framed link, the construction yields a complex-valued topological invariant ZRT​(M)Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M).

The construction proceeds in two stages. First, Reshetikhin and Turaev [59] defined invariants of framed oriented links in S3S^{3} colored by representations of a quantum group, generalizing the Jones polynomial. Second, using the Kirby calculus (which relates different surgery presentations of the same 3-manifold via a finite set of moves), they showed that an appropriate normalization of the colored link invariant, summed over all irreducible representations, yields a topological invariant of the resulting 3-manifold. Turaev [66] later axiomatized the algebraic input as a modular tensor category, abstracting away from the specific quantum group origin (see [9, 32] for textbook treatments).

On the field-theoretic side, Witten’s seminal insight [67] identifies these invariants as the partition function of Chern–Simons gauge theory at level kk. For a compact simple Lie group GG with Lie algebra 𝔤\mathfrak{g}, and a closed oriented 3-manifold MM, the Chern–Simons partition function is formally

(1) ZCS​(M)=∫𝒜/𝒢exp⁡(i​k4​π​∫MTr⁡(A∧d​A+23​A∧A∧A))​𝒟​A,Z_{\mathrm{CS}}(M)=\int_{\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{G}}\exp\!\left(\frac{ik}{4\pi}\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\left(A\wedge dA+\tfrac{2}{3}A\wedge A\wedge A\right)\right)\mathcal{D}A,

where 𝒜\mathcal{A} is the space of connections on a principal GG-bundle over MM, 𝒢\mathcal{G} is the gauge group, Tr\operatorname{Tr} denotes an invariant bilinear form on 𝔤\mathfrak{g} normalized so that the action is 2​π2\pi-periodic in kk, and 𝒟​A\mathcal{D}A is the (formal) path integral measure. Witten argued, using a combination of canonical quantization and path integral methods, that this produces the Jones polynomial for knot complements and the RT invariant for closed 3-manifolds, with the identification q=e2​π​i/(k+h∨)q=e^{2\pi i/(k+h^{\vee})}, where h∨h^{\vee} is the dual Coxeter number of 𝔤\mathfrak{g}. (In the quantum group literature, one often writes q=ei​π/(k+h∨)q=e^{i\pi/(k+h^{\vee})} (e.g. [66]), so that q2q^{2} equals Witten’s parameter; we adopt this latter convention from Section 3 onward.)

Making this path integral rigorous in the perturbative regime has been the subject of extensive work by Axelrod–Singer [5], Kontsevich [51], Bar-Natan [10], Bott–Cattaneo [11], and many others. The modern culmination of this line of work is the BV-BFV program of Cattaneo, Mnev, and Reshetikhin [21, 23, 24], which provides a general framework for the perturbative quantization of gauge theories on manifolds with boundary, assigning to the bulk a BV structure and to the boundary a BFV structure, subject to a compatibility condition (the modified quantum master equation). Applied to Chern–Simons theory, this yields well-defined perturbative invariants as formal power series in ℏ=2​π​i/(k+h∨)\hbar=2\pi i/(k+h^{\vee}).

1.2. The fundamental gap

The fundamental open problem that motivates this paper is:

Main Question: How are the perturbative invariants produced by BV-BFV quantization related to the exact, non-perturbative RT invariants?

The standard expectation, going back to Witten and elaborated by many authors [35, 49, 40, 43, 55], is that the RT invariant admits an asymptotic expansion as k→∞k\to\infty of the form

(2) ZRT​(M,k)∼∑[A]∈FlatG​(M)e2​π​i​k​CS​(A)⋅kdA/2⋅τA​(M)⋅(1+∑n=1∞an​(A)​k−n),Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M,k)\;\sim\;\sum_{[A]\in\mathrm{Flat}_{G}(M)}e^{2\pi ik\,\mathrm{CS}(A)}\cdot k^{d_{A}/2}\cdot\tau_{A}(M)\cdot\left(1+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_{n}(A)\,k^{-n}\right),

where the sum runs over gauge-equivalence classes of flat GG-connections on MM (critical points of the Chern–Simons functional), CS​(A)\mathrm{CS}(A) is the classical Chern–Simons invariant of the flat connection,

dA=dimH0​(M;AdA)−dimH1​(M;AdA)d_{A}=\dim H^{0}(M;\operatorname{Ad}_{A})-\dim H^{1}(M;\operatorname{Ad}_{A})

is a spectral dimension encoding the size of the stabilizer and deformation space, τA​(M)\tau_{A}(M) is the Reidemeister–Ray–Singer analytic torsion [58] (a regularized determinant of the twisted de Rham operator), and the coefficients an​(A)a_{n}(A) are the Feynman diagram contributions at (n+1)(n+1) loops, which are precisely the quantities computed by the BV-BFV formalism.

Several deep obstructions prevent a direct passage from (2) to an identification of ZRTZ_{\mathrm{RT}} with ZBVZ_{\mathrm{BV}}:

  1. (a)

    Divergence of the perturbative series. The power series ∑nan​(A)​k−n\sum_{n}a_{n}(A)k^{-n} is generically divergent (zero radius of convergence). Making sense of the right-hand side of (2) requires Borel resummation or more general resurgent analysis.

  2. (b)

    Stokes phenomena. The Borel resummation of the perturbative series around different flat connections are not independent. They are related by Stokes automorphisms that encode non-perturbative tunneling between critical points. The Stokes constants carry information invisible to any single perturbative expansion.

  3. (c)

    Change of description. The BV-BFV formalism works within gauge theory (connections on bundles), while the RT construction works within algebra (modular tensor categories, surgery calculus). These are fundamentally different mathematical frameworks, and matching them requires identifying the algebraic structures that emerge from quantization.

  4. (d)

    Phase and normalization. Even where the asymptotic expansion is verified, matching the overall phase and normalization requires careful treatment of framings, η\eta-invariants, and gravitational Chern–Simons terms.

  5. (e)

    Functoriality. Both frameworks produce TQFT-like structures (assignment of state spaces to surfaces, gluing), but matching these structures requires identifying not just the partition functions for closed manifolds but the entire functorial apparatus.

1.3. Our approach

In this paper, we propose an approach to the Main Question that avoids the analytic difficulties of asymptotic resummation entirely, instead passing through algebraic and categorical structures. Our thesis is:

Main Thesis. The modular tensor category 𝒞\mathscr{C} used in the RT construction should be canonically equivalent to the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-monoidal category arising from BV-BFV quantization of Chern–Simons theory on the disk. If established, this equivalence, mediated by the derived geometry of character stacks, would provide a direct identification of the RT functor with the (properly completed) BV-BFV partition function, bypassing the need for resurgent resummation. This paper formulates a precise program toward this identification and assembles supporting evidence, but significant technical gaps remain open.

The strategy involves four layers of structure:

  1. Layer 1.

    Classical: The derived character stack LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma), with its shifted symplectic structure (PTVV [57]), provides the classical phase space common to both frameworks. This identification is well-established.

  2. Layer 2.

    Quantum-algebraic: Deformation quantization of LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) produces the quantum group Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}) and its representation category 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G), which at roots of unity yields the modular tensor category (CPTVV [20], Safronov [61], BBJ [12]). This layer rests on established theorems, though the compatibility with BV-BFV quantization (Layer 4) remains conjectural.

  3. Layer 3.

    Topological: Factorization homology [3, 4] integrates the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) over surfaces to produce quantum character varieties (BBJ [12]), which at roots of unity yield the RT state spaces as distinguished objects (AKZ [2]). These are established results; the conjectural step is connecting them to the BV-BFV side.

  4. Layer 4.

    Field-theoretic: The BV-BFV quantization, reformulated in derived algebraic geometry, should coincide with the deformation quantization of Layer 2. This is the main open problem and the subject of Conjecture 6.11.

1.4. Structure of the paper

In Section 2, we give a detailed review of the BV-BFV formalism. We begin with the classical BV formalism (graded symplectic geometry, the classical master equation, and the BRST operator) and the BFV formalism for boundaries (the boundary phase space, BFV charge, and physical state space). We then specialize to Chern–Simons theory, giving the explicit BV fields, the superfield formulation, the BFV boundary data with the Atiyah–Bott symplectic form, and a proof that the BV-BFV axiom is satisfied. We develop the gauge-fixing procedure (Lorenz gauge), the perturbative expansion around flat connections, the structure of Feynman diagram contributions as configuration space integrals, the modified quantum master equation, and the gluing formula that gives the BV-BFV partition function the structure of a symmetric monoidal functor to 𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭ℏ\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}_{\hbar}. We also describe the cellular (simplicial) version of the construction.

In Section 3, we review the Reshetikhin–Turaev construction. We define ribbon categories and their graphical calculus, modular tensor categories with the modularity condition on the SS-matrix, and give the quantum group example 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) with the explicit Kac–Peterson formula for the SS-matrix. We present the RT-TQFT functor, including the Verlinde formula for state space dimensions, the surgery formula for closed 3-manifolds, invariance under Kirby moves, and the extended TQFT structure assigning the MTC to circles and conformal blocks to punctured surfaces.

In Section 4, we develop factorization homology. We define 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-operads and algebras, explain the hierarchy from 𝔼0\mathbb{E}_{0} (formal moduli) through 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2} (braided monoidal) to 𝔼∞\mathbb{E}_{\infty} (commutative), and define factorization homology as a colimit over disk embeddings. We state the ⊗\otimes-excision theorem, compute factorization homology of S1S^{1} as Hochschild homology and of S1S^{1} at the categorical level as the Drinfeld center, and state the fundamental theorems of Ben-Zvi–Brochier–Jordan (identifying factorization homology of braided tensor categories with quantum character varieties) and of Ai–Kong–Zheng (identifying the distinguished object of factorization homology of an MTC on a closed surface with the RT state space). We connect this to the Cobordism Hypothesis.

In Section 5, we introduce derived algebraic geometry (derived stacks, cotangent complexes), define the derived character stack LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma), and develop the PTVV theory of shifted symplectic structures. We explain the AKSZ mechanism producing these structures, the Lagrangian structure on restriction maps (Calaque), and the precise dictionary between BV-BFV data and derived geometry. We then develop the deformation quantization of shifted symplectic stacks (combining CPTVV, Safronov, and Etingof–Kazhdan) and the BBJ identification of quantized character stacks with factorization homology, distinguishing the two levels of the theory: quantum character varieties at generic qq and the collapse to 𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}} with a distinguished object at roots of unity.

In Section 6, we state our main conjectures. We construct the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category from BV-BFV via vertical and horizontal composition, state the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-equivalence conjecture and the Main Conjecture (natural equivalence of functors), and develop the four-step proof strategy: classical identification, quantization comparison (with three detailed sub-steps), factorization homology transport, and cobordism map matching via quantized Lagrangian correspondences.

In Section 7, we develop 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-Koszul duality (the general theory and its geometric interpretation), apply it to reconstruct the global category ℬCS\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}} from local perturbative data via ind-coherent sheaves on the formal completion, and work out the solid torus example explicitly.

In Section 8, we introduce resurgent series, alien derivatives, and Stokes automorphisms in the Chern–Simons context, present the
Gukov–Mariño–Putrov evidence for the Poincaré homology sphere, and formulate the resurgence–Koszul duality correspondence conjecture identifying Stokes data with algebraic transition maps.

In Section 9, we test the conjectural picture in the abelian case (G=U​(1)G=U(1), where everything is exact), genus zero and one (including the pillowcase computation and S​L2​(ℤ)SL_{2}(\mathbb{Z}) representations), mapping class group representations (including Andersen’s asymptotic faithfulness theorem), lens spaces (with explicit formulas and Jeffrey’s verification), and Seifert manifolds.

In Section 10, we discuss the remaining gaps toward a proof, higher-categorical extensions (the fully extended TQFT conjecture),
4-dimensional implications (Crane–Yetter theory and Khovanov homology categorification), and connections to the geometric Langlands program via Betti, de Rham, and Dolbeault variants of the character stack.

Acknowledgements. The author thanks P. Mnev, P. Safronov and K. Wernli for valuable comments and remarks.

2. The BV-BFV Formalism for Chern–Simons Theory

2.1. Classical BV formalism

The classical Batalin–Vilkovisky formalism provides a systematic cohomological framework for handling gauge symmetry in field theories. Before incorporating boundary structures, we recall the essential ingredients for theories on closed manifolds.

The starting point is a ℤ\mathbb{Z}-graded manifold ℱ\mathcal{F}, called the space of BV fields, which collects the physical fields (in degree 0), the ghosts encoding infinitesimal gauge transformations (in degree +1+1), the antifields serving as sources for BRST variations (in degree −1-1), and, for reducible gauge theories, ghosts-for-ghosts and higher ghosts and their antifields in further degrees. This graded space carries a symplectic form ω\omega of cohomological degree −1-1, known as the BV symplectic form or antibracket, which pairs fields with their corresponding antifields. The induced Poisson bracket {−,−}BV\{-,-\}_{\mathrm{BV}} has degree +1+1.

Definition 2.1 (BV manifold).

A classical BV manifold is a quadruple (ℱ,ω,S,Q)(\mathcal{F},\omega,S,Q) consisting of a ℤ\mathbb{Z}-graded manifold ℱ\mathcal{F} with a degree (−1)(-1) symplectic form ω\omega, a degree 0 function S∈C∞​(ℱ)S\in C^{\infty}(\mathcal{F}) called the BV action, and a degree +1+1 cohomological vector field111This is for the finite-dimensional setting, where the odd symplectic form is non-degenerate and the Hamiltonian vector field of SS is globally defined. One has to be more careful in the infinite-dimensional setting. Q={S,−}BVQ=\{S,-\}_{\mathrm{BV}}. These are subject to the classical master equation (CME):

(3) {S,S}BV=0,\{S,S\}_{\mathrm{BV}}=0,

which implies Q2=0Q^{2}=0 by the graded Jacobi identity.

The CME encodes the full consistency of the gauge symmetry. It simultaneously contains the gauge invariance of the classical action, the closure of the gauge algebra, and all higher coherence conditions. The resulting cohomology H0​(Q)H^{0}(Q) computes the algebra of classical observables modulo gauge equivalence, while the higher cohomology groups Hk​(Q)H^{k}(Q) detect obstructions and redundancies in the gauge structure.

At the quantum level, the BV formalism introduces an additional piece of structure: a second-order differential operator ΔBV\Delta_{\mathrm{BV}} of degree +1+1, the BV Laplacian, satisfying ΔBV2=0\Delta_{\mathrm{BV}}^{2}=0. This operator measures the failure of the antibracket to be a derivation and is related to ω\omega by the identity

{f,g}BV=(−1)|f|​ΔBV​(f​g)−(−1)|f|​ΔBV​(f)​g−f​ΔBV​(g).\{f,g\}_{\mathrm{BV}}=(-1)^{|f|}\Delta_{\mathrm{BV}}(fg)-(-1)^{|f|}\Delta_{\mathrm{BV}}(f)\,g-f\,\Delta_{\mathrm{BV}}(g).
Definition 2.2 (BV quantization).

A quantum BV theory is a classical BV manifold equipped with a BV Laplacian222This is canonically defined for the finite-dimensional case. In the infinite-dimensional case one has to introduce a suitable regularization. ΔBV\Delta_{\mathrm{BV}} as above, such that the BV action satisfies the quantum master equation (QME):

(4) ΔBV​ei​S/ℏ=0⟺{S,S}BV−2​i​ℏ​ΔBV​S=0.\Delta_{\mathrm{BV}}\,e^{iS/\hbar}=0\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad\{S,S\}_{\mathrm{BV}}-2i\hbar\,\Delta_{\mathrm{BV}}S=0.

The QME is a quantum deformation of the CME: at ℏ=0\hbar=0 it reduces to {S,S}BV=0\{S,S\}_{\mathrm{BV}}=0, while the correction term i​ℏ​ΔBV​Si\hbar\Delta_{\mathrm{BV}}S accounts for the anomalies that can arise upon quantization. When the QME is satisfied, one defines the partition function by choosing a Lagrangian submanifold ℒ⊂ℱ\mathcal{L}\subset\mathcal{F}. This is the BV formulation of gauge-fixing. When we integrate, we get

(5) Z=∫ℒei​S/ℏ​μℒ,Z=\int_{\mathcal{L}}e^{iS/\hbar}\,\mu_{\mathcal{L}},

where μℒ\mu_{\mathcal{L}} is the half-density on ℒ\mathcal{L} induced by the BV symplectic form. A key consequence of the QME is that ZZ does not depend on the choice of gauge-fixing Lagrangian ℒ\mathcal{L}, up to appropriate cohomological equivalence. This gauge-independence is the BV analogue of the BRST Ward identities and is the fundamental reason the formalism produces well-defined invariants.

2.2. The BFV formalism for boundaries

When the manifold MM has a non-empty boundary ∂M=Σ\partial M=\Sigma, the BV formalism as described above breaks down. The integration-by-parts argument that ensures gauge-independence of the partition function produces boundary contributions that do not vanish. The Batalin–Fradkin–Vilkovisky (BFV) formalism addresses this by equipping the boundary with its own Hamiltonian structure, compatible with the bulk BV data.

The idea is that the boundary inherits a phase space from the restriction of the bulk fields, and this phase space carries a degree 0 symplectic structure (in contrast to the degree (−1)(-1) symplectic structure in the bulk). On the boundary, the cohomological data are encoded by a degree +1+1 Hamiltonian on an even symplectic graded manifold.

Definition 2.3 (BFV manifold).

A BFV manifold is a quadruple (ℱ∂,ω∂,S∂,Q∂)(\mathcal{F}^{\partial},\omega^{\partial},S^{\partial},Q^{\partial}) where ℱ∂\mathcal{F}^{\partial} is a ℤ\mathbb{Z}-graded manifold (the boundary phase space), ω∂\omega^{\partial} is a symplectic form of degree 0, S∂∈C∞​(ℱ∂)S^{\partial}\in C^{\infty}(\mathcal{F}^{\partial}) is a degree +1+1 function called the BFV charge satisfying {S∂,S∂}∂=0\{S^{\partial},S^{\partial}\}^{\partial}=0, and Q∂={S∂,−}∂Q^{\partial}=\{S^{\partial},-\}^{\partial} is the resulting cohomological vector field333We have (Q∂)2=0(Q^{\partial})^{2}=0..

The condition {S∂,S∂}∂=0\{S^{\partial},S^{\partial}\}^{\partial}=0 is the BFV analogue of the classical master equation. It ensures that Q∂Q^{\partial} squares to zero and thus defines a cohomology theory on the boundary. Quantization of the boundary data proceeds by geometric quantization of the symplectic manifold (ℱ∂,ω∂)(\mathcal{F}^{\partial},\omega^{\partial}), producing444In perturbative field theory one more often gets a cochain complex or a space of states constructed perturbatively rather than a literal canonically defined Hilbert space by geometric quantization. a graded Hilbert space ℋΣ\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma}. The BFV charge S∂S^{\partial} quantizes to a nilpotent operator Ω^Σ\hat{\Omega}_{\Sigma} on ℋΣ\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma} with Ω^Σ2=0\hat{\Omega}_{\Sigma}^{2}=0, and the physical state space is its cohomology H∙​(ℋΣ,Ω^Σ)H^{\bullet}(\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma},\hat{\Omega}_{\Sigma}). This is the BFV analogue of BRST cohomology555In a formal quantization, one expects the boundary BFV data to produce a complex of states ℋΣ\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma} together wth a quantized boundary operator Ω^Σ:ℋΣ→ℋΣ\hat{\Omega}_{\Sigma}:\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma}\to\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma} with Ω^Σ2=0\hat{\Omega}_{\Sigma}^{2}=0, its cohomology H∙​(ℋΣ,Ω^Σ)H^{\bullet}(\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma},\hat{\Omega}_{\Sigma}) is interpreted as the physical boundary state space. In perturbative field theory, this construction is generally formal and should not be understood as automatic geometric quantization in the strict analytic sense.. In particular, it selects the gauge-invariant states on the boundary.

The central construction of the CMR program is the coupling of the bulk BV structure to the boundary BFV structure through a compatibility condition.

Definition 2.4 (BV-BFV data).

A BV-BFV pair for a manifold MM with ∂M=Σ\partial M=\Sigma consists of bulk BV data (ℱM,ωM,SM,QM)(\mathcal{F}_{M},\omega_{M},S_{M},Q_{M}) and boundary BFV data
(ℱΣ∂,ωΣ∂=δ​αΣ∂,SΣ∂,QΣ∂)(\mathcal{F}_{\Sigma}^{\partial},\omega^{\partial}_{\Sigma}=\delta\alpha^{\partial}_{\Sigma},S^{\partial}_{\Sigma},Q^{\partial}_{\Sigma}), together with a surjective submersion π:ℱM→ℱΣ∂\pi:\mathcal{F}_{M}\to\mathcal{F}_{\Sigma}^{\partial} (restriction of bulk fields to the boundary), subject to the BV-BFV axiom:

(6) ιQM​ωM=δ​SM+π∗​αΣ∂.\iota_{Q_{M}}\omega_{M}=\delta S_{M}+\pi^{*}\alpha^{\partial}_{\Sigma}.

An equivalent and often more useful formulation is that the failure of the classical master equation in the bulk is precisely controlled by the pullback of the boundary BFV charge:

(7) 12​{SM,SM}BV=π∗​SΣ∂.\frac{1}{2}\{S_{M},S_{M}\}_{\mathrm{BV}}=\pi^{*}S^{\partial}_{\Sigma}.

The BV-BFV axiom has a transparent physical meaning. On a closed manifold, the classical master equation {S,S}BV=0\{S,S\}_{\mathrm{BV}}=0 holds because all boundary terms from integration by parts vanish. When the manifold has a boundary, these terms survive, and the axiom (7) states that they are precisely captured by the BFV charge SΣ∂S^{\partial}_{\Sigma}. This is the cohomological refinement of the familiar variational principle. The variation of the action on a manifold with boundary takes the form δ​S=(equations of motion)+∫Σθ\delta S=(\text{equations of motion})+\int_{\Sigma}\theta, where θ\theta is the pre-symplectic current on the boundary. The BFV structure organizes this boundary contribution into a consistent Hamiltonian framework, ensuring that the gauge symmetry of the bulk theory is compatible with the symplectic geometry of the boundary phase space.

2.3. Application to Chern–Simons theory

We now specialize the general BV-BFV framework to Chern–Simons theory with a compact simple Lie group GG and Lie algebra 𝔤\mathfrak{g}, on an oriented 3-manifold MM with boundary Σ=∂M\Sigma=\partial M. This is the central example for our program, and we describe the BV-BFV data in full detail. The classical Chern–Simons action for A∈Ω1​(M,𝔤)A\in\Omega^{1}(M,\mathfrak{g}) is given by

CS​(A)=∫MTr⁡(A∧d​A+23​A∧A∧A).\mathrm{CS}(A)=\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\left(A\land dA+\tfrac{2}{3}A\land A\land A\right).

2.3.1. The BV space of fields

The key observation underlying the BV formulation of Chern–Simons theory is that the entire field content (physical fields, ghosts, and antifields) can be packaged into a single inhomogeneous differential form. The space of BV fields on MM is the shifted de Rham complex

(8) ℱM=Ω∙​(M,𝔤)​[1]=⨁k=03Ωk​(M,𝔤)​[1−k],\mathcal{F}_{M}=\Omega^{\bullet}(M,\mathfrak{g})[1]=\bigoplus_{k=0}^{3}\Omega^{k}(M,\mathfrak{g})[1-k],

and a general element is written as a superfield 𝐀=c+A+A++c+\mathbf{A}=c+A+A^{+}+c^{+}, where the components and their roles are summarized in Table 2.3.1.

The physical field is the connection 1-form AA; the ghost cc is a 𝔤\mathfrak{g}-valued 0-form encoding infinitesimal gauge transformations; the antifields A+A^{+} and c+c^{+} are differential forms of degrees 2 and 3, respectively, serving as the BV duals of AA and cc. The BV symplectic form of degree −1-1,

(9) ωM=12​∫MTr⁡(δ​𝐀∧δ​𝐀)=∫MTr⁡(δ​A∧δ​A++δ​c∧δ​c+),\omega_{M}=\frac{1}{2}\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\,(\delta\mathbf{A}\wedge\delta\mathbf{A})=\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\,(\delta A\wedge\delta A^{+}+\delta c\wedge\delta c^{+}),

pairs each field with its antifield via the trace form on 𝔤\mathfrak{g} and the wedge product on MM.

The elegance of the superfield formulation is that the BV action takes exactly the same functional form as the classical Chern–Simons action, but applied to the full superfield:

(10) SMBV=∫MTr⁡(𝐀∧d​𝐀+23​𝐀∧𝐀∧𝐀).S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}}=\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\left(\mathbf{A}\wedge d\mathbf{A}+\tfrac{2}{3}\,\mathbf{A}\wedge\mathbf{A}\wedge\mathbf{A}\right).

Expanding this in terms of the component fields and sorting by ghost number, one obtains:

SMBV=∫MTr⁡(A∧d​A+23​A∧A∧A+12​A+∧dA​c+12​c∧dA​A++c+∧12​[c,c]),S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}}=\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\Big(A\wedge dA+\tfrac{2}{3}\,A\wedge A\wedge A+\tfrac{1}{2}A^{+}\wedge d_{A}c+\tfrac{1}{2}c\wedge d_{A}A^{+}+c^{+}\wedge\tfrac{1}{2}[c,c]\Big),

where dA=d+[A,−]d_{A}=d+[A,-] is the covariant derivative. The first line is the classical Chern–Simons action functional; the second line encodes the gauge structure. The term A+∧dA​cA^{+}\wedge d_{A}c couples the antifield of the connection to the linearized gauge transformation and c+∧12​[c,c]c^{+}\wedge\frac{1}{2}[c,c] encodes the closure of the gauge algebra (the structure constants of 𝔤\mathfrak{g}).

2.3.2. The BFV boundary data

Restricting the bulk fields to the boundary
Σ=∂M\Sigma=\partial M produces the BFV phase space. Since Σ\Sigma is a surface, the shifted de Rham complex truncates to forms of degree at most 2, giving

(11) ℱΣ∂=Ω∙​(Σ,𝔤)​[1],\mathcal{F}_{\Sigma}^{\partial}=\Omega^{\bullet}(\Sigma,\mathfrak{g})[1],

with boundary fields (𝕔,𝔸,𝔸+)(\mathbb{c},\mathbb{A},\mathbb{A}^{+}) in ghost-number degrees (+1,0,−1)(+1,0,-1), obtained by restricting the bulk superfield 𝐀|Σ=𝕔+𝔸+𝔸+\mathbf{A}|_{\Sigma}=\mathbb{c}+\mathbb{A}+\mathbb{A}^{+}. Here 𝔸\mathbb{A} is the boundary connection, 𝕔\mathbb{c} is the boundary ghost, and 𝔸+\mathbb{A}^{+} is the boundary momentum (the antifield conjugate to the curvature constraint).

The BFV symplectic form of degree 0 is the Atiyah–Bott form, extended to include the ghost–momentum pairing:

(12) ωΣ∂=12​∫ΣTr⁡(δ​𝐀|Σ∧δ​𝐀|Σ)=∫ΣTr⁡(δ​𝔸∧δ​𝔸)+∫ΣTr⁡(δ​𝕔∧δ​𝔸+).\omega^{\partial}_{\Sigma}=\frac{1}{2}\int_{\Sigma}\operatorname{Tr}(\delta\mathbf{A}|_{\Sigma}\land\delta\mathbf{A}|_{\Sigma})=\int_{\Sigma}\operatorname{Tr}\,(\delta\mathbb{A}\wedge\delta\mathbb{A})+\int_{\Sigma}\operatorname{Tr}\,(\delta\mathbb{c}\wedge\delta\mathbb{A}^{+}).

The first term is the standard symplectic structure on the space of connections on Σ\Sigma, familiar from the work of Atiyah and Bott; the second is the canonical cotangent pairing between the ghost and its conjugate momentum. The BFV charge that generates both the constraint and the gauge symmetry is:

(13) SΣ∂=∫ΣTr(𝕔∧F𝔸+12𝔸+∧[𝕔,𝕔])),S^{\partial}_{\Sigma}=\int_{\Sigma}\operatorname{Tr}\left(\mathbb{c}\wedge F_{\mathbb{A}}+\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbb{A}^{+}\wedge[\mathbb{c},\mathbb{c}]\big)\right),

where F𝔸=d​𝔸+12​𝔸∧𝔸F_{\mathbb{A}}=d\mathbb{A}+\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbb{A}\wedge\mathbb{A} is the curvature of the boundary connection. On the constraint surface 𝔸+=0\mathbb{A}^{+}=0, the condition SΣ∂=0S^{\partial}_{\Sigma}=0 imposes nothing, but the Hamiltonian flow of SΣ∂S^{\partial}_{\Sigma} generates the flatness constraint F𝔸=0F_{\mathbb{A}}=0 together with the infinitesimal gauge transformations on all boundary fields.

Proposition 2.5.

The Chern–Simons BV-BFV data defined above satisfies the BV-BFV axiom (6).

Proof.

We prove the BV–BFV identity in the form

ιQM​ωM=δ​SMBV+π∗​αΣ∂,\iota_{Q_{M}}\omega_{M}=\delta S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}}+\pi^{*}\alpha^{\partial}_{\Sigma},

from which it follows that

12​{SMBV,SMBV}BV=π∗​SΣ∂,\frac{1}{2}\{S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}},S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}}\}_{\mathrm{BV}}=\pi^{*}S^{\partial}_{\Sigma},

where SΣ∂S^{\partial}_{\Sigma} is the BFV charge on the boundary. Let us consider the BV action666Here AA is 𝔤\mathfrak{g}-valued, so the product A∧A∧AA\wedge A\wedge A combines the wedge product of differential forms with the multiplication in the Lie algebra (or, after choosing a matrix realization, with matrix multiplication). Using the invariance of Tr\operatorname{Tr} and the antisymmetry of the wedge product, one has Tr⁡(A∧[A,A])=Tr⁡(A∧(A∧A−A∧A)graded Lie)=6​Tr⁡(A∧A∧A),\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(A\wedge[A,A]\bigr)=\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(A\wedge(A\wedge A-A\wedge A)_{\text{graded Lie}}\bigr)=6\,\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(A\wedge A\wedge A\bigr), so that 16​Tr⁡(A∧[A,A])=Tr⁡(A∧A∧A).\frac{1}{6}\,\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(A\wedge[A,A]\bigr)=\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(A\wedge A\wedge A\bigr). Equivalently, this explains the standard identity Tr⁡(A∧d​A+23​A∧A∧A)=Tr⁡(A∧d​A+13​A∧[A,A]),\operatorname{Tr}\left(A\wedge dA+\tfrac{2}{3}A\wedge A\wedge A\right)=\operatorname{Tr}\left(A\wedge dA+\tfrac{1}{3}A\wedge[A,A]\right), up to the chosen convention for absorbing the Lie bracket into the cubic term.

SMBV=∫MTr⁡(12​𝐀∧d​𝐀+16​𝐀∧[𝐀,𝐀]),S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}}=\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{A}\wedge d\mathbf{A}+\tfrac{1}{6}\mathbf{A}\wedge[\mathbf{A},\mathbf{A}]\right),

where 𝐀=c+A+A++c+∈Ω∙​(M,𝔤)​[1]\mathbf{A}=c+A+A^{+}+c^{+}\in\Omega^{\bullet}(M,\mathfrak{g})[1] is the BV superfield. Its variation is

δ​SMBV=∫MTr⁡(δ​𝐀∧(d​𝐀+12​[𝐀,𝐀]))+∫∂MTr⁡(12​𝐀∧δ​𝐀).\delta S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}}=\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\left(\delta\mathbf{A}\wedge\bigl(d\mathbf{A}+\tfrac{1}{2}[\mathbf{A},\mathbf{A}]\bigr)\right)+\int_{\partial M}\operatorname{Tr}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbf{A}\wedge\delta\mathbf{A}\right).

Thus, the bulk Euler–Lagrange expression is

d​𝐀+12​[𝐀,𝐀],d\mathbf{A}+\frac{1}{2}[\mathbf{A},\mathbf{A}],

and the boundary term is the pullback of the boundary one-form

αΣ∂=12​∫ΣTr⁡(𝐀|Σ∧δ​𝐀|Σ).\alpha^{\partial}_{\Sigma}=\frac{1}{2}\int_{\Sigma}\operatorname{Tr}\!\left(\mathbf{A}|_{\Sigma}\wedge\delta\mathbf{A}|_{\Sigma}\right).

The bulk BV symplectic form is

ωM=∫MTr⁡(δ​A∧δ​A++δ​c∧δ​c+),\omega_{M}=\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(\delta A\wedge\delta A^{+}+\delta c\wedge\delta c^{+}\bigr),

or, equivalently, in superfield notation,

ωM=12​∫MTr⁡(δ​𝐀∧δ​𝐀).\omega_{M}=\frac{1}{2}\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}(\delta\mathbf{A}\land\delta\mathbf{A}).

Hence, the Hamiltonian vector field QMQ_{M} generated by SMBVS_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}} is determined by

ιQM​ωM=δ​SMBV+π∗​αΣ∂,\iota_{Q_{M}}\omega_{M}=\delta S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}}+\pi^{*}\alpha^{\partial}_{\Sigma},

that is,

QM​𝐀=d​𝐀+12​[𝐀,𝐀].Q_{M}\mathbf{A}=d\mathbf{A}+\frac{1}{2}[\mathbf{A},\mathbf{A}].

Restricting to the boundary gives the induced cohomological vector field

QΣ∂​𝐀|Σ=d​𝐀|Σ+12​[𝐀|Σ,𝐀|Σ].Q^{\partial}_{\Sigma}\mathbf{A}|_{\Sigma}=d\mathbf{A}|_{\Sigma}+\frac{1}{2}\Big[\mathbf{A}|_{\Sigma},\mathbf{A}|_{\Sigma}\Big].

Writing

𝐀|Σ=𝕔+𝔸+𝔸+,\mathbf{A}|_{\Sigma}=\mathbb{c}+\mathbb{A}+\mathbb{A}^{+},

with

𝕔∈Ω0​(Σ,𝔤),𝔸∈Ω1​(Σ,𝔤),𝔸+∈Ω2​(Σ,𝔤),\mathbb{c}\in\Omega^{0}(\Sigma,\mathfrak{g}),\qquad\mathbb{A}\in\Omega^{1}(\Sigma,\mathfrak{g}),\qquad\mathbb{A}^{+}\in\Omega^{2}(\Sigma,\mathfrak{g}),

one obtains

QΣ∂​𝔸=d𝔸​𝕔,QΣ∂​𝕔=12​[𝕔,𝕔],QΣ∂​𝔸+=F𝔸+[𝔸+,𝕔],Q^{\partial}_{\Sigma}\mathbb{A}=d_{\mathbb{A}}\mathbb{c},\qquad Q^{\partial}_{\Sigma}\mathbb{c}=\frac{1}{2}[\mathbb{c},\mathbb{c}],\qquad Q^{\partial}_{\Sigma}\mathbb{A}^{+}=F_{\mathbb{A}}+[\mathbb{A}^{+},\mathbb{c}],

up to the overall sign convention for the BV/BFV bracket.

The boundary BFV symplectic form is

ωΣ∂=∫ΣTr⁡(δ​𝔸∧δ​𝔸+δ​𝕔∧δ​𝔸+),\omega^{\partial}_{\Sigma}=\int_{\Sigma}\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(\delta\mathbb{A}\land\delta\mathbb{A}+\delta\mathbb{c}\land\delta\mathbb{A}^{+}\bigr),

and the corresponding BFV charge is the Hamiltonian generating QΣ∂Q^{\partial}_{\Sigma}, namely

SΣ∂=∫ΣTr⁡(𝕔∧F𝔸+12​𝔸+∧[𝕔,𝕔]),S^{\partial}_{\Sigma}=\int_{\Sigma}\operatorname{Tr}\!\left(\mathbb{c}\land F_{\mathbb{A}}+\tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbb{A}^{+}\land[\mathbb{c},\mathbb{c}]\right),

again up to the global sign convention.

Therefore, the boundary defect of the bulk BV action is exactly the pullback of the BFV data on Σ\Sigma, and the BV–BFV compatibility identity holds:

ιQM​ωM=δ​SMBV+π∗​αΣ∂.\iota_{Q_{M}}\omega_{M}=\delta S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}}+\pi^{*}\alpha^{\partial}_{\Sigma}.

Equivalently, we get

12​{SMBV,SMBV}BV=π∗​SΣ∂.\frac{1}{2}\{S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}},S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}}\}_{\mathrm{BV}}=\pi^{*}S^{\partial}_{\Sigma}.

This proves the claim. ∎

2.4. Gauge-fixing and the perturbative expansion

To extract concrete numbers from the BV partition function (5), one must choose a gauge-fixing Lagrangian ℒ⊂ℱM\mathcal{L}\subset\mathcal{F}_{M}. Although Chern–Simons theory is topological, the gauge-fixing procedure requires an auxiliary Riemannian metric gg on MM, which will not appear in the final answer.

The perturbative approach proceeds by expanding around a classical solution (a flat connection A0A_{0} satisfying FA0=0F_{A_{0}}=0) and organizing the resulting Feynman diagram expansion as a formal power series in ℏ\hbar.

Construction 2.6 (Lorenz gauge-fixing).

To define perturbation theory around a flat background connection A0A_{0}, one sets

A=A0+a,FA0=0,A=A_{0}+a,\qquad F_{A_{0}}=0,

where a∈Ω1​(M,𝔤)a\in\Omega^{1}(M,\mathfrak{g}) is the fluctuation field. Explicit gauge-fixing requires passing from the minimal BV space of fields to the non-minimal one by adjoining an antighost c¯\bar{c}, a Nakanishi–Lautrup field bb, and their antifields:

c¯∈Ω0​(M,𝔤),gh​(c¯)=−1,\bar{c}\in\Omega^{0}(M,\mathfrak{g}),\qquad\mathrm{gh}(\bar{c})=-1,
b∈Ω0​(M,𝔤),gh​(b)=0.b\in\Omega^{0}(M,\mathfrak{g}),\qquad\mathrm{gh}(b)=0.

The non-minimal action is

Snm=SMBV+∫MTr⁡(c¯+∧b).S_{\mathrm{nm}}=S_{M}^{\mathrm{BV}}+\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}(\bar{c}^{+}\wedge b).

Fix an auxiliary Riemannian metric on MM. The Lorenz gauge condition

dA0∗​a=0d_{A_{0}}^{*}a=0

is implemented by the gauge-fixing fermion

Ψ=∫MTr⁡(c¯∧dA0∗​a).\Psi=\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}(\bar{c}\wedge d_{A_{0}}^{*}a).

The gauge-fixed Lagrangian is defined by the usual BV prescription

Φ+=δ​Ψδ​Φ\Phi^{+}=\frac{\delta\Psi}{\delta\Phi}

for all fields Φ\Phi. In particular, we have

A+=δ​Ψδ​a,c¯+=δ​Ψδ​c¯=dA0∗​a,c+=0,b+=0.A^{+}=\frac{\delta\Psi}{\delta a},\qquad\bar{c}^{+}=\frac{\delta\Psi}{\delta\bar{c}}=d_{A_{0}}^{*}a,\qquad c^{+}=0,\qquad b^{+}=0.

Substituting these conditions into the non-minimal action yields the usual Lorenz-gauge-fixed action. Up to cubic order in the fluctuations one obtains

(14) Sgf=CS​(A0)+12​∫MTr⁡(a∧dA0​a)+∫MTr⁡(b∧dA0∗​a)+∫MTr⁡(c¯∧ΔA0​c)+16​∫MTr⁡(a∧[a,a])+∫MTr⁡(c¯∧[a,c]),S_{\mathrm{gf}}=\mathrm{CS}(A_{0})+\frac{1}{2}\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}(a\wedge d_{A_{0}}a)+\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(b\wedge d_{A_{0}}^{*}a\bigr)\\ +\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(\bar{c}\wedge\Delta_{A_{0}}c\bigr)+\frac{1}{6}\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(a\wedge[a,a]\bigr)+\int_{M}\operatorname{Tr}\bigl(\bar{c}\wedge[a,c]\bigr),

where

ΔA0=dA0​dA0∗+dA0∗​dA0\Delta_{A_{0}}=d_{A_{0}}d_{A_{0}}^{*}+d_{A_{0}}^{*}d_{A_{0}}

is the covariant Laplacian. Integrating out bb imposes the Lorenz gauge condition

dA0∗​a=0.d_{A_{0}}^{*}a=0.

The quadratic part determines the propagators, while the cubic terms determine the interaction vertices. The first term is the classical Chern–Simons invariant of the flat connection. The ghost propagator is governed by the Green’s operator of ΔA0\Delta_{A_{0}}, whereas the gauge-field propagator arises from the first-order Chern–Simons kinetic operator together with the gauge condition. The interaction vertices are determined by the cubic terms in (14). Higher-order terms in ℏ\hbar arise from iterating this cubic vertex in Feynman diagrams.

In the perturbative BV–BFV formalism, one must in general also account for residual fields arising from zero modes. We suppress these technicalities here and state only the standard formal structure of the perturbative expansion around an acyclic flat background. Carrying out the Gaussian integral over the quadratic part and summing the perturbative corrections gives the following result.

Proposition 2.7 (Structure of the perturbative expansion).

Formally, the perturbative partition function of Chern–Simons theory around a flat connection A0A_{0} has the form

(15) ZA0pert​(M)=ei​k​CS​(A0)⋅τA0​(M)1/2⋅ei​π4​η​(A0)⋅exp⁡(∑n≥1ℏn​In​(A0,M)),Z^{\mathrm{pert}}_{A_{0}}(M)=e^{ik\,\mathrm{CS}(A_{0})}\cdot\tau_{A_{0}}(M)^{1/2}\cdot e^{\frac{i\pi}{4}\eta(A_{0})}\cdot\exp\!\left(\sum_{n\geq 1}\hbar^{n}I_{n}(A_{0},M)\right),

where τA0​(M)\tau_{A_{0}}(M) is the Ray–Singer torsion [58], η​(A0)\eta(A_{0}) is the eta-invariant of the relevant odd-signature operator, and the coefficients In​(A0,M)I_{n}(A_{0},M) are given by sums of compactified configuration-space integrals associated to connected trivalent Feynman diagrams.

Equation (15) should be understood as the standard formal structure of the perturbative expansion rather than as a literal consequence of the naive finite-dimensional Gaussian integral.

Remark 2.8 (Structure of the Feynman diagrams).

Since the only interaction vertex is cubic (trivalent), the Feynman diagrams contributing to InI_{n} are trivalent graphs Γ\Gamma with 2​n2n vertices and 3​n3n edges, at loop order n+1n+1. The contribution of each graph is not an ordinary integral but a configuration space integral: one integrates a product of propagator forms over a compactified configuration space of points in MM labeled by the vertices of Γ\Gamma. Explicitly, we have

(16) IΓ​(A0,M)=∫CΓ​(M)⋀e∈E​(Γ)ωe​(xs​(e),xt​(e)),I_{\Gamma}(A_{0},M)=\int_{C_{\Gamma}(M)}\bigwedge_{e\in E(\Gamma)}\omega_{e}(x_{s(e)},\,x_{t(e)}),

where CΓ​(M)C_{\Gamma}(M) is the Axelrod–Singer compactification of the configuration space of |V​(Γ)||V(\Gamma)| distinct points in MM, and ωe\omega_{e} is a propagator form (the integral kernel of the Green’s operator of ΔA0\Delta_{A_{0}}) associated to the edge ee connecting the source vertex s​(e)s(e) to the target vertex t​(e)t(e). The compactification is essential: it resolves the singularities that arise when two or more points collide, rendering the integral well-defined. This regularization procedure, introduced by Axelrod–Singer [5] and independently by Kontsevich [51], is one of the technical cornerstones of perturbative Chern–Simons theory (see also [22]).

2.5. The modified quantum master equation

Proposition 2.9 (CMR [23]).

The effective BV action SMeffS_{M}^{\mathrm{eff}} satisfies the modified quantum master equation:

(17) (ℏ2​ΔM+Ω^Σ)​ei​SMeff/ℏ=0,(\hbar^{2}\Delta_{M}+\hat{\Omega}_{\Sigma})\,e^{iS_{M}^{\mathrm{eff}}/\hbar}=0,

where ΔM\Delta_{M} is the BV Laplacian on residual fields and Ω^Σ\hat{\Omega}_{\Sigma} is the quantized BFV operator.

Remark 2.10.

The mQME says that ZBV​(M)=ei​SMeff/ℏZ_{\mathrm{BV}}(M)=e^{iS_{M}^{\mathrm{eff}}/\hbar} is a cocycle in the BFV cohomology of the boundary, i.e., ZBV​(M)∈H∙​(Ω^Σ)=ℋΣphysZ_{\mathrm{BV}}(M)\in H^{\bullet}(\hat{\Omega}_{\Sigma})=\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma}^{\mathrm{phys}}. The partition function is a physical state associated to the boundary.

2.6. Functoriality and gluing

A central virtue of the BV-BFV formalism is that it is designed from the outset to be compatible with cutting and gluing of manifolds. This is what gives the partition function the structure of a topological quantum field theory, and it is the property that will allow us to compare it with the RT functor.

Suppose a closed 3-manifold MM is decomposed as M=M1∪ΣM2M=M_{1}\cup_{\Sigma}M_{2} by cutting along an embedded surface Σ\Sigma. Each piece MiM_{i} is a cobordism with Σ\Sigma as a boundary component, and the BV-BFV formalism assigns to each a partition function ZBV​(Mi)Z_{\mathrm{BV}}(M_{i}) living in the BFV state space ℋΣ\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma} of the cutting surface. The partition function of the glued manifold is then recovered by pairing these two states.

Theorem 2.11 (CMR gluing [23, 24]).

Let M=M1∪ΣM2M=M_{1}\cup_{\Sigma}M_{2} be a decomposition along a common boundary component Σ\Sigma. Then the BV-BFV partition function satisfies the gluing formula

(18) ZBV​(M)=⟨ZBV​(M1),ZBV​(M2)⟩ℋΣ,Z_{\mathrm{BV}}(M)=\langle Z_{\mathrm{BV}}(M_{1}),\,Z_{\mathrm{BV}}(M_{2})\rangle_{\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma}},

where ⟨−,−⟩ℋΣ\langle-,\,-\rangle_{\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma}} is the pairing on the BFV state space induced by the symplectic structure on the boundary. At the level of the path integral, this pairing is realized as a fiber BV integral over the boundary fields:

(19) ZBV​(M1∪ΣM2)=∫ℒΣZBV​(M1)⋅ZBV​(M2)​μΣ,Z_{\mathrm{BV}}(M_{1}\cup_{\Sigma}M_{2})=\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\Sigma}}Z_{\mathrm{BV}}(M_{1})\cdot Z_{\mathrm{BV}}(M_{2})\;\mu_{\Sigma},

where ℒΣ\mathcal{L}_{\Sigma} is a Lagrangian submanifold in the BFV phase space of Σ\Sigma and μΣ\mu_{\Sigma} is the induced measure.

The gluing formula endows the BV-BFV partition function with the algebraic structure of a TQFT. Surfaces are assigned state spaces, cobordisms are assigned vectors (or linear maps) in those state spaces, and composition of cobordisms corresponds to the pairing (18).

Corollary 2.12.

The BV-BFV partition function defines a symmetric monoidal functor

(20) ZBV:𝐁𝐨𝐫𝐝3or⟶𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭ℏ,Z_{\mathrm{BV}}:\mathrm{\mathbf{Bord}}_{3}^{\mathrm{or}}\longrightarrow\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}_{\hbar},

where 𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭ℏ=𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}_{\hbar}=\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}_{\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!]} denotes the category of topological vector spaces over the ring of formal power series in ℏ\hbar. This is an ordinary (non-extended) TQFT functor. It assigns vector spaces to surfaces and formal power series to closed 3-manifolds, but does not assign categorical data to circles. One of the goals of our program is to promote this to a (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended functor by extracting the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} at the circle level (Section 6).

Remark 2.13 (Perturbative nature).

It is important to emphasize that the functor ZBVZ_{\mathrm{BV}} is perturbative. Its values are formal power series in ℏ\hbar, not complex numbers. The partition function of a closed 3-manifold is an element of ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!], and the state space of a surface is a module over this ring. In particular, ZBVZ_{\mathrm{BV}} cannot directly “see” non-perturbative phenomena such as tunneling between distinct flat connections. The fundamental question motivating this paper is precisely how to de-formalize this functor, i.e., how to pass from the formal power series ring ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!] to ℂ\mathbb{C} by specializing ℏ\hbar to a concrete value, and whether the result agrees with the RT invariants.

2.7. The BV-BFV formalism on simplicial decompositions

An important refinement of the CMR program, developed in [24], is the construction of the BV-BFV theory on simplicial (or more generally CW) decompositions of MM. This “cellular” version of the theory replaces the infinite-dimensional spaces of differential forms with finite-dimensional combinatorial data, making the partition function concretely computable while preserving the essential algebraic structure.

Construction 2.14 (Cellular BV-BFV [24]).

Let Δ\Delta be a triangulation of MM. The cellular BV-BFV construction assigns a finite-dimensional BV-BFV theory to each simplex: the BV space associated to a 3-simplex is 𝔤​[1]⊕𝔤⊕𝔤​[−1]⊕𝔤​[−2]\mathfrak{g}[1]\oplus\mathfrak{g}\oplus\mathfrak{g}[-1]\oplus\mathfrak{g}[-2] (one copy of the Lie algebra in each degree, mirroring the four components of the superfield), and the BFV space associated to a 2-simplex is 𝔤​[1]⊕𝔤⊕𝔤​[−1]\mathfrak{g}[1]\oplus\mathfrak{g}\oplus\mathfrak{g}[-1] (the boundary truncation). The partition function of MM is then computed by assembling the simplices: one performs an iterated BV pushforward, gluing the finite-dimensional BV-BFV data of adjacent simplices step by step according to the combinatorics of Δ\Delta.

The key advantage of this approach is computability. Each step involves a finite-dimensional integral rather than a functional integral, and the entire computation reduces to a sequence of linear-algebraic operations on copies of 𝔤\mathfrak{g}. The price one pays is an apparent dependence on the triangulation Δ\Delta. However, the partition function is in fact independent of this choice. This follows from a cellular version of the modified quantum master equation, together with the observation that any two triangulations of MM are related by a finite sequence of Pachner moves (bistellar flips), and the BV-BFV partition function is invariant under each such move. This cellular construction will play an important role in Section 6, where we compare it with the factorization homology approach to computing topological invariants. See also [22] for the semiclassical perspective on this construction and [26, 27, 45] for the factorization algebra viewpoint.

3. The Reshetikhin–Turaev Construction

We now turn to the other side of the bridge, namely, the Reshetikhin–Turaev invariants. These are constructed purely algebraically, starting from a modular tensor category, and produce a fully functorial (2+1)(2+1)-dimensional TQFT. Our goal in this section is to describe the construction in enough detail to make the comparison with the BV-BFV formalism precise.

3.1. Ribbon categories and graphical calculus

The algebraic input for the RT construction is a ribbon category, i.e., a monoidal category with enough additional structure (braiding, twist, duality) to support a topological calculus for morphisms.

Definition 3.1 (Ribbon category).

A ribbon category is a monoidal category 𝒞\mathscr{C} equipped with:

  1. (i)

    A braiding, i.e., a natural family of isomorphisms
    cV,W:V⊗W→∼W⊗Vc_{V,W}:V\otimes W\xrightarrow{\sim}W\otimes V satisfying the hexagon axioms, which are the categorical incarnation of the Yang–Baxter equation.

  2. (ii)

    A twist (or balancing), i.e., a natural automorphism
    θV:V→∼V\theta_{V}:V\xrightarrow{\sim}V compatible with the braiding in the sense that
    θV⊗W=cW,V​cV,W​(θV⊗θW)\theta_{V\otimes W}=c_{W,V}\,c_{V,W}\,(\theta_{V}\otimes\theta_{W}).

  3. (iii)

    Rigidity: every object VV possesses a dual V∗V^{*}, together with evaluation evV:V∗⊗V→𝟏\mathrm{ev}_{V}:V^{*}\otimes V\to\mathbf{1} and coevaluation
    coevV:𝟏→V⊗V∗\mathrm{coev}_{V}:\mathbf{1}\to V\otimes V^{*} morphisms satisfying the usual zigzag identities, and these are compatible with the braiding and twist.

The power of ribbon categories lies in their graphical calculus. Morphisms in 𝒞\mathscr{C} can be represented as ribbon tangles in the strip ℝ2×[0,1]\mathbb{R}^{2}\times[0,1]. Objects label the strands (oriented ribbons), the braiding corresponds to one strand crossing over another, the twist corresponds to a full 2​π2\pi-rotation of a ribbon, and duality is represented by cups (coevaluation) and caps (evaluation). The key property is that isotopic ribbon tangles represent the same morphism, so the algebraic relations in 𝒞\mathscr{C} are faithfully encoded by the topology of the tangles. This calculus is what ultimately connects the algebra to 3-dimensional topology.

A ribbon category comes with natural notions of dimension and trace that generalize the ordinary ones.

Definition 3.2 (Quantum dimension and trace).

For an object VV in a ribbon category, the quantum trace of an endomorphism f:V→Vf:V\to V is defined using the ribbon structure as

Trq⁡(f)=evV∘cV,V∗∘(θV​f⊗idV∗)∘coevV,\operatorname{Tr}_{q}(f)=\mathrm{ev}_{V}\circ c_{V,V^{*}}\circ(\theta_{V}f\otimes\mathrm{id}_{V^{*}})\circ\mathrm{coev}_{V},

where θV\theta_{V} is the twist. The quantum dimension of VV is the quantum trace of the identity: dimq(V)=Trq⁡(idV)\dim_{q}(V)=\operatorname{Tr}_{q}(\mathrm{id}_{V}). In the graphical calculus, this is computed by closing a strand labeled VV into a loop with a single twist. The resulting “framed unknot” evaluates to dimq(V)\dim_{q}(V). The total quantum dimension of the category is 𝒟2=∑i∈Irr​(𝒞)(dimqVi)2\mathcal{D}^{2}=\sum_{i\in\mathrm{Irr}(\mathscr{C})}(\dim_{q}V_{i})^{2}, where the sum runs over isomorphism classes of simple objects.

The quantum dimension need not be a positive integer. It is in general a complex number, and for quantum groups at roots of unity it is an algebraic number that can be negative or even zero for certain objects. This departure from ordinary linear algebra is one of the sources of the richness of quantum topology.

3.2. Modular tensor categories

We establish notation that will be used throughout the paper. For a simple Lie algebra 𝔤\mathfrak{g} and qq not a root of unity, 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) denotes the braided monoidal category of finite-dimensional representations of Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}); this is a non-semisimple, infinite category. At a root of unity q=ei​π/(k+h∨)q=e^{i\pi/(k+h^{\vee})}, the category 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) acquires a tensor ideal 𝒩\mathcal{N} of negligible morphisms. The semisimplification (or modularization) is the quotient 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G):=𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)/𝒩\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G):=\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G)/\mathcal{N}, which is a finite semisimple ribbon category; it is moreover modular (Definition 3.3). We use the bar notation (−)¯\overline{(-)} consistently to denote this semisimplification. When the distinction matters, we will always specify whether we work at generic qq, at a root of unity with the non-semisimplified category, or with the semisimplified MTC.

Not every ribbon category is suitable for constructing 3-manifold invariants. The additional condition needed is modularity, i.e., a non-degeneracy requirement on the braiding that ensures the category carries enough information to detect all topological features of 3-manifolds.

To state this precisely, recall that a fusion category is a semisimple ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear rigid monoidal category with finitely many isomorphism classes of simple objects (including the unit object, which is required to be simple) and finite-dimensional Hom spaces. The tensor product of simple objects decomposes into simples according to the fusion rules Vi⊗Vj≅⨁kNi​jk​VkV_{i}\otimes V_{j}\cong\bigoplus_{k}N_{ij}^{k}\,V_{k}, where the multiplicities Ni​jk∈ℤ≥0N_{ij}^{k}\in\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0} encode the multiplicative structure of the Grothendieck ring.

Definition 3.3 (Modular tensor category).

A modular tensor category (MTC) is a ribbon fusion category 𝒞\mathscr{C} that is non-degenerate in the following sense. The SS-matrix, defined by

(21) Si​j=1𝒟​Trq⁡(cVj,Vi∘cVi,Vj),S_{ij}=\frac{1}{\mathcal{D}}\,\operatorname{Tr}_{q}(c_{V_{j},V_{i}}\circ c_{V_{i},V_{j}}),

where {Vi}i∈I\{V_{i}\}_{i\in I} are the simple objects and 𝒟\mathcal{D} is the total quantum dimension, is invertible.

In the graphical calculus, the entry Si​jS_{ij} is computed by evaluating the Hopf link (two linked unknots, one colored by ViV_{i} and the other by VjV_{j}) and normalizing by 𝒟\mathcal{D}. The modularity condition (invertibility of SS) says that the braiding is “maximally non-symmetric”, i.e., no non-trivial object braids trivially with every other object. This condition is both natural and essential. Physically, it corresponds to the absence of “transparent” anyons in the associated topological phase of matter. Mathematically, it is what makes the surgery formula for 3-manifold invariants well-defined. The invertibility of SS is needed to show invariance under Kirby moves.

The prototypical and motivating family of examples comes from quantum groups at roots of unity.

Example 3.4 (Quantum group MTC).

Let 𝔤\mathfrak{g} be a simple Lie algebra of a compact group GG, let kk be a positive integer (the level), and set q=ei​π/(k+h∨)q=e^{i\pi/(k+h^{\vee})}, where h∨h^{\vee} is the dual Coxeter number. The quantum group Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}) at this root of unity has a category of tilting modules Tilt​(Uq​(𝔤))\mathrm{Tilt}(U_{q}(\mathfrak{g})), which is a ribbon category but is neither semisimple nor finite. To obtain a modular tensor category, one passes to the semisimplification, i.e., the quotient by the tensor ideal of negligible morphisms (those whose quantum trace vanishes in every composition),

(22) 𝒞=𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G):=Tilt​(Uq​(𝔤))/𝒩.\mathscr{C}=\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G):=\mathrm{Tilt}(U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}))\,/\,\mathcal{N}.

The resulting category is a semisimple ribbon category whose simple objects are indexed by the dominant integral weights λ\lambda lying in the Weyl alcove

(23) 𝒜k={λ∈Λdom+: 0≤⟨λ,θ⟩≤k},\mathcal{A}_{k}=\big\{\lambda\in\Lambda^{+}_{\mathrm{dom}}\;:\;0\leq\langle\lambda,\theta\rangle\leq k\big\},

where θ\theta is the highest root and Λdom+\Lambda^{+}_{\mathrm{dom}} is the set of dominant integral weights. This is a finite set, so 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) is a fusion category. It is moreover modular. Indeed, the SS-matrix is given explicitly by the Kac–Peterson formula

(24) Sλ​μ=i|Δ+|(k+h∨)rk⁡(𝔤)/2⋅∑w∈W(−1)ℓ​(w)​e−2​π​i​⟨w​(λ+ρ),μ+ρ⟩/(k+h∨)∏α∈Δ+2​sin⁡(π​⟨α,ρ⟩/(k+h∨)),S_{\lambda\mu}=\frac{i^{|\Delta_{+}|}}{(k+h^{\vee})^{\operatorname{rk}(\mathfrak{g})/2}}\cdot\frac{\displaystyle\sum_{w\in W}(-1)^{\ell(w)}\,e^{-2\pi i\langle w(\lambda+\rho),\,\mu+\rho\rangle/(k+h^{\vee})}}{\displaystyle\prod_{\alpha\in\Delta_{+}}2\sin\!\big(\pi\langle\alpha,\rho\rangle/(k+h^{\vee})\big)},

where WW is the Weyl group, ρ\rho is the Weyl vector (half-sum of positive roots), Δ+\Delta_{+} is the set of positive roots, and ℓ​(w)\ell(w) is the length function on WW. The invertibility of this matrix is a classical result in the representation theory of affine Lie algebras.

For 𝔤=𝔰​𝔩2\mathfrak{g}=\mathfrak{sl}_{2} at level kk, the Weyl alcove contains k+1k+1 weights (corresponding to spins j=0,12,1,…,k2j=0,\tfrac{1}{2},1,\ldots,\tfrac{k}{2}), and the SS-matrix reduces to Sj​j′=2/(k+2)​sin⁡((j+1)​(j′+1)​π/(k+2))S_{jj^{\prime}}=\sqrt{2/(k+2)}\;\sin\!\big((j+1)(j^{\prime}+1)\pi/(k+2)\big), which is manifestly invertible. This is the simplest non-trivial example and the one most frequently encountered in the literature. We note that the RT construction has been extended to the non-semisimple setting in [29], which is relevant to our program since the non-semisimplified category 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) at a root of unity is the natural output of BV-BFV quantization before the semisimplification step.

3.3. The RT-TQFT functor

Given a modular tensor category 𝒞\mathscr{C}, the Reshetikhin–Turaev construction produces a symmetric monoidal functor

(25) ZRT:𝐁𝐨𝐫𝐝3ext⟶2​-​𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭,Z_{\mathrm{RT}}:\mathrm{\mathbf{Bord}}_{3}^{\mathrm{ext}}\longrightarrow 2\text{-}\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}},

assigning categories to circles (namely, the MTC 𝒞\mathscr{C} itself), vector spaces to closed surfaces, linear maps to 3-cobordisms, and complex numbers to closed 3-manifolds. We describe each of these assignments in turn.

3.3.1. Assignment to surfaces

To a closed oriented surface Σg\Sigma_{g} of genus gg, the RT construction assigns a finite-dimensional vector space V​(Σg)=ZRT​(Σg)V(\Sigma_{g})=Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{g}), interpreted as the “space of states” on Σg\Sigma_{g}. Its dimension is determined by the algebraic data of the MTC through the celebrated Verlinde formula, given by

(26) dimV​(Σg)=∑i∈Irr​(𝒞)(𝒟dimqVi)2−2​g=𝒟2​g−2​∑i(S0​i)2−2​g,\dim V(\Sigma_{g})=\sum_{i\in\mathrm{Irr}(\mathscr{C})}\left(\frac{\mathcal{D}}{\dim_{q}V_{i}}\right)^{2-2g}=\mathcal{D}^{2g-2}\sum_{i}(S_{0i})^{2-2g},

where the sum runs over the (finitely many) isomorphism classes of simple objects, 𝒟\mathcal{D} is the total quantum dimension, and S0​i=dimq(Vi)/𝒟S_{0i}=\dim_{q}(V_{i})/\mathcal{D} relates the first row of the SS-matrix to the quantum dimensions. For genus 0 this gives dimV​(S2)=1\dim V(S^{2})=1; for genus 11 it gives dimV​(T2)=|Irr​(𝒞)|\dim V(T^{2})=|\mathrm{Irr}(\mathscr{C})|, the number of simple objects.

For the quantum group category 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(𝔰​𝔩2)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(\mathfrak{sl}_{2}) at level kk, the simple objects are V0,V1,…,VkV_{0},V_{1},\ldots,V_{k} (corresponding to spins 0,12,…,k20,\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{k}{2}), and the Verlinde formula specializes to

(27) dimV​(Σg)=(k+22)g−1​∑j=0k(sin⁡((j+1)​πk+2))2−2​g.\dim V(\Sigma_{g})=\left(\frac{k+2}{2}\right)^{g-1}\sum_{j=0}^{k}\left(\sin\left(\frac{(j+1)\pi}{k+2}\right)\right)^{2-2g}.

This formula, originally conjectured by Verlinde on the basis of conformal field theory, was one of the early triumphs of the RT construction. It provides a rigorous mathematical proof of a prediction from physics.

More generally, for a surface with punctures Σg,n\Sigma_{g,n} where the jj-th puncture is labeled by a simple object Vij∈𝒞V_{i_{j}}\in\mathscr{C}, the state space is the space of conformal blocks, a finite-dimensional vector space whose dimension can be computed recursively from the fusion rules Ni​jkN_{ij}^{k} by decomposing the surface into pairs of pants. The dimension depends on the genus, the number of punctures, and the labels, and is given by a generalization of the Verlinde formula involving the full SS-matrix.

3.3.2. Surgery formula for closed 3-manifolds

The RT invariant of a closed 3-manifold is computed via a surgery presentation. Recall that every closed oriented 3-manifold can be obtained from S3S^{3} by Dehn surgery on a framed link, and two surgery presentations yield the same 3-manifold if and only if they are related by a finite sequence of Kirby moves (stabilization and handle slides). The RT construction exploits this by defining an expression associated to a surgery presentation and proving that it is invariant under Kirby moves.

Concretely, suppose MM is presented as surgery on a framed link L=L1∪⋯∪LmL=L_{1}\cup\cdots\cup L_{m} in S3S^{3}, with each component LjL_{j} carrying an integer framing fjf_{j}. The RT invariant is

(28) ZRT​(M)=κ−σ​(L)⋅𝒟−(m+1)​∑V1,…,Vm∈Irr​(𝒞)(∏j=1mdimq(Vj))​JL​(V1,…,Vm).Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M)=\kappa^{-\sigma(L)}\cdot\mathcal{D}^{-(m+1)}\sum_{V_{1},\ldots,V_{m}\in\mathrm{Irr}(\mathscr{C})}\left(\prod_{j=1}^{m}\dim_{q}(V_{j})\right)J_{L}(V_{1},\ldots,V_{m}).

Here JL​(V1,…,Vm)J_{L}(V_{1},\ldots,V_{m}) is the colored link invariant (or quantum link invariant), obtained by evaluating the ribbon tangle presentation of LL in the graphical calculus of 𝒞\mathscr{C} with the jj-th component colored by the simple object VjV_{j}. The linking matrix BB has entries Bi​j=lk​(Li,Lj)B_{ij}=\mathrm{lk}(L_{i},L_{j}) for i≠ji\neq j and Bi​i=fiB_{ii}=f_{i}, and σ​(L)=σ+−σ−\sigma(L)=\sigma_{+}-\sigma_{-} is its signature. The constant κ=𝒟−1​∑i(dimqVi)2​θi\kappa=\mathcal{D}^{-1}\sum_{i}(\dim_{q}V_{i})^{2}\,\theta_{i}, where θi\theta_{i} is the twist eigenvalue of the simple object ViV_{i}, is a root of unity whose argument is related to the central charge of the associated conformal field theory. The overall normalization factor 𝒟−(m+1)\mathcal{D}^{-(m+1)} is chosen to ensure the correct behavior under stabilization.

The essential content of the RT construction is that this expression is a topological invariant:

Theorem 3.5 (Reshetikhin–Turaev [59]).

The expression (28) is independent of the choice of surgery presentation of MM. That is, it is invariant under the two Kirby moves:

  1. (i)

    Stabilization: adding or removing an unknotted component with framing ±1\pm 1, disjoint from the rest of the link. Invariance under this move is guaranteed by the normalization factors 𝒟−(m+1)\mathcal{D}^{-(m+1)} and κ−σ​(L)\kappa^{-\sigma(L)}.

  2. (ii)

    Handle slide: replacing one component by its band-sum with another. Invariance under this move relies on the modularity of 𝒞\mathscr{C} (specifically, on the invertibility of the SS-matrix) and is where the non-degeneracy condition plays its essential role.

3.4. Extended TQFT structure

The RT construction as described so far assigns vector spaces to closed surfaces and numbers to closed 3-manifolds. However, the theory has a richer structure: it can be extended one categorical level down, assigning data not only to 2- and 3-dimensional manifolds but also to 1-dimensional manifolds (circles) and their cobordisms (surfaces with boundary). This extended structure is both mathematically natural and essential for the comparison with factorization homology in Section 4.

Definition 3.6 (Extended TQFT).

A (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended TQFT is a symmetric monoidal 2-functor

(29) Z:𝐁𝐨𝐫𝐝3ext⟶2​-​𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭,Z:\mathrm{\mathbf{Bord}}_{3}^{\mathrm{ext}}\longrightarrow 2\text{-}\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}},

where 𝐁𝐨𝐫𝐝3ext\mathrm{\mathbf{Bord}}_{3}^{\mathrm{ext}} is the extended bordism 2-category whose objects are closed 1-manifolds, whose 1-morphisms are surfaces with boundary (cobordisms between 1-manifolds), and whose 2-morphisms are 3-manifolds with corners (cobordisms between cobordisms) and 2​-​𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭2\text{-}\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}} is the 2-category of ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear categories, linear functors, and natural transformations.

For the Reshetikhin–Turaev theory built from a modular tensor category 𝒞\mathscr{C}, the extended structure takes the following form:

  1. (i)

    To a circle S1S^{1}, the theory assigns the modular tensor category itself, i.e., ZRT​(S1)=𝒞Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(S^{1})=\mathscr{C}. This is the fundamental “local datum” from which everything else is built.

  2. (ii)

    To a surface Σ\Sigma with incoming boundary circles Sin1S^{1}_{\mathrm{in}} and outgoing boundary circles Sout1S^{1}_{\mathrm{out}}, the theory assigns a functor ZRT​(Σ):𝒞⊠|in|→𝒞⊠|out|Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma):\mathscr{C}^{\boxtimes|\mathrm{in}|}\to\mathscr{C}^{\boxtimes|\mathrm{out}|} between (Deligne) tensor powers of 𝒞\mathscr{C}. Gluing surfaces corresponds to composition of functors.

  3. (iii)

    The annulus S1×[0,1]S^{1}\times[0,1], viewed as a cobordism from S1S^{1} to itself, is assigned the identity functor. More interestingly, the category associated to the annulus as an object in its own right (via a different decomposition) is the Drinfeld center 𝒵​(𝒞)\mathcal{Z}(\mathscr{C}) which is the category of objects of 𝒞\mathscr{C} equipped with a natural half-braiding. This is the categorical analogue of the fact that the Hochschild cohomology of an algebra acts on its modules. The full dualizability of 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) as an object in the Morita 4-category, which is required for the Cobordism Hypothesis to apply, is established in [16] (Full dualizability pertains to the fully extended (3​-​2​-​1​-​0)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1\text{-}0)-setting; for the (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended framework used in our Main Conjecture, the relevant input is the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-structure on 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) and its factorization homology over surfaces.).

For a surface with punctures Σg,n\Sigma_{g,n}, where the jj-th puncture is labeled by an object Vij∈𝒞V_{i_{j}}\in\mathscr{C}, the state space takes the form of a Hom space in the factorization homology of the surface:

(30) V​(Σg,n;Vi1,…,Vin)=Hom𝒞⁡(𝟏,∬Σg,n𝒞​(Vi1,…,Vin)).V(\Sigma_{g,n};\,V_{i_{1}},\ldots,V_{i_{n}})=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{C}}\!\left(\mathbf{1},\;\iint_{\Sigma_{g,n}}\mathscr{C}(V_{i_{1}},\ldots,V_{i_{n}})\right).

This is the space of conformal blocks associated to the punctured surface with the given labels. Its dimension can be computed recursively by decomposing the surface into pairs of pants and applying the fusion rules. They say that each pair of pants contributes a tensor Ni​jkN_{ij}^{k}, and the dimension of the space of conformal blocks is obtained by contracting these tensors according to the combinatorics of the decomposition. The result is independent of the pants decomposition, reflecting the associativity of the tensor product in 𝒞\mathscr{C}.

4. Factorization Homology

Factorization homology provides a systematic machine for producing topological invariants of manifolds from algebraic input. It is the mathematical framework that will mediate between the field-theoretic (BV-BFV) and the combinatorial (RT) sides of our bridge. The basic idea is that given an algebraic object 𝒜\mathcal{A} with a multiplication parametrized by configurations of disks (an 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra), one can “integrate” 𝒜\mathcal{A} over any nn-manifold to obtain a topological invariant. The resulting theory satisfies a gluing axiom (⊗\otimes-excision) that parallels both the BV-BFV gluing formula and the TQFT composition law.

4.1. 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebras

The algebraic input for factorization homology is the notion of an 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra which is an algebra whose multiplication operations are parametrized by the little nn-disks operad. We recall the definitions.

Definition 4.1 (Little nn-disks operad).

The little nn-disks operad 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n} is the topological operad whose space of kk-ary operations is

(31) 𝔼n​(k)=Embrec​(∐i=1kDn,Dn),\mathbb{E}_{n}(k)=\mathrm{Emb}^{\mathrm{rec}}\!\left(\coprod_{i=1}^{k}D^{n},\;D^{n}\right),

the space of rectilinear embeddings of kk disjoint copies of the standard nn-disk into a single nn-disk. Composition in the operad is given by substitution of embeddings, i.e., plugging the output disk of one configuration into an input disk of another.

Definition 4.2 (𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra).

An 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra in a symmetric monoidal (∞,1)(\infty,1)-category 𝒱\mathcal{V} is an algebra over the operad 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n} in 𝒱\mathcal{V}. Concretely, this is an object A∈𝒱A\in\mathcal{V} together with, for each k≥0k\geq 0 and each configuration φ∈𝔼n​(k)\varphi\in\mathbb{E}_{n}(k) of kk non-overlapping disks inside a larger disk, a kk-ary multiplication map A⊗k→AA^{\otimes k}\to A, subject to coherent associativity and equivariance conditions encoded by the topology of the configuration spaces 𝔼n​(k)\mathbb{E}_{n}(k).

The 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebras form a hierarchy of increasingly commutative algebraic structures, interpolating between fully associative and fully commutative.

Example 4.3 (The hierarchy of 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebras).
  1. (i)

    𝔼0\mathbb{E}_{0}-algebras are pointed objects, i.e., a cochain complex AA equipped with a distinguished cocycle η:ℂ→A\eta:\mathbb{C}\to A. These encode formal deformation problems and correspond to formal moduli spaces in derived algebraic geometry.

  2. (ii)

    𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-algebras are associative algebras up to coherent homotopy, also known as A∞A_{\infty}-algebras. The space 𝔼1​(k)\mathbb{E}_{1}(k) is contractible for each kk (an interval is the space of configurations of kk points on a line), so the only structure is a binary product with higher associativity homotopies.

  3. (iii)

    𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebras carry a braided multiplication. The configuration space 𝔼2​(2)\mathbb{E}_{2}(2) of two disks in a larger disk is homotopy equivalent to S1S^{1}, so there is a non-trivial loop exchanging the two inputs, giving rise to a braiding. By the Dunn additivity theorem, 𝔼2≃𝔼1⊗𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{2}\simeq\mathbb{E}_{1}\otimes\mathbb{E}_{1}, which means that an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebra has two compatible associative products (one for each coordinate direction). In the categorical setting, 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebras in the (∞,2)(\infty,2)-category of linear categories are precisely braided monoidal categories.

  4. (iv)

    𝔼3\mathbb{E}_{3}-algebras in 2​-​𝐂𝐚𝐭2\text{-}\mathrm{\mathbf{Cat}} are braided monoidal 2-categories with additional symmetry related to the ribbon structure. The extra room in three dimensions allows the braiding to be “untwisted,” which is connected to the framing anomaly in 3-dimensional topology.

  5. (v)

    𝔼∞\mathbb{E}_{\infty}-algebras are fully commutative up to all higher homotopies. Since 𝔼∞​(k)≃∗\mathbb{E}_{\infty}(k)\simeq* for all kk, the multiplication is commutative and all the homotopies witnessing this commutativity are contractible. These are commutative differential graded algebras up to quasi-isomorphism.

The key point for our purposes is that a ribbon category (such as the modular tensor category 𝒞\mathscr{C} appearing in the RT construction) is naturally an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebra in the (∞,2)(\infty,2)-category of linear categories. This is the algebraic structure that factorization homology will “integrate” over surfaces.

4.2. Definition and basic properties of Factorization homology

With the notion of 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra in hand, we can define factorization homology as the operation that “integrates” an 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra over a manifold to produce a topological invariant.

Definition 4.4 (Factorization homology).

Let 𝒜\mathcal{A} be an 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra in a symmetric monoidal (∞,1)(\infty,1)-category 𝒱\mathcal{V}, and let MM be a closed nn-framed manifold. The factorization homology of MM with coefficients in 𝒜\mathcal{A} is777We will denote the factorization homology over a 1-dimensional manifold by ∫\int, over a 2-dimensional manifold by ∬\iint and over a 3-dimensional manifold by ∭\iiint. For the general nn-dimensional case we just use ∫\int.

(32) ∫M𝒜:=colim𝐃𝐢𝐬𝐤n/M𝒜(−)∈𝒱,\int_{M}\mathcal{A}\;:=\;\mathop{\mathrm{colim}}\limits_{\mathbf{Disk}_{n/M}}\;\mathcal{A}^{(-)}\;\;\in\;\mathcal{V},

where 𝐃𝐢𝐬𝐤n/M\mathrm{\mathbf{Disk}}_{n/M} is the (∞,1)(\infty,1)-category whose objects are rectilinear embeddings

φ:∐i∈IDn↪M\varphi:\coprod_{i\in I}D^{n}\hookrightarrow M

of finite disjoint unions of nn-disks into MM, and the functor 𝒜(−)\mathcal{A}^{(-)} sends such an embedding to the tensor product ⨂i∈I𝒜\bigotimes_{i\in I}\mathcal{A}. See Figure 4 for a visualization.

Remark 4.5.

The colimit in (32) can be understood informally as a “continuous tensor product”. In particular, one places a copy of 𝒜\mathcal{A} at each point of MM and multiplies nearby copies using the 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-structure, then assembles the result globally by taking the colimit over all ways of covering MM by disks. This is the higher-algebraic analogue of integrating a function over a manifold, with the 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-multiplication playing the role of pointwise multiplication and the colimit playing the role of the integral. From the perspective of quantum field theory, if 𝒜\mathcal{A} is the algebra of local observables of a topological field theory (as constructed by Costello–Gwilliam [26, 27]), then ∫M𝒜\int_{M}\mathcal{A} is the algebra of global observables on MM. So the factorization homology reconstructs the global theory from its local data. See also [64] for the construction of factorization homology as a fully extended topological field theory, and [4] for the extension to stratified spaces.

The fundamental computational tool for factorization homology is the excision property, which allows one to compute ∫M𝒜\int_{M}\mathcal{A} by cutting MM into simpler pieces and assembling the results.

Theorem 4.6 (⊗\otimes-Excision, Ayala–Francis [3]).

Let M=M1∪N×ℝM2M=M_{1}\cup_{N\times\mathbb{R}}M_{2} be a decomposition of an nn-manifold along a collar neighborhood of a codimension-1 submanifold NN (a “collar-gluing”). Then factorization homology satisfies

(33) ∫M𝒜≃∫M1𝒜⊗∫N×ℝ𝒜∫M2𝒜,\int_{M}\mathcal{A}\;\simeq\;\int_{M_{1}}\mathcal{A}\;\otimes_{\int_{N\times\mathbb{R}}\mathcal{A}}\;\int_{M_{2}}\mathcal{A},

where ⊗∫N×ℝ𝒜\otimes_{\int_{N\times\mathbb{R}}\mathcal{A}} denotes the relative tensor product (bar construction) over the 𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-algebra ∫N×ℝ𝒜\int_{N\times\mathbb{R}}\mathcal{A}. See Figure 4 for a visualization.

Remark 4.7.

The excision formula (33) is the factorization homology incarnation of the TQFT gluing axiom. The structural parallel with the BV-BFV gluing formula (18) is striking and not coincidental. In both cases, the invariant of a manifold obtained by gluing along a hypersurface is computed as a pairing (or relative tensor product) over the data associated to the gluing surface. We would like to make this parallel precise. In particular, we want to show that the BV-BFV pairing over ℋΣ\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma} and the factorization homology relative tensor product over ∬Σ𝒜\iint_{\Sigma}\mathcal{A} are computing the same thing.

4.3. Low-dimensional computations

To build intuition and establish the key identifications that our bridge relies on, we compute factorization homology in the simplest cases.

Proposition 4.8.

For an 𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-algebra AA (i.e., an associative algebra up to homotopy), the factorization homology of the circle is the Hochschild homology of AA. So, we get

(34) ∫S1A≃HH∙⁡(A):=A⊗A⊗AopA.\int_{S^{1}}A\;\simeq\;\operatorname{HH}_{\bullet}(A)\;:=\;A\otimes_{A\otimes A^{\mathrm{op}}}A.
Proof sketch.

Decompose the circle as a union of two intervals glued at their endpoints, i.e., S1=I∪{0,1}IS^{1}=I\cup_{\{0,1\}}I. Factorization homology of an interval is simply ∫IA≃A\int_{I}A\simeq A (since the interval is contractible, the colimit over disk embeddings degenerates). The two boundary points contribute ∫{0,1}A≃A⊗A\int_{\{0,1\}}A\simeq A\otimes A, which acts on each copy of AA as a bimodule via left and right multiplication. Applying ⊗\otimes-excision (Theorem 4.6) to this decomposition gives ∫S1A≃A⊗A⊗AopA\int_{S^{1}}A\simeq A\otimes_{A\otimes A^{\mathrm{op}}}A, which is the bar construction computing the Hochschild homology of AA. ∎

This computation has a natural categorical upgrade. When we pass from 𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-algebras (associative algebras) to 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebras (braided monoidal categories), the Hochschild homology is replaced by its categorical analogue which is the Drinfeld center.

Proposition 4.9.

Let 𝒞\mathscr{C} be an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebra in the (∞,2)(\infty,2)-category of ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear categories (i.e., a braided monoidal category). Then we get that

  1. (i)

    ∫S1𝒞≃𝒵​(𝒞)\int_{S^{1}}\mathscr{C}\simeq\mathcal{Z}(\mathscr{C}), the Drinfeld center of 𝒞\mathscr{C}. This is the categorical Hochschild homology.

  2. (ii)

    ∬ℝ2𝒞≃𝒞\iint_{\mathbb{R}^{2}}\mathscr{C}\simeq\mathscr{C}. Factorization homology of Euclidean space recovers the algebra itself, reflecting the fact that ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} is the “unit” for the gluing operation.

  3. (iii)

    ∬S1×ℝ𝒞≃𝒵​(𝒞)\iint_{S^{1}\times\mathbb{R}}\mathscr{C}\simeq\mathcal{Z}(\mathscr{C}) as a monoidal category, where the monoidal structure comes from the non-compact ℝ\mathbb{R} direction. This is the category that factorization homology assigns to an annulus, consistent with the extended TQFT picture of Section 3.

Remark 4.10 (The Drinfeld center).

The Drinfeld center 𝒵​(𝒞)\mathcal{Z}(\mathscr{C}) is the category whose objects are pairs (V,σ)(V,\sigma), where VV is an object of 𝒞\mathscr{C} and

σ={σW:V⊗W→∼W⊗V}W∈𝒞\sigma=\{\sigma_{W}:V\otimes W\xrightarrow{\sim}W\otimes V\}_{W\in\mathscr{C}}

is a half-braiding, i.e., a natural family of isomorphisms compatible with the tensor product, but not required to come from the braiding of 𝒞\mathscr{C} itself. The Drinfeld center is always a braided monoidal category, even when 𝒞\mathscr{C} is only monoidal (not braided); when 𝒞\mathscr{C} is already braided, 𝒵​(𝒞)\mathcal{Z}(\mathscr{C}) is typically larger. For the full (non-semisimplified) quantum group category, there is a canonical equivalence 𝒵​(𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G))≃𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)⊠𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)¯\mathcal{Z}(\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G))\simeq\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G)\boxtimes\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G)}, where 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)¯\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G)} denotes the same category with the reversed braiding and ⊠\boxtimes is the Deligne tensor product. This is a categorical analogue of the algebraic identity HH∙⁡(A)≃A⊗AeA\operatorname{HH}_{\bullet}(A)\simeq A\otimes_{A^{e}}A, where Ae=A⊗AopA^{e}=A\otimes A^{\mathrm{op}} is the enveloping algebra.

4.4. The Ben-Zvi–Brochier–Jordan theorem

The results of the previous subsection (factorization homology of S1S^{1} giving the Drinfeld center, and the excision formula allowing computations on arbitrary surfaces) set the stage for the theorems that form the first mathematical pillar supporting our conjectural bridge. We emphasize that the BBJ and AKZ theorems stated below are established results in the literature; the conjectural content of our program begins with the identification of these structures with the BV-BFV output in Section 6. It connects the factorization homology of a braided tensor category to quantum character varieties, which at roots of unity yield the Reshetikhin–Turaev state spaces via the Ai–Kong–Zheng theorem.

Theorem 4.11 (Ben-Zvi–Brochier–Jordan [12, 13]).

Let 𝒜\mathcal{A} be a braided tensor category (e.g., 𝒜=𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathcal{A}=\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) for a reductive group GG at generic qq), viewed as an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebra in the symmetric monoidal (∞,2)(\infty,2)-category of presentable ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear categories. Then:

  1. (i)

    For any oriented surface Σ\Sigma (possibly with boundary and punctures), the factorization homology ∬Σ𝒜\iint_{\Sigma}\mathcal{A} is a well-defined ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear category, the quantum character variety of Σ\Sigma.

  2. (ii)

    For a punctured surface Σ∘\Sigma^{\circ}, the quantum character variety ∬Σ∘𝒜\iint_{\Sigma^{\circ}}\mathcal{A} is equivalent to the category of modules over an explicitly presented algebra, quantizing the algebra of functions on the classical character variety.

  3. (iii)

    For the torus, ∬T2𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)≃𝒟q​(G/G)​-​𝐦𝐨𝐝\iint_{T^{2}}\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G)\simeq\mathcal{D}_{q}(G/G)\text{-}\mathrm{\mathbf{mod}}, the category of equivariant quantum 𝒟\mathcal{D}-modules on GG.

  4. (iv)

    For a closed surface Σg\Sigma_{g}, the quantum character variety is computed as a quantum Hamiltonian reduction of the punctured surface category.

Theorem 4.12 (Ai–Kong–Zheng [2]).

Let 𝒞\mathscr{C} be a unitary modular tensor category, viewed as an anomaly-free888See [2] for a detailed definition of anomaly-free. coefficient system on a closed oriented surface Σg\Sigma_{g}. Then

(35) ∬Σg𝒞≃(𝐇,uΣg),\iint_{\Sigma_{g}}\mathscr{C}\;\simeq\;(\mathbf{H},\,u_{\Sigma_{g}}),
