where 𝐇\mathbf{H} is the category of finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces and uΣg∈𝐇u_{\Sigma_{g}}\in\mathbf{H} is a distinguished object that coincides with the RT state space VRT​(Σg)V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{g}).

Remark 4.13.

For the quantum group category 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) with GG a compact simple Lie group and q=ei​π/(k+h∨)q=e^{i\pi/(k+h^{\vee})}, unitarity follows from the ∗*-structure on Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}) induced by the compact real form, and modularity from the invertibility of the Kac–Peterson SS-matrix and anomaly-freeness follows from modularity via Müger’s theorem 𝒞⊠𝒞¯≃𝒵​(𝒞)\mathscr{C}\boxtimes\overline{\mathscr{C}}\simeq\mathcal{Z}(\mathscr{C}), so the hypotheses of Theorem 4.12 are satisfied.

Remark 4.14 (Two levels of the theory).

It is important to distinguish two levels:

  1. (a)

    At generic qq, the factorization homology ∬Σ𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\iint_{\Sigma}\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) is a genuinely non-trivial category (the quantum character variety of BBJ). This is the natural setting for comparison with BV-BFV, which produces formal deformations over ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!].

  2. (b)

    At a root of unity, after semisimplification to the MTC 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G), the factorization homology of a closed surface yields (𝐇,uΣg)(\mathbf{H},\,u_{\Sigma_{g}}), where uΣgu_{\Sigma_{g}} is the RT state space (Theorem 4.12).

The passage from (a) to (b) is part of the conjectural Step 2c of our program. The dualizability properties required for these constructions are established in [16], the classification of invertible braided tensor categories relevant to the framing anomaly is given in [17], and the extension of factorization homology computations to the 4-dimensional Crane–Yetter theory appears in [50].

4.5. Factorization homology and the Cobordism Hypothesis

The factorization homology framework connects to the deepest structural result in the theory of topological quantum field theories which is the Cobordism Hypothesis, that classifies fully extended TQFTs in terms of a single piece of algebraic data.

Theorem 4.15 (Cobordism Hypothesis; Lurie [53]).

A fully extended framed nn-dimensional TQFT with values in a symmetric monoidal (∞,n)(\infty,n)-category 𝒱\mathcal{V} is completely determined by the object it assigns to a point, and this object must be fully dualizable in 𝒱\mathcal{V}. More precisely, there is an equivalence

(36) 𝐅𝐮𝐧⊗​(𝐁𝐨𝐫𝐝nfr,𝒱)≃(𝒱fd)∼,\mathrm{\mathbf{Fun}}^{\otimes}(\mathrm{\mathbf{Bord}}_{n}^{\mathrm{fr}},\,\mathcal{V})\;\simeq\;(\mathcal{V}^{\mathrm{fd}})^{\sim},

where 𝒱fd\mathcal{V}^{\mathrm{fd}} denotes the full subcategory of fully dualizable objects and (−)∼(-)^{\sim} takes the underlying ∞\infty-groupoid (discarding non-invertible morphisms).

Remark 4.16.

Factorization homology provides the explicit construction behind the Cobordism Hypothesis. Given a fully dualizable 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra 𝒜∈𝒱\mathcal{A}\in\mathcal{V}, the assignment M↦∬M𝒜M\mapsto\iint_{M}\mathcal{A} is precisely the fully extended TQFT corresponding to 𝒜\mathcal{A} under the equivalence of the Cobordism Hypothesis. This is the content of a theorem of Ayala–Francis [3], who show that a symmetric monoidal functor from 𝐁𝐨𝐫𝐝nfr\mathrm{\mathbf{Bord}}_{n}^{\mathrm{fr}} to 𝒱\mathcal{V} satisfying ⊗\otimes-excision is uniquely determined by its value on a disk (which is the 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra 𝒜\mathcal{A}), and conversely that factorization homology of any 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra satisfies ⊗\otimes-excision. In other words, factorization homology is not merely a tool for computing TQFTs, but it is the TQFT, in the precise sense that it provides the unique excisive extension of local data to a global invariant of manifolds. This perspective will be important in Section 6, where we argue that the BV-BFV partition function, which also satisfies a gluing axiom, must agree with factorization homology once the local data (the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebra) is matched.

5. The Derived Character Stack and Shifted Symplectic Geometry

The second pillar of our bridge is the derived algebraic geometry of the character stack, in particular, the moduli space of local systems (flat connections) on a manifold. This is the geometric object that both the BV-BFV formalism and the RT construction “see,” albeit from different angles. The BV-BFV formalism quantizes it perturbatively via gauge-fixing and Feynman diagrams, while the RT construction accesses it non-perturbatively through the representation theory of quantum groups. The shifted symplectic structures discovered by Pantev–Toën–Vaquié–Vezzosi (PTVV) [57] provide the geometric framework in which these two perspectives can be compared.

5.1. Essentials of derived algebraic geometry

We briefly recall the notions from derived algebraic geometry (DAG) that we need. The reader may consult [65, 37, 54] for comprehensive treatments.

The basic idea of DAG is to replace ordinary commutative rings by commutative differential graded algebras (or, more generally, simplicial commutative rings), allowing the “functions” on a space to form a cochain complex rather than a plain ring. This extra structure records higher-order information about the geometry. In particular, the obstructions and deformations that are invisible to the classical (underived) moduli space.

Definition 5.1 (Derived stack).

A derived stack (over ℂ\mathbb{C}) is a functor

X:𝐜𝐝𝐠𝐚ℂ≤0→𝒮X:\mathrm{\mathbf{cdga}}_{\mathbb{C}}^{\leq 0}\to\mathcal{S}

from the ∞\infty-category of connective commutative differential graded ℂ\mathbb{C}-algebras to the ∞\infty-category of spaces (simplicial sets), satisfying étale descent. Every derived stack XX has an underlying classical truncation Xcl=X|𝐜𝐀𝐥𝐠ℂX_{\mathrm{cl}}=X|_{\mathrm{\mathbf{cAlg}}_{\mathbb{C}}}, obtained by restricting the functor to ordinary commutative algebras (dg-algebras concentrated in degree 0). The derived stack remembers strictly more information than its classical truncation. Actually, the values on dg-algebras with non-trivial cohomology encode the deformation theory and obstruction theory of XX.

Definition 5.2 (Cotangent complex).

For a derived stack XX, the cotangent complex 𝕃X∈𝐐𝐂𝐨𝐡​(X)\mathbb{L}_{X}\in\mathrm{\mathbf{QCoh}}(X) is the quasi-coherent sheaf that represents the functor of derivations; it is the derived-geometric replacement of the sheaf of Kähler differentials. Its fiber at a point x∈Xx\in X is the cochain complex governing the deformation theory of xx. The tangent space H0​(𝕋X,x)H^{0}(\mathbb{T}_{X,x}) parametrizes first-order deformations, H1​(𝕋X,x)H^{1}(\mathbb{T}_{X,x}) contains obstructions, and H−1​(𝕋X,x)H^{-1}(\mathbb{T}_{X,x}) detects infinitesimal automorphisms (where 𝕋X=𝕃X∨\mathbb{T}_{X}=\mathbb{L}_{X}^{\vee} is the tangent complex). For a derived mapping stack X=Map⁡(Y,Z)X=\operatorname{Map}(Y,Z) with YY a compact oriented manifold, there is an explicit formula 𝕃X≃ev∗​𝕃Z⊗pY∗​ωY\mathbb{L}_{X}\simeq\mathrm{ev}^{*}\,\mathbb{L}_{Z}\otimes p_{Y}^{*}\,\omega_{Y}, where ωY\omega_{Y} is the dualizing sheaf of YY and the tensor product implements integration over the fibers of the projection pYp_{Y}.

5.2. The derived character stack

The geometric object at the heart of our program is the derived moduli space of local systems (the “character stack”) which parametrizes flat GG-connections on a manifold up to gauge equivalence, together with all of the higher homotopical data that the classical moduli space forgets.

Definition 5.3 (Derived character stack).

Let GG be a reductive algebraic group over ℂ\mathbb{C} and let Σ\Sigma be a compact oriented manifold. The derived character stack (or derived moduli stack of local systems) is the derived mapping stack

(37) LocG​(Σ):=Map⁡(ΣB,B​G),\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)\;:=\;\operatorname{Map}(\Sigma_{B},\,BG),

where ΣB\Sigma_{B} is the Betti stack (the constant derived stack associated to the homotopy type of Σ\Sigma) and B​G=[pt/G]BG=[\mathrm{pt}/G] is the classifying stack of GG.

This definition is compact but rich in content. The mapping stack construction automatically produces a derived stack that encodes, in a single object, the space of representations of π1​(Σ)\pi_{1}(\Sigma) into GG, the gauge equivalences between them, the deformation theory around each representation, and the obstructions to extending infinitesimal deformations. We unpack this in the following proposition.

Proposition 5.4.

The derived character stack LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) has the following concrete descriptions:

  1. (i)

    Points. The set of isomorphism classes of points of LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) is the character variety π0​(LocG​(Σ))=Hom⁡(π1​(Σ),G)/G\pi_{0}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma))=\operatorname{Hom}(\pi_{1}(\Sigma),G)/G, parametrizing conjugacy classes of representations of the fundamental group into GG.

  2. (ii)

    Tangent complex. At a point [ρ]∈LocG​(Σ)[\rho]\in\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) corresponding to a representation ρ:π1​(Σ)→G\rho:\pi_{1}(\Sigma)\to G, the tangent complex is the shifted group cohomology complex 𝕋[ρ]​LocG​(Σ)≃C∙​(Σ;𝔤Adρ)​[1]\mathbb{T}_{[\rho]}\,\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)\simeq C^{\bullet}(\Sigma;\,\mathfrak{g}_{\operatorname{Ad}_{\rho}})[1], where 𝔤Adρ\mathfrak{g}_{\operatorname{Ad}_{\rho}} is the adjoint local system determined by ρ\rho. In particular, H0H^{0} of the tangent complex (first-order deformations) is H1​(Σ;𝔤Adρ)H^{1}(\Sigma;\,\mathfrak{g}_{\operatorname{Ad}_{\rho}}), while H−1H^{-1} (infinitesimal automorphisms) is H0​(Σ;𝔤Adρ)H^{0}(\Sigma;\,\mathfrak{g}_{\operatorname{Ad}_{\rho}}), the centralizer of the representation.

  3. (iii)

    The circle. For Σ=S1\Sigma=S^{1}, the fundamental group is ℤ\mathbb{Z}, and a representation ℤ→G\mathbb{Z}\to G is determined by the image of the generator (an element of GG). Conjugation acts by the adjoint action, so LocG​(S1)=[G/G]\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(S^{1})=[G/G] is the adjoint quotient stack.

  4. (iv)

    Closed surfaces. For a closed surface Σg\Sigma_{g} of genus gg, the fundamental group has the standard presentation with 2​g2g generators a1,b1,…,ag,bga_{1},b_{1},\ldots,a_{g},b_{g} and one relation ∏i=1g[ai,bi]=1\prod_{i=1}^{g}[a_{i},b_{i}]=1. The classical truncation of the character stack is therefore the GIT quotient Hom(π1(Σg),G)//G\operatorname{Hom}(\pi_{1}(\Sigma_{g}),G)/\!/G, parametrizing conjugacy classes of tuples (A1,B1,…,Ag,Bg)∈G2​g(A_{1},B_{1},\ldots,A_{g},B_{g})\in G^{2g} satisfying the relation ∏i=1g[Ai,Bi]=1\prod_{i=1}^{g}[A_{i},B_{i}]=1. The derived structure enhances this by remembering the full deformation–obstruction complex at each point.

5.3. Shifted symplectic structures

The central result connecting derived character stacks to both symplectic geometry and the BV formalism is the theory of shifted symplectic structures, developed by Pantev, Toën, Vaquié, and Vezzosi in [57]. The idea is that on a derived stack, symplectic forms can exist in degrees other than zero, and the degree is dictated by the dimension of the source manifold.

Definition 5.5 (nn-shifted symplectic structure; PTVV [57]).

An nn-shifted symplectic structure on a derived stack XX is a closed 2-form ω∈𝒜2,cl​(X,n)\omega\in\mathcal{A}^{2,\mathrm{cl}}(X,n) of cohomological degree nn in the de Rham complex of XX, such that the induced map ω♯:𝕋X→𝕃X​[n]\omega^{\sharp}:\mathbb{T}_{X}\to\mathbb{L}_{X}[n] is an equivalence of quasi-coherent sheaves. The non-degeneracy condition generalizes the classical requirement that a symplectic form induce an isomorphism between tangent and cotangent bundles.

The following theorem reveals a striking pattern. Namely, that the character stack of a dd-dimensional manifold carries a natural symplectic structure whose degree is determined by dd, and the three cases d=1,2,3d=1,2,3 correspond precisely to the three types of structure (BFV, classical symplectic, BV) that appear in the BV-BFV formalism.

Theorem 5.6 (PTVV [57]).

Let GG be reductive and let Σ\Sigma be a compact oriented dd-dimensional manifold. Then the derived character stack LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) carries a canonical (2−d)(2-d)-shifted symplectic structure. The three cases relevant to Chern–Simons theory are:

  1. (i)

    d=1d=1 (circles): LocG​(S1)=[G/G]\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(S^{1})=[G/G] carries a 11-shifted symplectic structure. At a point [g]∈G/G[g]\in G/G, the tangent complex is the two-term complex 𝔤→Adg−1𝔤\mathfrak{g}\xrightarrow{\operatorname{Ad}_{g}-1}\mathfrak{g} in degrees 0 and 11, and the symplectic form is given by the Killing form on 𝔤\mathfrak{g} composed with the Poincaré duality pairing of S1S^{1}. The 11-shifted degree reflects the fact that the symplectic pairing takes values in degree 11, corresponding to the “odd” nature of the BFV phase space.

  2. (ii)

    d=2d=2 (surfaces): LocG​(Σg)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma_{g}) carries a 0-shifted symplectic structure. This is a symplectic structure in the ordinary sense (on the smooth locus of the classical truncation), and it recovers the classical Atiyah–Bott–Goldman symplectic form given by

    (38) ωA​B​([α],[β])=∫ΣTr⁡(α∧β),\omega_{AB}([\alpha],\,[\beta])=\int_{\Sigma}\operatorname{Tr}\,(\alpha\wedge\beta),

    for tangent vectors [α],[β]∈H1​(Σ;𝔤Adρ)≅T[ρ]​LocG​(Σ)cl[\alpha],[\beta]\in H^{1}(\Sigma;\,\mathfrak{g}_{\operatorname{Ad}_{\rho}})\cong T_{[\rho]}\,\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)_{\mathrm{cl}}. This is the symplectic structure on the phase space that Chern–Simons theory assigns to a spatial slice.

  3. (iii)

    d=3d=3 (3-manifolds): LocG​(M)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M) carries a (−1)(-1)-shifted symplectic structure. A (−1)(-1)-shifted symplectic structure is precisely a BV symplectic structure in the sense of Definition 2.1: it pairs degree kk fields with degree (−1−k)(-1-k) antifields. This is the derived-geometric origin of the BV structure on the space of solutions to the Chern–Simons equations of motion.

Remark 5.7 (The PTVV mechanism).

The shifted symplectic structure on
LocG​(Σ)=Map⁡(Σ,B​G)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)=\operatorname{Map}(\Sigma,BG) is constructed by a derived-geometric version of the AKSZ transgression, using three ingredients:

  1. (a)

    The invariant pairing on 𝔤\mathfrak{g} (the Killing form), which endows the classifying stack B​G=[pt/G]BG=[\mathrm{pt}/G] with a 22-shifted symplectic structure. This is a “symplectic form of degree 22” in the sense that it pairs the tangent complex 𝕋B​G=𝔤​[1]\mathbb{T}_{BG}=\mathfrak{g}[1] with itself using the Killing form, producing a closed 2-form in 𝒜2,cl​(B​G,2)\mathcal{A}^{2,\mathrm{cl}}(BG,2).

  2. (b)

    The Poincaré duality of the source manifold Σ\Sigma. For a compact oriented dd-manifold, Poincaré duality provides a non-degenerate pairing Hk​(Σ)⊗Hd−k​(Σ)→ℂH^{k}(\Sigma)\otimes H^{d-k}(\Sigma)\to\mathbb{C}, which shifts the degree of the symplectic structure by −d-d.

  3. (c)

    The AKSZ transgression: the derived mapping stack Map⁡(Σ,B​G)\operatorname{Map}(\Sigma,BG) inherits a (2−d)(2-d)-shifted symplectic structure by “integrating” the 22-shifted structure on B​GBG over the fundamental class of Σ\Sigma. This integration is the derived-geometric analogue of the classical AKSZ construction, which produces BV symplectic structures on mapping spaces between a source manifold with a volume form and a target with a symplectic structure.

The beauty of this construction is that a single mechanism (transgression of the Killing form via Poincaré duality) simultaneously produces the BFV structure in dimension 1, the Atiyah–Bott form in dimension 2, and the BV structure in dimension 3. See also [19] for the shifted cotangent perspective on these structures.

5.4. Lagrangian structures and the BV-BFV correspondence

The PTVV theorem tells us that the character stacks of the bulk and the boundary each carry shifted symplectic structures, of degrees −1-1 and 0 respectively. The final ingredient from derived geometry is the observation that the relationship between bulk and boundary, i.e., the restriction of flat connections from MM to ∂M\partial M, is captured by a Lagrangian structure on the restriction map. This provides a precise derived-geometric reformulation of the BV-BFV axiom.

Definition 5.8 (Lagrangian morphism; PTVV [57]).

Let (X,ω)(X,\omega) be an nn-shifted symplectic derived stack. A morphism f:L→Xf:L\to X is Lagrangian if it is equipped with an isotropic structure, i.e., a homotopy f∗​ω∼0f^{*}\omega\sim 0 witnessing the vanishing of the pullback of the symplectic form, such that the induced map on tangent/cotangent complexes 𝕋L→𝕃L​[n−1]\mathbb{T}_{L}\to\mathbb{L}_{L}[n-1] is an equivalence. The isotropic condition says that LL is “half-dimensional” relative to XX; the equivalence condition says it is maximally so (not merely coisotropic or isotropic in a weaker sense).

The key theorem connecting this to the BV-BFV formalism is the following.

Theorem 5.9 (Calaque [18]).

Let MM be a compact oriented 3-manifold with boundary ∂M=Σ\partial M=\Sigma. The restriction map

(39) res:LocG​(M)⟶LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{res}:\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)\longrightarrow\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)

carries a canonical Lagrangian structure, where LocG​(M)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M) has its (−1)(-1)-shifted symplectic structure and LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) has its 0-shifted symplectic structure.

This theorem provides a complete derived-geometric reinterpretation of the BV-BFV formalism for Chern–Simons theory. Every element of the BV-BFV data described in Section 2 has a counterpart in the language of derived symplectic geometry. In particular, we have the following table.

BV-BFV Derived geometry
BV symplectic (bulk) (−1)(-1)-shifted symplectic on LocG​(M)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)
BFV symplectic (boundary) 0-shifted symplectic on LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)
BV-BFV axiom Lagrangian structure of restriction
BV action / CME Derived critical locus
Gauge-fixing Choice of Lagrangian in LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)

This dictionary is the foundation of our bridge. On the left, we have the differential-geometric and perturbative framework of the BV-BFV formalism; on the right, the algebro-geometric and potentially non-perturbative framework of derived symplectic geometry. The two descriptions agree at the classical level (they encode the same moduli problem: flat connections on MM restricting to flat connections on Σ\Sigma), and the central question is whether they also agree at the quantum level. The remainder of this paper is devoted to arguing that they do, with factorization homology providing the mechanism for the comparison. The quantization of shifted coisotropic and Lagrangian structures is developed in [56]; see also [63] for a shifted geometric quantization perspective.

5.5. Deformation quantization of shifted symplectic stacks

Having identified the classical geometric structure underlying both the BV-BFV and RT frameworks (the derived character stack with its shifted symplectic form) we now turn to quantization. The key result is that the shifted symplectic structures on character stacks can be deformation-quantized in a canonical way, and the output of this quantization is precisely the quantum group and its representation category.

Theorem 5.10 (CPTVV [20], Safronov [61], Etingof–Kazhdan [31], BBJ [12]).

Combining the shifted deformation quantization framework of [20], the identification of Poisson-Lie structures with shifted Poisson structures [61], and the Etingof–Kazhdan quantization theorem [31], one obtains the following hierarchy of quantum objects from the character stack:

  1. (i)

    The classifying stack B​GBG, with its 22-shifted Poisson structure, quantizes to the quantum group Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}), viewed as an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebra (equivalently, a braided Hopf algebra).

  2. (ii)

    The adjoint quotient [G/G][G/G], with its 11-shifted Poisson structure, quantizes to the representation category 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G), viewed as an 𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-category (equivalently, a monoidal category).

  3. (iii)

    For a closed surface Σg\Sigma_{g}, the character stack LocG​(Σg)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma_{g}), with its 0-shifted Poisson structure, quantizes to the factorization homology ∬Σg𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\iint_{\Sigma_{g}}\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) (by BBJ [12]).

These are formal deformations over ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!] with q=eℏ/2q=e^{\hbar/2}.

The remarkable feature of this theorem is the compatibility across dimensions. In fact, quantizing the character stack of a surface can be computed either directly (as a deformation quantization of a 0-shifted symplectic stack) or by first quantizing the local data (the 22-shifted structure on B​GBG, producing the quantum group) and then integrating over the surface via factorization homology. The two procedures give the same answer.

Theorem 5.11 (Ben-Zvi–Brochier–Jordan [12]).

The deformation quantization of the character stack LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) is computed by factorization homology of the quantized local data, so we have

(40) Quant​(LocG​(Σ))≃∬Σ𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G).\mathrm{Quant}\big(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)\big)\;\simeq\;\iint_{\Sigma}\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G).
Remark 5.12.

This is the second pillar of our bridge. The RT state spaces arise from quantizing the same geometric object (namely, the character stack) that underlies the BV-BFV formalism. The classical phase spaces are identical; the question is whether the quantization procedures also agree. At a root of unity, after semisimplification, the factorization homology of a closed surface Σg\Sigma_{g} yields (𝐇,uΣg)(\mathbf{H},\,u_{\Sigma_{g}}) with uΣg≅VRT​(Σg)u_{\Sigma_{g}}\cong V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{g}) (Theorem 4.12).

Remark 5.13 (Quantum character varieties).

The term quantum character variety, introduced by Ben-Zvi–Brochier–Jordan [12], refers to the factorization homology ∬Σ𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\iint_{\Sigma}\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G). The content of Theorem 5.11 is that this coincides with the deformation quantization Quant​(LocG​(Σ))\mathrm{Quant}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)) of the derived character stack, so the two descriptions are equivalent. In the diagram of Conjecture 6.8, the arrow labeled “BBJ” is this identification; the substantive open problem is the arrow labeled “Step 2,” which asserts that the BV-BFV quantization agrees with the algebro-geometric deformation quantization Quant​(LocG​(Σ))\mathrm{Quant}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)).

Remark 5.14 (The quantization parameter).

The deformation quantization of Theorem 5.10 produces formal deformations over the power series ring ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!], with q=eℏ/2q=e^{\hbar/2}. The modular tensor category, however, lives at a specific non-formal value that is given by ℏ=2​π​i/(k+h∨)\hbar=2\pi i/(k+h^{\vee}), i.e., q=ei​π/(k+h∨)q=e^{i\pi/(k+h^{\vee})}. Passing from the formal to the actual value requires an algebraization step. One must show that the formal power series deformation extends to an algebraic family over ℂ​[ℏ]\mathbb{C}[\hbar] (or a suitable localization) that can be specialized. For quantum groups, this extension is known to exist thanks to the explicit Drinfeld–Jimbo construction, but establishing it purely from the deformation-quantization perspective remains a non-trivial step, and it is one of the key technical challenges in our program.

Remark 5.15 (Comparison with geometric quantization).

There is an alternative, more classical approach to quantizing the character variety. It can be done by geometric quantization. One chooses a complex structure on Σ\Sigma, which identifies LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) with a moduli space of holomorphic bundles, and then defines the quantum Hilbert space as the space of holomorphic sections of a certain line bundle (the determinant bundle at level kk). Different choices of complex structure give a priori different Hilbert spaces, but the celebrated Hitchin connection provides a projectively flat connection on the resulting bundle of Hilbert spaces over Teichmüller space, ensuring that the quantization is independent of the complex structure up to projective equivalence. This produces the same projective representations of MCG⁡(Σg)\operatorname{MCG}(\Sigma_{g}) as the RT theory. The deformation quantization approach of Theorem 5.10 has the advantage of being manifestly algebraic, i.e., it does not require a choice of complex structure, and of being naturally compatible with factorization homology. On the other hand, geometric quantization gives more direct access to the Hilbert space structure (inner products, unitarity). The geometric quantization approach was initiated by Hitchin [48]; for further developments connecting it to the RT representations, see [7]. Establishing a rigorous equivalence between the two quantization procedures, beyond the level of projective MCG representations, remains an important open problem and a consistency check for our program.

6. Main Conjectures and Proof Strategy

We now assemble the ingredients of the previous sections into precise conjectural statements and develop the strategy for establishing them. The logic is as follows: from the BV-BFV quantization of Chern–Simons theory, we extract an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category; we conjecture that this category is equivalent to the modular tensor category used in the RT construction; and we argue that this equivalence, together with the factorization homology machinery, implies the identification of the two TQFT functors.

6.1. The 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category from BV-BFV

Throughout this section and the Main Conjecture, we work in the (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended TQFT framework of Definition 3.6, in which the circle S1S^{1} is the lowest-dimensional stratum and is assigned an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category. The fully extended (3​-​2​-​1​-​0)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1\text{-}0)-framework (assigning data to points) is discussed separately in Section 10.

The first step is to extract an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-monoidal category from the BV-BFV quantization of Chern–Simons theory. The key geometric input is the topology of the disk. The little 2-disks operad acts on configurations of sub-disks inside a larger disk, and this action translates into algebraic structure on the BFV state space.

Construction 6.1.

Consider Chern–Simons theory on the cylinder D2×[0,1]D^{2}\times[0,1], where D2D^{2} is a disk. We take the following points into account.

  1. (i)

    To the spatial boundary circle S1=∂D2S^{1}=\partial D^{2}, the extended TQFT assigns a category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} of boundary conditions (not a vector space; the BFV state space is assigned to codimension-1 surfaces, while S1S^{1} is codimension-2).

  2. (ii)

    Vertical composition. Stacking two cylinders D2×[0,1]D^{2}\times[0,1] end to end (gluing along a common D2D^{2} face) gives a longer cylinder D2×[0,2]D^{2}\times[0,2]. At the level of partition functions, this gluing is computed by the BV-BFV pairing over the BFV state space ℋD2\mathcal{H}_{D^{2}} of the intermediate disk. For fixed boundary conditions on the lateral circle S1=∂D2S^{1}=\partial D^{2}, this defines composition of morphisms in the category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}, giving it an 𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-structure.

  3. (iii)

    Horizontal juxtaposition. Embedding two disjoint smaller disks D12⊔D22D^{2}_{1}\sqcup D^{2}_{2} into a larger disk D2D^{2} and considering the partition function on (D12⊔D22)×[0,1](D^{2}_{1}\sqcup D^{2}_{2})\times[0,1] versus D2×[0,1]D^{2}\times[0,1] gives a second multiplication, parametrized by the space of such embeddings.

The two operations (ii) and (iii) are compatible in the precise sense required by the little 2-disks operad. By the recognition principle for 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebras, they endow the category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} of boundary conditions (objects: boundary conditions on the lateral circle S1=∂D2S^{1}=\partial D^{2}; morphisms: BV-BFV partition functions on the annulus) with an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-monoidal structure, i.e., a braided monoidal structure.

The central question is: which braided monoidal category does this construction produce?

Construction 6.2 (The category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}).

We define the category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} as the category of boundary conditions for Chern–Simons theory on the disk D2D^{2}. Consider CS theory on the 3-manifold D2×[0,1]D^{2}\times[0,1], where D2D^{2} is the spatial slice and [0,1][0,1] is the time direction. The full boundary decomposes as

∂(D2×[0,1])=D2×{0,1}⏟temporal: incoming/outgoing∪S1×[0,1]⏟spatial: lateral boundary.\partial(D^{2}\times[0,1])=\underbrace{D^{2}\times\{0,1\}}_{\text{temporal: incoming/outgoing}}\;\cup\;\underbrace{S^{1}\times[0,1]}_{\text{spatial: lateral boundary}}.

The BV-BFV formalism assigns state spaces to the temporal boundary components D2×{0,1}D^{2}\times\{0,1\}, but the partition function depends on the choice of boundary condition imposed on the lateral boundary S1×[0,1]S^{1}\times[0,1]. The objects of ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} are these boundary conditions. In the extended TQFT language, they correspond to the data assigned to the codimension-2 stratum S1=∂D2S^{1}=\partial D^{2}; physically, they are the labels for Wilson lines ending on the boundary circle. The morphisms Hom⁡(V,W)\operatorname{Hom}(V,W) are defined by the BV-BFV partition functions on the annulus S1×[0,1]S^{1}\times[0,1] interpolating between boundary conditions VV and WW, and composition is given by the gluing formula (Theorem 2.11). The monoidal structure is defined by the pair-of-pants cobordism. Given boundary conditions V,WV,W on two smaller disks D12,D22⊂D2D^{2}_{1},D^{2}_{2}\subset D^{2}, their tensor product V⊗WV\otimes W is the boundary condition on ∂D2\partial D^{2} obtained by evaluating the BV-BFV partition function on the trinion D2∖(D12⊔D22)D^{2}\setminus(D^{2}_{1}\sqcup D^{2}_{2}). The braiding arises from exchanging the two inner disks by a half-rotation in D2D^{2}, which is a non-trivial path in 𝔼2​(2)≃S1\mathbb{E}_{2}(2)\simeq S^{1}. The resulting 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-monoidal structure encodes the braided monoidal category of boundary conditions for CS theory on the disk. At the perturbative level (over ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!]), the objects of ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} can be identified with representations of the quantum group Uℏ​(𝔤)U_{\hbar}(\mathfrak{g}) (these are the Wilson line labels) with the braiding given by the universal RR-matrix, but the definition above is independent of this identification. See Figure 6 and 7 for a visualization.

Remark 6.3 (Boundary conditions as Wilson lines).

The “boundary conditions on S1=∂D2S^{1}=\partial D^{2}” appearing in Construction 6.2 admit a concrete physical interpretation. They are Wilson line labels. In Chern–Simons theory, a Wilson line is an observable associated to a representation VV of GG, supported on an embedded curve. When the curve is the boundary circle ∂D2\partial D^{2}, the Wilson line imposes a boundary condition on the fields. In particular, the holonomy of the connection around ∂D2\partial D^{2} is weighted by the character of VV. In the RT framework, the simple objects of 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) (the irreducible representations in the Weyl alcove 𝒜k\mathcal{A}_{k}) correspond precisely to the distinct Wilson line types, and the morphism spaces correspond to local operators at junctions between Wilson lines. Thus the objects of ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} are Wilson line labels and the category structure encodes their fusion and braiding, which is the physical origin of the braided monoidal structure described in Construction 6.1. We emphasize that this physical picture, while well-established in the physics literature (see e.g. [67]), has not been formulated as a rigorous theorem within the BV-BFV framework. Making the notion of “boundary condition on a codimension-2 stratum” mathematically precise in the CMR setting is one of the open problems discussed in Section 10.

Remark 6.4.

Construction 6.1 and 6.2 implicitly use an extended version of the BV-BFV formalism, in which data is assigned not only to codimension-1 boundaries (surfaces, carrying BFV phase spaces and vector spaces of states) but also to codimension-2 corners (circles, carrying categories of boundary conditions). In the standard CMR formalism [21, 23], Chern–Simons theory on a 3-manifold MM with boundary Σ=∂M\Sigma=\partial M assigns a BFV phase space to the surface Σ\Sigma and a state in its quantization to the bulk MM. The category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} arises one level lower in the extended TQFT hierarchy. The circle S1S^{1}, viewed as a codimension-2 stratum (the boundary of the spatial slice D2D^{2}), is assigned a category whose objects are boundary conditions on S1S^{1} and whose morphisms are partition functions on the annulus S1×[0,1]S^{1}\times[0,1]. The 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-structure comes from the 2-dimensional geometry of the spatial slice D2D^{2}. The extension of the BV-BFV formalism to this codimension-2 setting is initiated in [21] (which treats manifolds with corners) and is closely related to the factorization algebra approach of [26, 27]; a complete derived-geometric formulation remains part of the program outlined in Section 10. We note that the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-structure on ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} described in Constructions 6.1 and 6.2 is at present a heuristic construction. It relies on the BV-BFV formalism applied to manifolds with corners (the complement of sub-disks in D2D^{2}, times an interval), which goes beyond the standard CMR framework. On the algebraic side, the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-structure on 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) is well-established (it comes from the quasi-triangular Hopf algebra structure on Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g})). The conjecture that the BV-BFV construction reproduces this same 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-structure is part of the content of Conjecture 6.5.

Conjecture 6.5 (𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-equivalence).

The 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-monoidal category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} obtained from BV-BFV quantization of Chern–Simons theory at level kk with gauge group GG is equivalent, as an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category, to the ribbon category 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) at q=ei​π/(k+h∨)q=e^{i\pi/(k+h^{\vee})}:

(41) ℬCS(k)≃𝔼2𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G).\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}\;\simeq_{\mathbb{E}_{2}}\;\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G).
Remark 6.6.

At the perturbative level (working over ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!] rather than specializing qq), this conjecture is closely related to the Etingof–Kazhdan quantization theorem [31], which shows that every Lie bialgebra can be quantized to produce a quantum group, giving a canonical 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-deformation of 𝐑𝐞𝐩​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}(G). The classical rr-matrix underlying the Lie bialgebra structure on 𝔤\mathfrak{g} is precisely the datum encoded in the 11-shifted Poisson structure on [G/G][G/G]. The content of Conjecture 6.5 is that the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-deformation produced by the BV-BFV quantization of Chern–Simons theory on the disk agrees with the one produced by the Etingof–Kazhdan algebraic machinery, and moreover that this agreement persists after specializing to a root of unity and passing to the semisimple quotient. See also [28] for a related perspective connecting Costello’s 4-dimensional Chern–Simons theory to quantum group structures via factorization algebras.

Remark 6.7.

We work with the (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended TQFT convention of Definition 3.6, in which the circle S1S^{1} is the lowest-dimensional object and is assigned the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}. Factorization homology of this 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebra over a surface Σ\Sigma produces the category ∬ΣℬCS(k)\iint_{\Sigma}\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} assigned to Σ\Sigma. This should be distinguished from the fully extended (3​-​2​-​1​-​0)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1\text{-}0)-convention used in the Cobordism Hypothesis (Theorem 4.15), where the point is the lowest-dimensional object, ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} is the value at a point, and S1S^{1} is assigned the Drinfeld center 𝒵​(ℬCS(k))\mathcal{Z}(\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}). The two conventions are equivalent once the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebra is fixed, but the distinction matters when interpreting the BV-BFV construction. The cylinder D2×[0,1]D^{2}\times[0,1] with its boundary conditions on S1=∂D2S^{1}=\partial D^{2} produces the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebra itself (the value at the circle in the (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-convention, or the value at a point in the fully extended convention).

6.2. The main identification

Assuming Conjecture 6.5, the factorization homology machinery of Section 4 would propagate the local identification (the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category assigned to a circle) to the surface-level data (the categories and distinguished objects assigned to closed surfaces). Extending this further to a full (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended TQFT, including cobordism maps, requires the additional conjectural input of quantized Lagrangian correspondences (Conjecture 6.14 below). With this caveat, we state the central conjecture of the paper.

Conjecture 6.8 (Main Conjecture).

There exists a natural equivalence of (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended topological quantum field theories

(42) ZBV,np(k)≃ZRT(k):𝐁𝐨𝐫𝐝3ext⟶2​-​𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭,Z_{\mathrm{BV,np}}^{(k)}\;\simeq\;Z_{\mathrm{RT}}^{(k)}\;:\;\mathrm{\mathbf{Bord}}_{3}^{\mathrm{ext}}\longrightarrow 2\text{-}\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}},

where ZBV,np(k)Z_{\mathrm{BV,np}}^{(k)} denotes the non-perturbative completion of the BV-BFV partition function at level kk and 2​-​𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭2\text{-}\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}} is the 22-category of ℂ\mathbb{C}-linear categories, functors, and natural transformations. At each categorical level, the equivalence identifies:

  1. (i)

    Circles: the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category ℬCS(k)≃𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}\simeq\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) (Conjecture 6.5);

  2. (ii)

    Closed surfaces: the factorization homology categories ∬Σg𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)≃(𝐇,uΣg)\iint_{\Sigma_{g}}\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G)\simeq(\mathbf{H},\,u_{\Sigma_{g}}), with uΣg=VRT​(Σg)u_{\Sigma_{g}}=V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{g}) (Theorem 4.12);

  3. (iii)

    3-cobordisms: the linear maps between RT state spaces ZRT​(M):VRT​(Σ1)→VRT​(Σ2)Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M):V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{1})\to V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{2}).

More precisely, for every closed oriented surface Σg\Sigma_{g}, the BV-BFV state space is identified with the RT state space via the chain

where the distinguished object uΣgu_{\Sigma_{g}} is the RT state space VRT​(Σg)V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{g}). For every oriented 3-dimensional cobordism M:Σ1→Σ2M:\Sigma_{1}\to\Sigma_{2}, the cobordism induces a functor ∭M\iiint_{M} between the factorization homology categories ∬ΣiℬCS(k)\iint_{\Sigma_{i}}\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}. After the identification with 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) via Conjecture 6.5, and the collapse ∬Σi𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)≃(𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭,uΣi)\iint_{\Sigma_{i}}\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G)\simeq(\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}},\,u_{\Sigma_{i}}) via AKZ, this functor induces a linear map between the distinguished objects. The conjecture asserts that this linear map agrees with the RT partition function:

(43)

Here the vertical equivalences are given by the Ai–Kong–Zheng theorem (Theorem 4.12), applied after identifying ℬCS(k)≃𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}\simeq\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) (Conjecture 6.5). The top horizontal arrow is the functor on factorization homology categories induced by the cobordism MM; the bottom horizontal arrow is the linear map between distinguished objects, which is the RT partition function ZRT​(M):VRT​(Σ1)→VRT​(Σ2)Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M):V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{1})\to V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{2}).

Remark 6.9 (Non-perturbative partition function).

The notation ZBV,np(k)Z_{\mathrm{BV,np}}^{(k)} requires clarification. The BV-BFV partition function as constructed by Cattaneo–Mnev–Reshetikhin is inherently perturbative. It takes values in ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!] and is defined by expansion around individual flat connections. By “non-perturbative completion” we mean the functor obtained by constructing the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} from the BV-BFV data on the disk (Construction 6.2), which is defined without reference to any particular flat connection, applying factorization homology to extend this local datum to all surfaces and cobordisms, and specializing to level kk. The resulting functor is non-perturbative in the sense that it does not depend on a choice of classical background and takes values in 𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}} rather than 𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭ℏ\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}_{\hbar}. The content of the Main Conjecture is that this procedure recovers the RT functor.

In other words, the Main Conjecture asserts that the BV-BFV and RT constructions define the same (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended TQFT: the same 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category assigned to the circle, the same categories (with distinguished objects) assigned to closed surfaces, and the same linear maps assigned to 3-cobordisms. The ordinary (non-extended) CMR functor ZBV:𝐁𝐨𝐫𝐝3or→𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭ℏZ_{\mathrm{BV}}:\mathrm{\mathbf{Bord}}_{3}^{\mathrm{or}}\to\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}_{\hbar} of Corollary 2.12 captures only the top two levels (surfaces and 3-manifolds); the conjectural non-perturbative completion promotes this to a (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended functor by adjoining the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} at the circle level. Namely, that both sides define (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended TQFTs determined by an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category (the value at the circle), and the conjecture is that the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-categories are the same. The surface-level identifications then follow from factorization homology, while the cobordism-level identifications require the additional input of quantized Lagrangian correspondences (Conjecture 6.14).

6.3. Detailed proof strategy

We outline a strategy for establishing the Main Conjecture in four steps, proceeding from the classical level to the fully quantum functorial identification. Each step builds on the previous one, and we identify the precise technical challenges at each stage.

6.3.1. Step 1: Classical phase space identification

The first step is at the classical level and is the most well-established part of the program, though a fully rigorous treatment in the derived setting requires care.

Proposition 6.10.

The classical BFV phase space for Chern–Simons theory on a surface Σ\Sigma is canonically identified with the derived character stack

(44) ℱΣ∂,cl≅LocG​(Σ).\mathcal{F}_{\Sigma}^{\partial,\,\mathrm{cl}}\;\cong\;\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma).

Under this identification, the Atiyah–Bott symplectic form (12) on the BFV phase space agrees with the PTVV 0-shifted symplectic structure of Theorem 5.6, and the BFV charge generates the Hamiltonian flow of the moment map for the gauge group action.

Proof sketch.

The space of flat GG-connections on Σ\Sigma modulo gauge transformations is precisely the character variety Hom⁡(π1​(Σ),G)/G=LocG​(Σ)cl\operatorname{Hom}(\pi_{1}(\Sigma),G)/G=\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)_{\mathrm{cl}}. The derived enhancement incorporates two layers of additional structure. These are the stacky (quotient) structure, which encodes the non-trivial stabilizers of flat connections with continuous automorphisms, and the derived structure, which encodes the obstruction–deformation complex C∙​(Σ;𝔤Adρ)C^{\bullet}(\Sigma;\,\mathfrak{g}_{\operatorname{Ad}_{\rho}}). The BFV fields (11) are exactly the shifted de Rham complex Ω∙​(Σ,𝔤)​[1]\Omega^{\bullet}(\Sigma,\mathfrak{g})[1], which is the Dolbeault model for the tangent complex of LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma), and the BFV charge (13) encodes both the flatness constraint (the moment map) and the gauge transformations (the Hamiltonian vector fields). The compatibility of the symplectic structures follows from the work of Calaque [18] and Safronov [60] on the AKSZ–PTVV correspondence (Theorem 5.9), though a complete verification at the level of derived stacks (as opposed to the smooth locus of the classical truncation) requires the full machinery of derived symplectic geometry. ∎

This step tells us that both, the BV-BFV and derived algebraic geometry framework, are quantizing the same classical object. The divergence between them, if any, must therefore arise at the quantum level.

6.3.2. Step 2: Quantization comparison

This is the most technically demanding step in the program. We must show that two a priori different quantization procedures, the BV-BFV perturbative quantization and the shifted deformation quantization (in the framework of CPTVV [20], Safronov [61], and Etingof–Kazhdan [31]), produce the same output when applied to the same classical input.

Conjecture 6.11 (Quantization agreement).

The BV-BFV quantization of Chern–Simons theory on D2×[0,1]D^{2}\times[0,1] (producing the 𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} of boundary conditions on the lateral circle S1=∂D2S^{1}=\partial D^{2}, as in Construction 6.2) agrees with the shifted deformation quantization of the 11-shifted symplectic stack LocG​(S1)=[G/G]\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(S^{1})=[G/G], as constructed in the framework of CPTVV [20], Safronov [61], and Etingof–Kazhdan [31].

Remark 6.12.

Both procedures start from the same classical data, which is the adjoint quotient [G/G][G/G] with its 11-shifted symplectic structure, and both produce 𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-categories (monoidal categories), which are deformations of the classical representation category 𝐑𝐞𝐩​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}(G) over the formal parameter ℏ\hbar. The conjecture asserts that these two deformations are isomorphic as 𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-categories, both being equivalent to 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) for q=eℏ/2q=e^{\hbar/2}. The promotion to an 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-equivalence (Conjecture 6.5) requires additionally matching the braiding, which on the BV-BFV side arises from the disk-embedding geometry of Construction 6.1(iii), and on the algebraic side from the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-structure on the quantum group Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}).

We break the argument into three sub-steps, each addressing a different aspect of the comparison.

Step 2a: Formality of the BV-BFV data. The first task is to show that the BV-BFV quantization on the cylinder D2×[0,1]D^{2}\times[0,1] can be expressed purely in terms of the L∞L_{\infty}-algebra structure on the tangent complex of LocG​(S1)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(S^{1}). The key simplification is that D2D^{2} is contractible, i.e., π1​(D2)=0\pi_{1}(D^{2})=0, so there is a unique flat connection (the trivial one), and the construction of ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} reduces to computing the BV pushforward of the Chern–Simons action from the fields on the interior of the spatial disk D2D^{2} to the fields on its boundary circle S1=∂D2S^{1}=\partial D^{2}. Since there is only one critical point, there are no non-perturbative corrections (no tunneling between distinct flat connections), and the gauge-fixed perturbative expansion captures the full deformation. The effective action on the boundary is therefore determined entirely by the L∞L_{\infty}-structure on the Lie algebra 𝔤\mathfrak{g}, the Lie bracket and its higher homotopies. This is closely related to Kontsevich’s formality theorem, which asserts that the Hochschild cochain complex of 𝒪​(𝔤∗)\mathcal{O}(\mathfrak{g}^{*}) is formal as an L∞L_{\infty}-algebra, and ensures that the perturbative expansion converges (in the formal sense) to the correct answer.

Step 2b: Matching with Kontsevich–Etingof–Kazhdan. The deformation quantization of [G/G][G/G] is governed by the Etingof–Kazhdan quantization of the Lie bialgebra (𝔤,δr)(\mathfrak{g},\delta_{r}) determined by the classical rr-matrix r∈𝔤⊗𝔤r\in\mathfrak{g}\otimes\mathfrak{g}, which encodes the 11-shifted Poisson structure on the adjoint quotient. On the BV-BFV side, the same rr-matrix arises from the propagator on the disk. In particular, the Green’s function for the covariant Laplacian on D2D^{2}, when restricted to the boundary circle, produces the Drinfeld associator, which is the key analytic datum governing the quantum group structure. The Feynman diagram expansion of the BV-BFV partition function on the disk computes the Kontsevich integral, which by the Etingof–Kazhdan theorem [31] yields exactly the quantum group Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}).

The technical heart of this step is the observation that the BV gauge-fixing on the disk, using the standard round metric, produces a propagator whose boundary behavior encodes precisely the Knizhnik–Zamolodchikov (KZ) connection, which is the flat connection on configuration spaces of points on the boundary circle that governs the braiding and associativity in the quantum group. The holonomy of the KZ connection gives the RR-matrix of Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}), and the monodromy around higher-order configurations gives the higher associators. This circle of ideas strongly suggests that the Feynman diagram expansion of Chern–Simons theory on the disk produces the quantum group structure, but assembling these ingredients into a rigorous proof of Conjecture 6.11 remains a substantial open problem. In particular, the passage from the analytic data (propagators, KZ connection) to the algebraic data (𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category structure) requires a derived-geometric reformulation of the BV-BFV formalism that is not yet available.

Step 2c: Root of unity specialization. The output of Steps 2a and 2b is a formal deformation, meaning an 𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-category over ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!] with q=eℏ/2q=e^{\hbar/2}. To make contact with the RT theory, we must specialize to the non-formal value q=ei​π/(k+h∨)q=e^{i\pi/(k+h^{\vee})}, which is a root of unity. This passage from formal to actual is subtle and requires three sub-steps:

  1. (i)

    Algebraization. One must show that the formal deformation of 𝐑𝐞𝐩​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}(G) over ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!] extends to an algebraic deformation over ℂ​[ℏ]\mathbb{C}[\hbar] (or a suitable Rees algebra) that can be evaluated at specific values of ℏ\hbar. For quantum groups, this extension is known to exist. It is provided by the explicit Drinfeld–Jimbo presentation of Uq​(𝔤)U_{q}(\mathfrak{g}) as an algebra over ℂ​[q,q−1]\mathbb{C}[q,q^{-1}]. However, it must be verified that this algebraic family matches the formal family produced by the BV-BFV expansion.

  2. (ii)

    Specialization. Setting ℏ=2​π​i/(k+h∨)\hbar=2\pi i/(k+h^{\vee}) yields a braided monoidal category 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) at a root of unity. This category is no longer semisimple. In fact, the quantum dimension of certain objects vanishes, and the category contains non-trivial extensions and negligible morphisms.

  3. (iii)

    Semisimplification. To obtain a modular tensor category, one passes to the quotient

    (45) 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)=𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)/𝒩\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G)\;=\;\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G)\,/\,\mathcal{N}

    by the tensor ideal of negligible morphisms (those whose quantum trace vanishes in every composition). This categorical localization is what produces the finite, semisimple, modular tensor category used in the RT construction. A key technical point, essential for the consistency of our program, is that this semisimplification must commute with factorization homology. In particular, one needs

    (46) ∬Σ(𝒜/𝒩)≃(∬Σ𝒜)/𝒩Σ,\iint_{\Sigma}\big(\mathcal{A}\,/\,\mathcal{N}\big)\;\simeq\;\Big(\iint_{\Sigma}\mathcal{A}\Big)\big/\,\mathcal{N}_{\Sigma},

    ensuring that it does not matter whether one semisimplifies before or after integrating over the surface. The interaction between non-semisimple categories and TQFT constructions, which is directly relevant to this step, is developed in [29, 25].

6.3.3. Step 3: Factorization homology transport (surfaces)

Once Conjecture 6.5 is established, factorization homology would propagate the local identification to surface-level data: for every closed surface Σg\Sigma_{g}, the factorization homology ∬ΣgℬCS(k)≃∬Σg𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\iint_{\Sigma_{g}}\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}\simeq\iint_{\Sigma_{g}}\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) would be identified, and the AKZ theorem would then give uΣg=VRT​(Σg)u_{\Sigma_{g}}=V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{g}). We emphasize that this step concerns only the surface-level invariants (the assignment of categories and distinguished objects to closed surfaces). It does not by itself establish the identification of cobordism maps; that requires the additional input of Step 4 below.

The state space that BV-BFV assigns to a surface Σ\Sigma is identified with the RT state space through the following chain of equivalences:

(47) ℋΣBV←Step 2Quant​(LocG​(Σ))→BBJ∼∬Σ𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G).\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma}^{\mathrm{BV}}\xleftarrow{\;\text{Step 2}\;}\mathrm{Quant}\big(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)\big)\xrightarrow[\text{BBJ}]{\;\sim\;}\iint_{\Sigma}\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G).

At generic qq, the right-hand side is a non-trivial quantum character variety. At a root of unity, after semisimplification, Theorem 4.12 gives ∬Σg𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)≃(𝐇,uΣg)\iint_{\Sigma_{g}}\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G)\simeq(\mathbf{H},\,u_{\Sigma_{g}}) with uΣg≅VRT​(Σg)u_{\Sigma_{g}}\cong V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{g}).

Remark 6.13.

The arrow labeled “Step 2” in the diagram of Conjecture 6.8 should be understood as follows. The comparison between the BV-BFV quantization and the deformation quantization Quant​(LocG​(Σ))\mathrm{Quant}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)) is not performed surface by surface. Rather, the key identification occurs on the disk D2D^{2}. One shows that the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)} produced by BV-BFV (Construction 6.2) is equivalent to 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) produced by deformation quantization of [G/G]=LocG​(S1)[G/G]=\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(S^{1}). Factorization homology then automatically transports this local equivalence to all surfaces, giving

ℋΣBV≃∬ΣℬCS(k)≃∬Σ𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)≃Quant​(LocG​(Σ)),\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma}^{\mathrm{BV}}\;\simeq\;\iint_{\Sigma}\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}\;\simeq\;\iint_{\Sigma}\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G)\;\simeq\;\mathrm{Quant}\big(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma)\big),

where the last equivalence is the BBJ theorem (Theorem 5.11). The power of this approach is that the infinite-dimensional analytical problem of comparing quantizations on a general surface Σ\Sigma is reduced to a finite-dimensional algebraic problem on the disk.

The power of this step is its uniformity. The identification ℋΣBV≅VRT​(Σ)\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma}^{\mathrm{BV}}\cong V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma) is not established by a separate argument for each genus, but follows from a single equivalence of 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-categories combined with a general machine (factorization homology) that automatically produces the correct answer for every surface, including surfaces with punctures and boundary components.

6.3.4. Step 4: Cobordism maps

Steps 1–3 address the surface-level data: the identification of the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-categories assigned to circles and, via factorization homology and AKZ, the state spaces assigned to closed surfaces. To complete the proof of the Main Conjecture at the level of a full (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended TQFT, we must also match the linear maps assigned to 3-dimensional cobordisms. This is a logically independent step that requires additional input beyond factorization homology. For every cobordism M:Σ1→Σ2M:\Sigma_{1}\to\Sigma_{2}, the BV-BFV partition function ZBV​(M):ℋΣ1→ℋΣ2Z_{\mathrm{BV}}(M):\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma_{1}}\to\mathcal{H}_{\Sigma_{2}} must agree with the RT map ZRT​(M):VRT​(Σ1)→VRT​(Σ2)Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M):V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{1})\to V_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma_{2}) under the identification of Step 3.

The key input is the derived-geometric interpretation of the BV-BFV partition function as a quantized Lagrangian correspondence.

Conjecture 6.14 (Quantized Lagrangian correspondence).

The Lagrangian morphism

(48) LocG​(M)⟶LocG​(Σ1)×LocG​(Σ2)−\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)\longrightarrow\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma_{1})\times\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma_{2})^{-}

of Theorem 5.9 (where the superscript (−)(-) denotes the opposite symplectic structure) quantizes to a bimodule over the quantized algebras of Σ1\Sigma_{1} and Σ2\Sigma_{2}. This bimodule, viewed as a linear map between the quantized state spaces, is the BV-BFV partition function ZBV​(M)Z_{\mathrm{BV}}(M). Under the identifications of Step 3, it equals the RT map ZRT​(M)Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M).

Remark 6.15.

This conjecture has a natural classical antecedent. In ordinary symplectic geometry, a Lagrangian submanifold L⊂T∗​X1−×T∗​X2L\subset T^{*}X_{1}^{-}\times T^{*}X_{2} defines a canonical relation between the phase spaces of X1X_{1} and X2X_{2}, which is a multi-valued, measure-preserving map. Upon geometric quantization, such a Lagrangian correspondence becomes a linear map between the quantum Hilbert spaces. This is the Guillemin–Sternberg “quantization commutes with reduction” principle, and it is the mechanism by which classical correspondences give rise to quantum operators.

The shifted analogue works in the same way, one categorical level up. The Lagrangian morphism LocG​(M)→LocG​(∂M)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)\to\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\partial M) is a “shifted canonical relation” between the derived phase spaces of the two boundary components. Its deformation quantization produces a bimodule (the categorified version of a linear map) and this bimodule is precisely the BV-BFV partition function of the cobordism MM, reformulated in the language of derived algebraic geometry. The content of Conjecture 6.14 is that this derived-geometric quantization of the Lagrangian correspondence reproduces the RT cobordism map, closing the circle between the two frameworks.

6.4. Compatibility with cellular decomposition

As an important consistency check on the program, we record one further conjecture relating the two computational approaches to the BV-BFV partition function. Namely, the cellular (simplicial) approach of Construction 2.14 and the factorization homology approach.

Conjecture 6.16 (Cellular-factorization compatibility).

The cellular BV-BFV partition function on a triangulated 3-manifold (M,Δ)(M,\Delta), computed by iteratively gluing finite-dimensional BV-BFV data along simplices (Construction 2.14), is equivalent to the value ZℬCS(k)​(M)Z_{\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}}(M) assigned to MM by the (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended TQFT determined by the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}. More precisely, the iterated BV pushforward along simplices of Δ\Delta should reproduce the same linear map between state spaces that the extended TQFT assigns to the cobordism MM, with the factorization homology of surfaces providing the intermediate state-space identifications.

This conjecture is natural because both constructions compute the partition function by assembling local data according to a decomposition of MM, and both satisfy the same gluing axioms. The cellular approach uses a triangulation and finite-dimensional BV-BFV algebras on simplices; the factorization homology approach uses the Ran space of disk embeddings and the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebra ℬCS(k)\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}. A proof would require showing that the finite-dimensional BV-BFV data assigned to a simplex by the CMR construction [24] can be identified with the restriction of the factorization algebra to a small disk, and that the iterated BV pushforward (gluing simplices) agrees with the colimit (gluing disks). If established, this conjecture would provide concrete computational access to the factorization homology side of our bridge, reducing it to the finite-dimensional integrals of the cellular theory. This is supported by both constructions satisfying the same gluing axioms and being determined by local data.

7. Koszul Duality and the Perturbative/Non-perturbative Bridge

The previous section formulated our main conjectures and proof strategy in terms of matching the BV-BFV and RT constructions via factorization homology. In this section, we address a complementary question: what is the algebraic mechanism that reconstructs the global, non-perturbative data (the modular tensor category, the exact RT invariant) from the local, perturbative data (the Feynman diagram expansion around each flat connection)? The answer, we argue, is 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-Koszul duality.

7.1. The general theory of 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-Koszul duality

Koszul duality is a classical construction in homological algebra that exchanges “small” and “large” algebraic objects, for instance, the symmetric algebra on a vector space and the exterior algebra on its dual. In the 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-setting, this duality is vastly generalized. It provides a systematic correspondence between local (formal, perturbative) algebraic data and global (non-perturbative) algebraic data, with the level of commutativity controlled by nn.

Theorem 7.1 (𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-Koszul duality; Lurie [52], Francis–Gaitsgory [34]).

There is a contravariant equivalence (the Koszul duality functor)

(49) 𝔻n:𝐀𝐥𝐠𝔼naug​(𝒱)op⟶𝐀𝐥𝐠𝔼naug​(𝒱)\mathbb{D}_{n}:\mathrm{\mathbf{Alg}}_{\mathbb{E}_{n}}^{\mathrm{aug}}(\mathcal{V})^{\mathrm{op}}\longrightarrow\mathrm{\mathbf{Alg}}_{\mathbb{E}_{n}}^{\mathrm{aug}}(\mathcal{V})

between augmented 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebras in a suitable ∞\infty-category 𝒱\mathcal{V}. The construction specializes as follows:

  1. (i)

    For n=0n=0: Koszul duality is the classical bar/cobar adjunction. The Koszul dual of an augmented algebra AA is its bar construction 𝔻0​(A)=Bar​(A)\mathbb{D}_{0}(A)=\mathrm{Bar}(A).

  2. (ii)

    For n=1n=1 (the associative case): Koszul duality generalizes the bar construction to A∞A_{\infty}-algebras. For the universal enveloping algebra U​(𝔤)U(\mathfrak{g}) of a Lie algebra, the Koszul dual is the Chevalley–Eilenberg cochain complex C∙​(𝔤)C^{\bullet}(\mathfrak{g}).

  3. (iii)

    For general nn: the Koszul dual of an 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra AA can be computed via factorization homology over the nn-sphere, i.e. 𝔻n​(A)≃∫SnA\mathbb{D}_{n}(A)\simeq\int_{S^{n}}A.

Remark 7.2.

The geometric content of Koszul duality can be understood as follows. An augmented 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-algebra AA encodes the structure of a “formal neighborhood” of a distinguished point in some moduli space. It is the algebra of functions on an infinitesimal neighborhood, together with all the higher homotopical data of the deformation problem. Its Koszul dual 𝔻n​(A)\mathbb{D}_{n}(A) encodes the “global” algebraic structure of that moduli space, which is the algebra of functions (or cochains, or sheaves) on the whole space, reconstructed from the local data. This is an enormous generalization of the classical fact that the symmetric algebra Sym∙⁡(V)\operatorname{Sym}^{\bullet}(V) (functions on the formal neighborhood of the origin in VV) and the exterior algebra ⋀∙(V∗)\bigwedge^{\bullet}(V^{*}) (the de Rham cohomology of the dual space) are Koszul dual. In our setting, the “formal neighborhood” is the perturbative BV-BFV data around a flat connection, and the “global structure” is the full non-perturbative category of boundary conditions.

7.2. Application to the Chern–Simons / RT bridge

We now apply the general theory of 𝔼n\mathbb{E}_{n}-Koszul duality to the specific problem at hand, which is the reconstruction of the global, non-perturbative category of Chern–Simons boundary conditions from the local perturbative data around each flat connection.

Construction 7.3 (Local-to-global via Koszul duality).

The reconstruction proceeds in three stages.

Local (perturbative) data. At each flat connection [A]∈FlatG​(M)/G[A]\in\mathrm{Flat}_{G}(M)/G, the BV-BFV perturbative expansion around AA produces a formal 𝔼0\mathbb{E}_{0}-algebra, which is the BV algebra of perturbative observables

(50) ObsAq​(M)=(C∙​(M;𝔤AdA),QA,{−,−}A).\mathrm{Obs}^{q}_{A}(M)=\big(C^{\bullet}(M;\,\mathfrak{g}_{\operatorname{Ad}_{A}}),\;Q_{A},\;\{-,-\}_{A}\big).

This is the twisted de Rham complex equipped with the BV differential and bracket induced by the Chern–Simons action expanded around AA. As an 𝔼0\mathbb{E}_{0}-algebra, this is a pointed cochain complex. It encodes the formal moduli problem of deforming the flat connection AA, together with the perturbative partition function (a distinguished cocycle) around AA.

Formal Koszul dual. Applying 𝔼0\mathbb{E}_{0}-Koszul duality (the bar construction) to each local algebra gives

(51) 𝔻0​(ObsAq​(M))≃𝒪^[A],\mathbb{D}_{0}\big(\mathrm{Obs}^{q}_{A}(M)\big)\;\simeq\;\widehat{\mathcal{O}}_{[A]},

the completed local ring of LocG​(M)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M) at the point [A][A]. This identification is a consequence of Lurie’s theorem on formal moduli problems that says that every formal 𝔼0\mathbb{E}_{0}-algebra (formal moduli problem) is equivalent to the data of a formal neighborhood of a point in a derived stack, and Koszul duality reconstructs the completed structure sheaf of that neighborhood from the deformation data.

Global reconstruction. The local Koszul duals 𝒪^[A]\widehat{\mathcal{O}}_{[A]}, one for each flat connection, must be assembled into a global object. This gluing is performed by the formalism of ind-coherent sheaves on the formal completion:

(52) ℬCS≃Glue​{𝔻0​(ObsAq​(M)):[A]∈LocG​(M)}≃𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧).\displaystyle\begin{split}\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}\;&\simeq\;\mathrm{Glue}\big\{\mathbb{D}_{0}(\mathrm{Obs}^{q}_{A}(M))\;:\;[A]\in\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)\big\}\;\\ &\simeq\;\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}\big(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}\big).\end{split}

The passage from local completed rings to a global category of sheaves is the algebraic analogue of reconstructing a sheaf from its stalks. See Figure 9 for a visualization.

Conjecture 7.4 (Koszul reconstruction).

The category of Chern–Simons boundary conditions is equivalent to ind-coherent sheaves on the formal completion of the character stack, i.e., we have

(53) ℬCS≃𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧),\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}\;\simeq\;\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}\big(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}\big),

where 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}} denotes the category of ind-coherent sheaves in the sense of Gaitsgory–Rozenblyum [37] and LocG​(M)∧\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge} is the formal completion of the derived character stack along its classical truncation. Concretely, this means that the BV-BFV partition function around each flat connection determines (and is determined by) the local structure of an ind-coherent sheaf at the corresponding point of the character stack, and the global sheaf encodes all of the perturbative data simultaneously, together with the non-perturbative “gluing data” that relates the different perturbative expansions to one another.

Remark 7.5.

It is essential that the conjecture involves 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}} (ind-coherent sheaves) rather than 𝐐𝐂𝐨𝐡\mathrm{\mathbf{QCoh}} (quasi-coherent sheaves). On a smooth scheme or stack, these two categories are equivalent, but on singular or derived stacks they differ significantly. The character stack LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) is typically singular because at reducible flat connections, where the gauge group has a non-trivial stabilizer, the moduli space develops orbifold and worse singularities, and 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}} is the correct category for sheaf-theoretic quantization in this setting. The distinction is analogous to the one between DD-modules and quasi-coherent sheaves in the geometric Langlands program. In particular, DD-modules (the analogue of 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}) are the objects that transform correctly under Langlands duality, while quasi-coherent sheaves do not. The role of singular support conditions for coherent sheaves on the character stack, which is expected to refine our conjecture, is developed in [1].

We illustrate the Koszul reconstruction concretely in the simplest non-trivial example.

Example 7.6 (Solid torus).

Consider M=S1×D2M=S^{1}\times D^{2} (the solid torus) with gauge group G=S​L2​(ℂ)G=SL_{2}(\mathbb{C}). Since π1​(S1×D2)=ℤ\pi_{1}(S^{1}\times D^{2})=\mathbb{Z}, a flat connection is determined by the holonomy around the S1S^{1} factor, which is a conjugacy class in S​L2SL_{2}. The character stack is therefore LocS​L2​(S1×D2)=[S​L2/S​L2]\mathrm{Loc}_{SL_{2}}(S^{1}\times D^{2})=[SL_{2}/SL_{2}], and the classical character variety is ℂ\mathbb{C}, parametrized by the trace t=z+z−1t=z+z^{-1} of the holonomy eigenvalues {z,z−1}\{z,z^{-1}\}.

The perturbative data varies over this moduli space. At a generic point t≠±2t\neq\pm 2 (corresponding to z≠±1z\neq\pm 1, i.e., an irreducible representation), the stabilizer is the maximal torus ℂ∗\mathbb{C}^{*}, the tangent complex has trivial cohomology in the relevant degrees, and the perturbative algebra is simply Obszq≃ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathrm{Obs}^{q}_{z}\simeq\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!] (the expansion is trivial because there are no deformations). At t=2t=2 (the trivial representation, z=1z=1), the stabilizer jumps to all of S​L2SL_{2}, the tangent complex acquires a 3-dimensional kernel, and the perturbative algebra ObsA0q\mathrm{Obs}^{q}_{A_{0}} is the non-trivial BV algebra C∙​(𝔤)​[[ℏ]]C^{\bullet}(\mathfrak{g})[\![\hbar]\!] encoding the deformation theory of the trivial flat connection.

The Koszul reconstruction assembles these local data (trivial at generic points, non-trivial at the identity) into a single global ind-coherent sheaf on [S​L2/S​L2]∧[SL_{2}/SL_{2}]^{\wedge}. After quantization at level kk and semisimplification, this sheaf determines a vector in the BFV state space of the boundary ∂(S1×D2)=T2\partial(S^{1}\times D^{2})=T^{2}, which is the “vacuum vector” generating the representation ring of 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(𝔰​𝔩2)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(\mathfrak{sl}_{2}). The fact that the non-trivial perturbative data is concentrated at the identity (precisely where the stabilizer is largest) is a concrete manifestation of the general principle that the interesting non-perturbative structure arises from the singular locus of the character stack.

7.3. Relation to resurgence

The Koszul reconstruction formula (52) assembles the global category ℬCS\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}} from local perturbative data by gluing the formal Koszul duals 𝒪^[A]\widehat{\mathcal{O}}_{[A]} across the character stack. A natural question is: what information is carried by the gluing data itself, i.e., the transition maps that connect the formal neighborhoods of different flat connections?

We conjecture that this gluing data corresponds to the Stokes data governing the resurgent structure of the perturbative Chern–Simons partition function. In the analytic approach to the asymptotic expansion (2), the perturbative series around different flat connections are not independent. In fact, their Borel resummations are related by Stokes automorphisms that encode non-perturbative tunneling effects. In the Koszul duality framework, these same tunneling effects appear algebraically as the morphisms in 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧)\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}) that connect the skyscraper sheaves at different points of the character stack. We develop this connection in detail in the next section.

8. Connection to Resurgence

The Koszul duality framework of the previous section provides an algebraic mechanism for assembling non-perturbative data from perturbative ingredients. There is, however, a parallel and more classical approach to the same problem, which is the theory of resurgence, that reconstructs non-perturbative quantities from the analytic structure of divergent perturbative series. In this section, we develop the connection between the two approaches and conjecture that they encode the same information.

8.1. Resurgence in Chern–Simons theory

The perturbative series appearing in the asymptotic expansion (2) are expected to be resurgent. Although they diverge (have zero radius of convergence), they possess a rich analytic structure that, in principle, allows the exact non-perturbative answer to be reconstructed from the perturbative data alone, provided one also knows certain discrete “Stokes constants” that govern the interaction between different perturbative sectors.

Definition 8.1 (Resurgent series).

A formal power series f~​(ℏ)=∑n≥0an​ℏn\tilde{f}(\hbar)=\sum_{n\geq 0}a_{n}\,\hbar^{n} is called resurgent if its Borel transform f^​(ζ)=∑n≥01n!​an​ζn\hat{f}(\zeta)=\sum_{n\geq 0}\tfrac{1}{n!}a_{n}\,\zeta^{n} (which typically has a finite radius of convergence) admits analytic continuation along any path in ℂ∖Ω\mathbb{C}\setminus\Omega, where Ω\Omega is a discrete set of alien singularities. At each singularity ω∈Ω\omega\in\Omega, the analytic continuation is multi-valued, and the alien derivative Δω​f~\Delta_{\omega}\tilde{f} measures the discontinuity, i.e., the difference between the analytic continuations along paths passing to the left and right of ω\omega. The alien derivatives are themselves resurgent series, and the collection of all alien derivatives and their iterations forms the alien algebra, which encodes the complete non-perturbative structure of the original series.

In the Chern–Simons context, the resurgent structure takes a particularly geometric form. The Borel singularities of the perturbative series around a flat connection AA occur at the values

(54) ωA​B=CS​(B)−CS​(A)(modℤ)\omega_{AB}=\mathrm{CS}(B)-\mathrm{CS}(A)\pmod{\mathbb{Z}}

for other flat connections BB on MM. The alien derivative ΔωA​B\Delta_{\omega_{AB}} at such a singularity encodes the non-perturbative correction from “tunneling” between the flat connections AA and BB. It measures how much of the perturbative expansion around BB leaks into the Borel resummation of the series around AA when one crosses the corresponding Stokes ray in the ℏ\hbar-plane.

Example 8.2 (Gukov–Mariño–Putrov [41]).

The resurgent structure of Chern–Simons theory has been studied in detail for the Poincaré homology sphere Σ​(2,3,5)\Sigma(2,3,5) with gauge group G=S​U​(2)G=SU(2). This manifold admits exactly two flat connections: the trivial connection A0A_{0} and a single irreducible connection A1A_{1}. The perturbative series around both have been computed to high loop order, and the Borel singularity structure has been verified. In particular, the series around A0A_{0} has a Borel singularity at ω=CS​(A1)−CS​(A0)\omega=\mathrm{CS}(A_{1})-\mathrm{CS}(A_{0}), and vice versa. The alien derivatives at these singularities have been computed explicitly, and they precisely account for the discrepancy between the Borel-resummed perturbative contributions and the exact RT invariant ZRT​(Σ​(2,3,5))Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma(2,3,5)). This provides a striking confirmation that the non-perturbative content of the RT invariant is, at least in this case, fully encoded in the resurgent structure of the perturbative series. Further developments on the resurgent structure of Chern–Simons invariants appear in [39]; for the connection to BPS spectra and categorification, see [42].

8.2. Resurgence meets Koszul duality

We conjecture that the resurgent structure described above and the Koszul duality reconstruction of Section 7 may be two different languages for expressing the same underlying phenomenon. In particular, the reconstruction of global, non-perturbative data from local, perturbative ingredients together with discrete “transition data” connecting different perturbative sectors. We conjecture that these two languages are not merely analogous but encode the same mathematical structure, though establishing this precisely would require significant further work.

Conjecture 8.3 (Resurgence–Koszul correspondence).

The Stokes data governing the resurgent structure of the perturbative Chern–Simons partition function are identified with the transition maps in the Koszul duality reconstruction (52). Specifically, the following hold:

  1. (i)

    The alien derivative ΔωA​B\Delta_{\omega_{AB}}, which measures the non-perturbative tunneling between flat connections AA and BB, corresponds to a morphism in 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧)\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}) from the skyscraper sheaf δ[A]\delta_{[A]} to the skyscraper sheaf δ[B]\delta_{[B]}, given by the residue of an ind-coherent sheaf at the connecting singularity of the character stack.

  2. (ii)

    The Stokes automorphism 𝔖θ\mathfrak{S}_{\theta} associated to a ray θ\theta in the Borel plane corresponds to the wall-crossing automorphism of 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧)\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}) associated to the corresponding stability condition on the derived category.

  3. (iii)

    The full resurgent structure, which is the totality of alien derivatives and their compositions, forming the “alien algebra”, is equivalent to the A∞A_{\infty}-structure on the Ext-algebra

    (55) ⨁[A],[B]∈FlatG​(M)/GExt𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡∙​(δ[A],δ[B]).\bigoplus_{[A],\,[B]\in\mathrm{Flat}_{G}(M)/G}\mathrm{Ext}^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}}\!\big(\delta_{[A]},\;\delta_{[B]}\big).
Remark 8.4.

If true, this conjecture provides a purely algebraic interpretation of resurgence phenomena in Chern–Simons theory. The non-perturbative corrections (trans-series terms, Stokes constants, the intricate pattern of tunneling between different saddle points) are not “extra data” that must be supplied in addition to the perturbative expansion. Rather, they are algebraically encoded in the Koszul duality relations between the local perturbative algebras at different points of the character stack. The category 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧)\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}) simultaneously “knows” both the perturbative data (through its restriction to formal neighborhoods of individual flat connections, recovering the BV-BFV expansions) and the non-perturbative data (through the global morphisms between skyscraper sheaves at different points, recovering the Stokes constants). From this perspective, the passage from perturbative to non-perturbative is not an analytic problem (requiring delicate Borel resummation and Stokes analysis) but an algebraic one. It is the passage from local sections to global sections in the sheaf theory on the character stack. We stress that this picture, while conceptually compelling, is at this stage a heuristic guide rather than a theorem; making it rigorous would require, at minimum, a precise definition of the gluing data and a proof that the resulting ind-coherent sheaf recovers the expected non-perturbative invariants.

8.3. Trans-series and Borel–Laplace reconstruction

To make the connection between resurgence and Koszul duality more explicit, we recall the standard trans-series representation of the RT invariant. The resurgent reconstruction expresses the exact invariant as a sum over all flat connections, with each contribution given by a Borel-resummed perturbative series dressed by exponential and power-law prefactors, and corrected by instanton contributions at all orders:

(56) ZRT​(M,k)=∑[A]∈FlatG​(M)/G∑ℓ≥0CA,ℓ⋅e2​π​i​k​CS​(A)⋅kdA/2−ℓ⋅𝒮θ​[Z~A(ℓ)​(k−1)].Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M,k)=\sum_{[A]\in\mathrm{Flat}_{G}(M)/G}\;\sum_{\ell\geq 0}\;C_{A,\ell}\cdot e^{2\pi ik\,\mathrm{CS}(A)}\cdot k^{d_{A}/2-\ell}\cdot\mathcal{S}_{\theta}\!\big[\tilde{Z}_{A}^{(\ell)}(k^{-1})\big].
