Here Z~A(0)\tilde{Z}_{A}^{(0)} is the perturbative series around the flat connection AA (the output of the BV-BFV formalism), Z~A(ℓ)\tilde{Z}_{A}^{(\ell)} for ℓ≥1\ell\geq 1 are the higher instanton corrections (the “trans-series sectors”), CA,ℓC_{A,\ell} are the trans-series parameters (complex constants not determined by perturbation theory alone), and 𝒮θ\mathcal{S}_{\theta} denotes the Borel resummation in the direction θ\theta in the complex ℏ\hbar-plane.

In the language of Conjecture 8.3, we propose the following speculative algebraic interpretations of the objects in this formula. The trans-series parameters CA,ℓC_{A,\ell} are expected to correspond to morphisms in 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧)\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}) connecting different flat connections. In particular, they are the “global” data of the ind-coherent sheaf that the Koszul reconstruction produces, encoding precisely the information that is invisible to any single perturbative expansion. We suggest that the Borel resummation 𝒮θ\mathcal{S}_{\theta} in a direction θ\theta should correspond to choosing a tt-structure or stability condition on 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧)\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}) compatible with the ray θ\theta. Different choices of θ\theta give different resummations (related by Stokes automorphisms); we expect this to mirror the fact that different stability conditions give different resolutions of the same derived category, though making this analogy precise remains an open problem. The exact RT invariant, which is independent of θ\theta, should then correspond to a canonical (stability-independent) object in the category. This interpretation, if correct, would give a purely algebraic explanation of the non-perturbative completeness of resurgence.

If correct, this perspective would offer an algebraic explanation for the non-perturbative completeness of resurgence (the expectation that all trans-series parameters can, in principle, be extracted from the perturbative data plus the alien derivatives). It would follow from the categorical assertion that 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧)\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}) is generated by the skyscraper sheaves at the flat connections. We stress that this remains a speculative interpretation. Whether it can be made mathematically precise, and whether the relevant categorical generation property actually holds in the required generality, are significant open problems.

9. Evidence and Explicit Computations

We now present evidence supporting the conjectures of Sections 6–8, by examining cases where explicit computation is possible. These examples serve as consistency checks and as motivation for the general program, but we emphasize that agreement in special cases does not constitute a proof of the general conjectures, as the main difficulties (particularly the derived-geometric reformulation of BV-BFV and the root-of-unity specialization) are not visible in these simplified settings. These examples range from the fully solvable abelian case (where the entire program can be carried out rigorously) to the non-abelian cases of low genus, lens spaces, and Seifert manifolds (where extensive partial results provide strong support).

9.1. The abelian case: G=U​(1)G=U(1)

When the gauge group is abelian, the entire program collapses to a tractable and completely explicit form, providing a proof-of-concept for the general framework.

Proposition 9.1.

For G=U​(1)G=U(1) at level kk, the conjectural picture of this paper can be verified explicitly in a simplified form. In this case many of the technical difficulties (divergent series, non-trivial Koszul duality, semisimplification) are absent, and the various frameworks reduce to elementary constructions:

  1. (i)

    The character stack LocU​(1)​(Σg)=H1​(Σg;U​(1))=(S1)2​g\mathrm{Loc}_{U(1)}(\Sigma_{g})=H^{1}(\Sigma_{g};U(1))=(S^{1})^{2g} is a smooth 2​g2g-dimensional torus, equipped with its standard symplectic structure given by the intersection form on H1​(Σg;ℤ)H^{1}(\Sigma_{g};\mathbb{Z}) tensored with ℝ/ℤ\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}. The PTVV 0-shifted symplectic structure of Theorem 5.6 reduces to this classical form, and the derived structure is trivial (since the moduli space is already smooth and unobstructed).

  2. (ii)

    The role of the quantum group is played, in a schematic sense, by Uq​(𝔲​(1))≅ℂ​[ℤ]U_{q}(\mathfrak{u}(1))\cong\mathbb{C}[\mathbb{Z}] (the group algebra of the integers; this identification is an expository shorthand, as the abelian case does not require the full Drinfeld–Jimbo machinery). The corresponding modular tensor category at level kk is 𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭ℤ/k​ℤ\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}_{\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z}}, which is the category of ℤ/k​ℤ\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z}-graded vector spaces, with braiding ca,b=qa​bc_{a,b}=q^{ab} for q=e2​π​i/kq=e^{2\pi i/k}. The SS-matrix is Sa​b=k−1/2​qa​bS_{ab}=k^{-1/2}\,q^{ab}, which is the discrete Fourier transform matrix and hence invertible, confirming modularity.

  3. (iii)

    The RT invariant is closely related to the Dijkgraaf–Witten invariant for the finite abelian group ℤ/k​ℤ\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z}. Up to standard normalization conventions, it takes the form:

    (57) ZRT​(M,k)=k−b1​(M)/2​∑γ∈H1​(M;ℤ/k​ℤ)e2​π​i​k⋅q​(γ),Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M,k)=k^{-b_{1}(M)/2}\sum_{\gamma\in H^{1}(M;\,\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z})}e^{2\pi ik\cdot q(\gamma)},

    where q:H1​(M;ℤ/k​ℤ)→ℚ/ℤq:H^{1}(M;\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z})\to\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z} is the linking form and b1​(M)b_{1}(M) is the first Betti number.

  4. (iv)

    The BV-BFV perturbative expansion is one-loop exact. Indeed, the abelian Chern–Simons action CS​(A)=14​π​∫MA∧d​A\mathrm{CS}(A)=\frac{1}{4\pi}\int_{M}A\wedge dA is quadratic in the connection, so there are no cubic vertices and hence no Feynman diagrams beyond one loop. All higher-order coefficients vanish, i.e., an=0a_{n}=0 for n≥1n\geq 1. The perturbative partition function ZApert=e2​π​i​k​CS​(A)⋅τA1/2⋅ei​π​ηA/4Z^{\mathrm{pert}}_{A}=e^{2\pi ik\,\mathrm{CS}(A)}\cdot\tau_{A}^{1/2}\cdot e^{i\pi\eta_{A}/4}, summed over flat connections A∈H1​(M;U​(1))A\in H^{1}(M;U(1)), reproduces ZRT​(M,k)Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M,k) exactly. No resurgence is needed because the perturbative expansion already gives the exact answer.

  5. (v)

    The Koszul reconstruction (Conjecture 7.4) becomes tautological in this case. The character stack (S1)2​g(S^{1})^{2g} is smooth, so the distinction between 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}} and 𝐐𝐂𝐨𝐡\mathrm{\mathbf{QCoh}} disappears, and the formal completion equals the stack itself. The non-trivial content of the conjecture (the gluing of perturbative data across distinct flat connections) is invisible here because the perturbative expansion is already exact.

  6. (vi)

    The factorization homology computation also simplifies because we get

    ∬Σg𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭ℤ/k​ℤ≃(𝐇,uΣg)\iint_{\Sigma_{g}}\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}_{\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z}}\simeq(\mathbf{H},\,u_{\Sigma_{g}})

    with dimuΣg=kg\dim u_{\Sigma_{g}}=k^{g}, matching the Verlinde formula for U​(1)U(1) at level kk.

Remark 9.2.

The abelian case is instructive precisely because of its simplicity. The perturbative expansion is exact, so the “gap” between perturbative and non-perturbative that motivates this paper vanishes entirely. In particular, there are no divergent series, no Stokes phenomena, no non-trivial Koszul duality, and no resurgent corrections. The passage from BV-BFV to RT is a straightforward summation.

The non-abelian case is fundamentally different. The cubic vertex produces an infinite sequence of Feynman diagrams, the perturbative series diverges, and the relationship between the perturbative and non-perturbative sides becomes genuinely non-trivial. Nevertheless, all of the structural features of our program (the character stack with its shifted symplectic structure, the deformation quantization producing the MTC, the factorization homology recovering the RT state spaces, and the Koszul reconstruction assembling local data into a global category) are already visible in the abelian case. The non-abelian generalization replaces each of these simple instances with a richer and more subtle version of the same structure.

9.2. Genus zero: Σ=S2\Sigma=S^{2}

The genus-zero case is the simplest non-trivial check of our framework for a non-abelian group, and it illustrates how the derived structure of the character stack interacts with the triviality of the fundamental group.

Proposition 9.3.

For Σ=S2\Sigma=S^{2}, all frameworks agree and produce a one-dimensional state space:

  1. (i)

    Since π1​(S2)=0\pi_{1}(S^{2})=0, the character stack LocG​(S2)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(S^{2}) has a unique point (the trivial flat connection) up to gauge equivalence. As a derived stack, however, it is not simply a point. It is LocG​(S2)≅B​G\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(S^{2})\cong BG, the classifying stack of GG. The tangent complex at the unique point is 𝔤​[1]→0𝔤​[2]\mathfrak{g}[1]\xrightarrow{0}\mathfrak{g}[2], reflecting the cohomology H∙​(S2;𝔤)=𝔤⊕𝔤​[−2]H^{\bullet}(S^{2};\,\mathfrak{g})=\mathfrak{g}\oplus\mathfrak{g}[-2]. The derived structure, though the classical truncation is a single point, is non-trivial. In particular, it remembers the automorphism group GG of the trivial connection and the 2-dimensional obstruction space H2​(S2;𝔤)≅𝔤H^{2}(S^{2};\,\mathfrak{g})\cong\mathfrak{g}.

  2. (ii)

    The factorization homology ∬S2𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\iint_{S^{2}}\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) can be computed using excision applied to the decomposition S2=D+2∪S1D−2S^{2}=D^{2}_{+}\cup_{S^{1}}D^{2}_{-} into two hemispheres glued along the equator. Since ∬D2𝒞≃𝒞\iint_{D^{2}}\mathscr{C}\simeq\mathscr{C} and ∫S1𝒞≃𝒵​(𝒞)\int_{S^{1}}\mathscr{C}\simeq\mathcal{Z}(\mathscr{C}) (the Drinfeld center), excision gives ∬S2𝒞≃𝒞⊗𝒵​(𝒞)𝒞\iint_{S^{2}}\mathscr{C}\simeq\mathscr{C}\otimes_{\mathcal{Z}(\mathscr{C})}\mathscr{C}. For a modular tensor category, this relative tensor product is equivalent to 𝐇\mathbf{H} (the category of finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces in the unitary setting, or 𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}} in the algebraic setting), reflecting the fact that for a modular tensor category, the canonical functor 𝒞⊠𝒞¯→𝒵​(𝒞)\mathscr{C}\boxtimes\overline{\mathscr{C}}\to\mathcal{Z}(\mathscr{C}) is an equivalence, from which it follows that the relative tensor product is equivalent to 𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}. The distinguished object uS2u_{S^{2}} of Theorem 4.12 is one-dimensional, consistent with VRT​(S2)=ℂV_{\mathrm{RT}}(S^{2})=\mathbb{C}.

  3. (iii)

    Both the RT state space V​(S2)=ℂV(S^{2})=\mathbb{C} and the BFV state space ℋS2BV=ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathcal{H}_{S^{2}}^{\mathrm{BV}}=\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!] are one-dimensional. Upon specialization ℏ↦2​π​i/(k+h∨)\hbar\mapsto 2\pi i/(k+h^{\vee}), they agree. The Verlinde formula (26) confirms this independently since we have dimV​(S2)=1\dim V(S^{2})=1 for all levels kk and all groups GG.

  4. (iv)

    From the BV-BFV perspective, the BFV phase space for S2S^{2} is

    T∗​[−1]​(Ω∙​(S2,𝔤)​[1]),T^{*}[-1](\Omega^{\bullet}(S^{2},\mathfrak{g})[1]),

    and the physical state space (the BFV cohomology) is one-dimensional. This can be understood as follows. The only flat connection on S2S^{2} is the trivial one, its stabilizer is all of GG, and the reduced phase space pt/G\mathrm{pt}/G has a one-dimensional quantization corresponding to the trivial representation.

9.3. Genus one: Σ=T2\Sigma=T^{2}

The torus is the first case where the character variety has positive dimension, and it provides a rich testing ground for our conjectures. We specialize to G=S​U​(2)G=SU(2), the simplest non-abelian group.

Proposition 9.4.

For G=S​U​(2)G=SU(2) at level kk, the three frameworks (BV-BFV, factorization homology, and RT) all produce a (k+1)(k+1)-dimensional state space for the torus, with matching S​L2​(ℤ)SL_{2}(\mathbb{Z})-representations:

  1. (i)

    The character variety. Flat S​U​(2)SU(2)-connections on T2T^{2} are pairs of commuting elements (A,B)∈S​U​(2)2(A,B)\in SU(2)^{2} modulo simultaneous conjugation. Since commuting elements in S​U​(2)SU(2) must lie in a common maximal torus U​(1)⊂S​U​(2)U(1)\subset SU(2), we may write A=diag​(ei​α,e−i​α)A=\mathrm{diag}(e^{i\alpha},e^{-i\alpha}) and B=diag​(ei​β,e−i​β)B=\mathrm{diag}(e^{i\beta},e^{-i\beta}), with α,β∈[0,π]\alpha,\beta\in[0,\pi]. The residual Weyl group ℤ/2​ℤ\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z} acts by (α,β)↦(−α,−β)(\alpha,\beta)\mapsto(-\alpha,-\beta), and the quotient [0,π]2/∼[0,\pi]^{2}/\sim is the “pillowcase”, i.e., topologically a 2-sphere with four orbifold points at the corners (α,β)∈{0,π}2(\alpha,\beta)\in\{0,\pi\}^{2}, where the stabilizer jumps from U​(1)U(1) (generic points) to all of S​U​(2)SU(2) (the four fixed points of the Weyl reflection).

  2. (ii)

    The Verlinde formula. The RT state space V​(T2)V(T^{2}) has dimension k+1k+1. This equals the number of integrable highest-weight representations of the affine Lie algebra 𝔰​𝔲​(2)^\widehat{\mathfrak{su}(2)} at level kk, corresponding to spins j=0,12,1,…,k2j=0,\frac{1}{2},1,\ldots,\frac{k}{2}.

  3. (iii)

    Factorization homology. By Theorem 4.12, ∬T2𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(𝔰​𝔩2)≃(𝐇,uT2)\iint_{T^{2}}\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(\mathfrak{sl}_{2})\simeq(\mathbf{H},u_{T^{2}}) where the distinguished object uT2u_{T^{2}} has dimension k+1k+1. This can also be seen from the non-semisimplified computation:

    ∬T2𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)≃𝒟q​(G/G)​-​𝐦𝐨𝐝\iint_{T^{2}}\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G)\simeq\mathcal{D}_{q}(G/G)\text{-}\mathrm{\mathbf{mod}}

    (Theorem 4.11), which upon semisimplification collapses to 𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}} with a (k+1)(k+1)-dimensional distinguished object.

  4. (iv)

    Geometric quantization. The same dimension k+1k+1 is obtained by geometric quantization of the pillowcase. The Bohr–Sommerfeld condition selects k+1k+1 orbits at α=j​π/(k+2)\alpha=j\pi/(k+2) for j=1,…,k+1j=1,\ldots,k+1 (the boundary values j=0j=0 and j=k+2j=k+2 are excluded due to the orbifold structure). These orbits are in bijection with the simple objects of 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(𝔰​𝔩2)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(\mathfrak{sl}_{2}). This has been verified rigorously by Andersen [6]; see also [48, 8] for the geometric quantization framework.

  5. (v)

    Mapping class group representations. The mapping class group of the torus is MCG⁡(T2)=S​L2​(ℤ)\operatorname{MCG}(T^{2})=SL_{2}(\mathbb{Z}), generated by the modular transformations τ↦−1/τ\tau\mapsto-1/\tau (the SS-transformation) and τ↦τ+1\tau\mapsto\tau+1 (the TT-transformation). All three frameworks produce the same projective S​L2​(ℤ)SL_{2}(\mathbb{Z})-representation on the (k+1)(k+1)-dimensional state space, with generators:

    (58) Sj​j′\displaystyle S_{jj^{\prime}} =2k+2​sin⁡((j+1)​(j′+1)​πk+2),\displaystyle=\sqrt{\frac{2}{k+2}}\;\sin\left(\frac{(j+1)(j^{\prime}+1)\pi}{k+2}\right),
    (59) Tj​j′\displaystyle T_{jj^{\prime}} =δj​j′​e2​π​i​(j​(j+2)/4​(k+2)− 1/8).\displaystyle=\delta_{jj^{\prime}}\;e^{2\pi i\left(j(j+2)/4(k+2)\,-\,1/8\right)}.

    These matrices satisfy the projective relations S4=𝟏S^{4}=\mathbf{1} and (S​T)3=S2(ST)^{3}=S^{2}, with the projectivity determined by the central charge c=3​k/(k+2)c=3k/(k+2) of the 𝔰​𝔲​(2)^k\widehat{\mathfrak{su}(2)}_{k} WZW model. The agreement between the RT prescription, the BV-BFV quantization of the modular transformations on the pillowcase, and the factorization homology autoequivalences is a strong consistency check on the program.

9.4. Mapping class group representations

A particularly stringent test of any TQFT-like construction is whether it produces the correct representations of mapping class groups. Since diffeomorphisms of a surface act as symmetries in all three of our frameworks, the resulting representations must agree if the frameworks are to be identified.

Proposition 9.5.

For a closed surface Σg\Sigma_{g} with g≥1g\geq 1, all three frameworks produce projective representations of the mapping class group MCG⁡(Σg)\operatorname{MCG}(\Sigma_{g}), and these representations are identified under our conjectural equivalence.

  1. (i)

    The RT side. The RT theory assigns a projective representation ρRT:MCG⁡(Σg)→P​G​L​(V​(Σg))\rho_{\mathrm{RT}}:\operatorname{MCG}(\Sigma_{g})\to PGL(V(\Sigma_{g})) via the cobordism formalism. For a mapping class φ∈MCG⁡(Σg)\varphi\in\operatorname{MCG}(\Sigma_{g}), one forms the mapping cylinder Mφ=Σg×[0,1]/(x,0)∼(φ​(x),1)M_{\varphi}=\Sigma_{g}\times[0,1]\,/\,(x,0)\sim(\varphi(x),1), which is a cobordism from Σg\Sigma_{g} to itself. The RT partition function ZRT​(Mφ):V​(Σg)→V​(Σg)Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(M_{\varphi}):V(\Sigma_{g})\to V(\Sigma_{g}) is the desired operator. Composition of mapping classes corresponds to gluing of mapping cylinders, which by the TQFT axiom corresponds to composition of linear maps, giving the representation.

  2. (ii)

    The BV-BFV side. A diffeomorphism φ\varphi of Σg\Sigma_{g} acts as a symplectomorphism of the character variety LocG​(Σg)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma_{g}), preserving the Atiyah–Bott symplectic form. Upon quantization (either geometric or deformation), this symplectomorphism lifts to a projective unitary operator on the quantum Hilbert space. The representation is only projective, not linear, because the quantization requires a choice of metaplectic correction (half-form bundle), and different choices are related by the central extension.

  3. (iii)

    The factorization homology side. A diffeomorphism φ:Σg→∼Σg\varphi:\Sigma_{g}\xrightarrow{\sim}\Sigma_{g} acts on factorization homology by functoriality. In particular, it induces an autoequivalence φ∗:∬Σg𝒞→∼∬Σg𝒞\varphi_{*}:\iint_{\Sigma_{g}}\mathscr{C}\xrightarrow{\sim}\iint_{\Sigma_{g}}\mathscr{C}, and the induced map on the Grothendieck group gives the projective representation on V​(Σg)V(\Sigma_{g}).

The projective ambiguity, given by the central extension of MCG⁡(Σg)\operatorname{MCG}(\Sigma_{g}) by which the representation is twisted, is the same in all three cases, determined by the central charge c=k​dim(G)/(k+h∨)c=k\dim(G)/(k+h^{\vee}) of the associated Wess–Zumino–Witten conformal field theory.

An important rigidity result confirms that these representations carry substantial topological information.

Theorem 9.6 (Andersen [6]; see also [7]).

The RT representations of mapping class groups are asymptotically faithful, i.e., for every non-trivial mapping class φ∈MCG⁡(Σg)\varphi\in\operatorname{MCG}(\Sigma_{g}), there exists a level k0k_{0} (depending on φ\varphi) such that ρRT(k)​(φ)≠id\rho_{\mathrm{RT}}^{(k)}(\varphi)\neq\mathrm{id} for all k≥k0k\geq k_{0}.

This result is consistent with the geometric quantization picture underlying the BV-BFV approach. In fact, as the level k→∞k\to\infty (equivalently, ℏ→0\hbar\to 0), the quantum representations converge to the classical action of MCG⁡(Σg)\operatorname{MCG}(\Sigma_{g}) on the character variety LocG​(Σg)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma_{g}), and this classical action is faithful by a theorem of Goldman. The asymptotic faithfulness theorem says that no information is lost in the quantization (at least not permanently, since any given mapping class is detected at sufficiently high level).

9.5. Lens spaces

Lens spaces provide the most extensively studied family of examples for testing the asymptotic expansion conjecture (2), because both the RT invariant and the perturbative Chern–Simons invariant can be computed in closed form.

Proposition 9.7.

For the lens space L​(p,1)L(p,1) with gauge group G=S​U​(2)G=SU(2) the following hold.

  1. (i)

    The RT invariant is given explicitly by the surgery formula applied to the unknot with framing pp:

    (60) ZRT​(L​(p,1),k)=2p​(k+2)​∑j=0ksin2⁡((j+1)​πk+2)​e−π​i​j​(j+2)​p/(2​(k+2)).Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(L(p,1),k)=\sqrt{\frac{2}{p(k+2)}}\sum_{j=0}^{k}\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{(j+1)\pi}{k+2}\right)e^{-\pi i\,j(j+2)\,p\,/\,(2(k+2))}.
  2. (ii)

    The flat connections on a general lens space L​(p,q)L(p,q) are representations of
    π1​(L​(p,q))=ℤ/p​ℤ\pi_{1}(L(p,q))=\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z} into S​U​(2)SU(2), which are classified (up to conjugation) by the image of the generator, that is an element of order dividing pp in S​U​(2)SU(2), modulo the Weyl group. These are labeled by m=0,1,…,⌊p/2⌋m=0,1,\ldots,\lfloor p/2\rfloor, where m=0m=0 is the trivial connection and m≥1m\geq 1 are the irreducible (non-central) representations.

  3. (iii)

    The perturbative expansion around the trivial connection A0A_{0} gives a one-loop contribution τA0​(L​(p,q))1/2=p−1/2\tau_{A_{0}}(L(p,q))^{1/2}=p^{-1/2} (the Reidemeister torsion) times a perturbative series

    (61) 1+∑n=1∞an​(L​(p,q))​k−n,1+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_{n}(L(p,q))\,k^{-n},

    whose coefficients ana_{n} are computable as configuration space integrals (Feynman diagrams) on L​(p,q)L(p,q). Similar expansions exist around each non-trivial flat connection, with different torsion prefactors and Chern–Simons phases.

  4. (iv)

    Jeffrey [49] verified that the asymptotic expansion of ZRT​(L​(p,q),k)Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(L(p,q),k) as k→∞k\to\infty matches the sum over flat connections in (2) to all orders in k−1k^{-1}. This provides one of the strongest pieces of evidence for the asymptotic expansion conjecture and is consistent with the algebraic bridge proposed in this paper. However, we note that the asymptotic matching, while necessary for our conjectures to hold, does not by itself establish the algebraic identification at the categorical level that our program requires.

9.6. Seifert fibered spaces

Seifert fibered spaces, which are 3-manifolds admitting a circle fibration with finitely many exceptional fibers, form a broad and computationally accessible class of examples that interpolate between the simple (lens spaces) and the general.

Proposition 9.8.

For Seifert fibered spaces Σ​(a1,…,an)\Sigma(a_{1},\ldots,a_{n}) with exceptional fiber types a1,…,ana_{1},\ldots,a_{n} the following hold.

  1. (i)

    Matching of invariants. The RT invariant and the perturbative Chern–Simons invariant have been computed independently by Hansen [47], Mariño [55], and others, using the surgery formula on the RT side and matrix integral techniques on the perturbative side. In all cases where the comparison has been carried out, the two sides agree: the asymptotic expansion of the RT invariant matches the sum over flat connections predicted by (2).

  2. (ii)

    Structure of the character variety. The character variety of a Seifert fibered space has a particularly tractable structure, owing to the S1S^{1}-fibration. Flat connections decompose into abelian components (where the holonomy around the generic fiber is central in GG) and non-abelian components (where it is not). This decomposition is compatible with the BV-BFV formalism: the perturbative data at each component can be computed explicitly in terms of the Seifert invariants (a1,…,an)(a_{1},\ldots,a_{n}) and the representation theory of GG, making Seifert manifolds an ideal testing ground for the conjectures of this paper.

  3. (iii)

    Resurgence for the Poincaré homology sphere. The most striking evidence comes from the Poincaré homology sphere Σ​(2,3,5)\Sigma(2,3,5), studied in Example 8.2. This manifold has exactly two S​U​(2)SU(2) flat connections (one trivial and one irreducible) and the resurgent structure of the perturbative series around both has been analyzed in detail by Gukov, Mariño, and Putrov [41]. The alien derivatives connecting the two perturbative sectors have been computed explicitly to high order, and they precisely account for the difference between the Borel-resummed perturbative contributions and the exact RT invariant. This demonstrates concretely that the full non-perturbative content of ZRT​(Σ​(2,3,5))Z_{\mathrm{RT}}(\Sigma(2,3,5)) is reconstructable from the perturbative data together with the Stokes constants, exactly as predicted by the resurgence–Koszul correspondence (Conjecture 8.3).

10. Outlook and Future Directions

We conclude by discussing the remaining gaps in the program, the prospects for closing them, and the broader implications of the framework for higher-dimensional topology, categorification, and the geometric Langlands program.

10.1. Toward a proof of the main conjecture

We are candid about the status of the program. Of the four steps in our proof strategy (Section 6), Step 1 is the most well-understood but still lacks a fully rigorous derived-geometric treatment. Step 2, the comparison of BV-BFV quantization with deformation quantization of shifted symplectic stacks (Conjecture 6.11), is the central open problem and requires substantial new ideas. Steps 3 and 4 would follow from general machinery once Step 2 is secured, but each involves non-trivial technical points (commutativity of semisimplification with factorization homology for Step 3; quantization of Lagrangian correspondences for Step 4) that are not yet resolved in the literature. The program as a whole should be regarded as a framework for organizing existing results and identifying the precise technical challenges, rather than as a near-complete proof. We identify three principal technical challenges.

  1. (a)

    A derived BV-BFV formalism. The BV-BFV formalism as developed by Cattaneo–Mnev–Reshetikhin is formulated in the language of differential geometry and infinite-dimensional functional analysis. In particular, the spaces of fields are Fréchet manifolds, the BV Laplacian is a functional-analytic operator, and the partition function is defined via regularized path integrals. A complete proof of Conjecture 6.11 requires reformulating this entire apparatus in derived algebraic geometry, where it can be compared directly with the Safronov deformation quantization. The work of Gwilliam and Rejzner [44] on BV quantization via factorization algebras, and of Gwilliam and Haugseng [45] on linear BV quantization as a functor of ∞\infty-categories, provide significant starting points. They show that the BV quantization of a field theory on a manifold without boundary produces a factorization algebra of observables. However, the boundary (BFV) side remains to be developed in the derived setting. In particular, one needs a derived version of the BFV state space that produces an (∞,1)(\infty,1)-category (rather than a vector space or cochain complex), and the modified quantum master equation must be reformulated as a condition on objects in this derived category rather than as a differential equation on functions.

  2. (b)

    A universal property for the BV pushforward. The BV pushforward (the fiber integration that produces the partition function from the BV action) is currently defined as a concrete integral, i.e., a choice of gauge-fixing Lagrangian and a regularization procedure. For comparison with deformation quantization, what is needed is a characterization of the BV pushforward in terms of a universal property within derived algebraic geometry. It should be the unique quantization compatible with the Lagrangian structure. We expect that the (−1)(-1)-shifted Lagrangian structure of Theorem 5.9 encodes precisely this universal property, i.e., the quantized BV pushforward should be the unique bimodule over the quantized boundary algebras that is compatible with the Lagrangian morphism LocG​(M)→LocG​(∂M)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)\to\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\partial M). Making this expectation precise requires developing a theory of “quantized Lagrangian correspondences” in the shifted symplectic setting, hence a categorified and derived version of the Guillemin–Sternberg “quantization commutes with reduction” principle.

  3. (c)

    Root of unity specialization. Even after the formal comparison is established (matching the BV-BFV and deformation quantization outputs over ℂ​[[ℏ]]\mathbb{C}[\![\hbar]\!]), the passage to actual values of the quantum parameter remains non-trivial. At a root of unity q=ei​π/(k+h∨)q=e^{i\pi/(k+h^{\vee})}, the category 𝐑𝐞𝐩q​(G)\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}_{q}(G) is no longer semisimple, and the modular tensor category is obtained only after semisimplification, i.e., the quotient by the tensor ideal 𝒩\mathcal{N} of negligible morphisms. This categorical localization interacts non-trivially with the other structures in our program. The key technical requirement is that semisimplification commutes with factorization homology giving

    (62) ∬Σ(𝒜/𝒩)≃(∬Σ𝒜)/𝒩Σ,\iint_{\Sigma}\big(\mathcal{A}\,/\,\mathcal{N}\big)\;\simeq\;\Big(\iint_{\Sigma}\mathcal{A}\Big)\big/\,\mathcal{N}_{\Sigma},

    where 𝒩\mathcal{N} is the negligible ideal and 𝒩Σ\mathcal{N}_{\Sigma} is its image under factorization homology. Without this commutativity, the chain of identifications in Step 3 would break down. Establishing it requires understanding how tensor ideals in braided monoidal categories interact with the colimit that defines factorization homology. This is a question that, to our knowledge, has not been addressed in the literature.

10.2. Higher-categorical extensions

Our framework extends naturally to the fully extended TQFT setting, where data is assigned not only to surfaces and 3-manifolds but all the way down to points. The Cobordism Hypothesis of Baez–Dolan, proven by Lurie [53], asserts that a fully extended nn-dimensional TQFT is completely determined by the object it assigns to a point, which must be fully dualizable in the target higher category. Applied to our setting, this yields the following conjecture.

Conjecture 10.1.

The fully extended 3-dimensional TQFT corresponding to the RT theory assigns

  1. (i)

    to a point the 𝔼3\mathbb{E}_{3}-algebra 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G), viewed as a fully dualizable object in the (∞,3)(\infty,3)-category of braided monoidal categories.

  2. (ii)

    to a circle the Drinfeld center 𝒵​(𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G))\mathcal{Z}(\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G)).

  3. (iii)

    to a closed surface Σ\Sigma the factorization homology ∬Σ𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\iint_{\Sigma}\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G).

  4. (iv)

    to a closed 3-manifold MM the RT invariant ZRT​(M)∈ℂZ_{\mathrm{RT}}(M)\in\mathbb{C}.

The 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) produced by BV-BFV quantization on the disk (Construction 6.2) is promoted to an 𝔼3\mathbb{E}_{3}-algebra (the value at a point in the fully extended theory) by the full dualizability result of [16] and the Cobordism Hypothesis. Alternatively, the 𝔼3\mathbb{E}_{3}-structure can be understood as arising from BV-BFV quantization of the 4-dimensional Crane–Yetter theory on D3×[0,1]D^{3}\times[0,1], where the 3-dimensional spatial slice D3D^{3} provides three independent directions of disk embeddings; see [50] for this perspective.

Remark 10.2.

The claim that 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) is fully dualizable in the (∞,3)(\infty,3)-category of braided monoidal categories is a non-trivial statement, requiring the existence of duals at every categorical level. At the level of objects, this is the rigidity (existence of duals) of the fusion category. At the level of 1-morphisms (functors between module categories) this requires the existence of adjoints, which amounts to the semisimplicity and finiteness of the module categories. At the level of 2-morphisms, it requires a sphericality or pivotality condition on the category. For modular tensor categories, full dualizability has been established by Douglas, Schommer-Pries, and Snyder [30], and extended to braided tensor categories by Brochier, Jordan, and Snyder [16], who showed more generally that all fusion categories are fully dualizable, confirming that the Cobordism Hypothesis applies to the RT theory.

10.3. 4-dimensional implications

Looking one dimension higher, our framework makes predictions about 4-dimensional topology. The Crane–Yetter / Broda invariant of a closed 4-manifold WW,

(63) ZCY​(W)=𝒟χ​(W)⋅κ−σ​(W),Z_{\mathrm{CY}}(W)=\mathcal{D}^{\chi(W)}\cdot\kappa^{-\sigma(W)},

depends only on the Euler characteristic χ​(W)\chi(W) and signature σ​(W)\sigma(W), making it a relatively coarse invariant. Within our framework, it should arise from the BV-BFV quantization of 4-dimensional BF theory, via the 𝔼3\mathbb{E}_{3}-structure on 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) integrated over 4-manifolds by factorization homology. This perspective connects to the program of Freed–Hopkins–Lurie–Teleman [33] on constructing fully extended TQFTs from compact Lie groups, and suggests that the Crane–Yetter theory is the shadow of a richer 4-dimensional structure. The factorization homology approach to the Crane–Yetter theory is developed in [50]. The slogan that the Witten–Reshetikhin–Turaev 3-TQFT is a boundary condition for the Crane–Yetter 4-TQFT has been recently established in [46], including a generalization to the non-semisimple setting [25].

The more interesting invariants arise when one considers defects, which are embedded surfaces or links inside the 4-manifold, and this leads to categorification.

Conjecture 10.3 (Categorification).

Khovanov homology of a link L⊂S3L\subset S^{3} is the value of a fully extended 4-dimensional TQFT on the link complement S3∖LS^{3}\setminus L, viewed as a 3-manifold with boundary. The relevant 4-dimensional theory is associated to an 𝔼4\mathbb{E}_{4}-algebra obtained from BV-BFV quantization of 4-dimensional Chern–Simons theory (in the sense of Costello) or Kapustin–Witten theory.

The logic of this conjecture is as follows. The Jones polynomial of a link is a number, namely, the value of the 3-dimensional RT theory on the link complement. Khovanov homology is a graded vector space whose graded Euler characteristic recovers the Jones polynomial. This passage from a number to a vector space is precisely what happens when one lifts a 3-dimensional TQFT to a 4-dimensional one. The 3-manifold that was previously assigned a number (as a closed manifold in the 3d theory) is now a boundary component of a 4-manifold and is assigned a vector space. In other words, categorification is the dimensional lift from (2+1)(2{+}1) to (3+1)(3{+}1) dimensions. Our framework, which connects the BV-BFV formalism to topological invariants via factorization homology, should extend to this 4-dimensional setting, with the 𝔼3\mathbb{E}_{3}-algebra 𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G)\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G) of the 3d theory replaced by an 𝔼4\mathbb{E}_{4}-algebra governing the 4d theory.

10.4. Arithmetic and the geometric Langlands program

The derived character stack LocG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\Sigma) that plays the central role in our program admits several variants, depending on the cohomology theory used to define “local systems.” These variants are deeply interrelated, and their relationships connect our work to one of the grand programs in modern mathematics called the geometric Langlands correspondence.

The three principal incarnations of the character stack are:

  1. (i)

    Betti: LocGB​(Σ)=Map⁡(Σ,B​G)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}^{B}(\Sigma)=\operatorname{Map}(\Sigma,BG), parametrizing flat connections (equivalently, local systems, or representations of π1\pi_{1}). This is the version we have used throughout this paper; it depends only on the homotopy type of Σ\Sigma.

  2. (ii)

    de Rham: LocGd​R​(Σ)=ConnG​(Σ)/𝒢\mathrm{Loc}_{G}^{dR}(\Sigma)=\mathrm{Conn}_{G}(\Sigma)/\mathcal{G}, parametrizing all algebraic connections modulo gauge equivalence (not just flat ones). This requires a choice of algebraic structure on Σ\Sigma and is the natural home for DD-modules and differential equations.

  3. (iii)

    Dolbeault: LocGD​o​l​(Σ)=HiggsG​(Σ)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}^{Dol}(\Sigma)=\mathrm{Higgs}_{G}(\Sigma), parametrizing Higgs bundles, which are pairs of a holomorphic bundle and a holomorphic section of the twisted endomorphism bundle. This requires a complex structure on Σ\Sigma.

For surfaces, these three moduli spaces are related by deep and largely conjectural correspondences. The Riemann–Hilbert correspondence relates Betti and de Rham (flat connections to DD-modules), while the non-abelian Hodge correspondence of Hitchin, Donaldson, Corlette, and Simpson relates de Rham and Dolbeault (flat connections to Higgs bundles). Together they form a triangle of equivalences that is central to the geometric Langlands program.

The geometric Langlands conjecture, in its strongest categorical form (due to Beilinson–Drinfeld, developed by Gaitsgory and collaborators), posits an equivalence of derived categories

(64) 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG∨d​R​(Σ))≃D​-​𝐦𝐨𝐝​(BunG​(Σ)),\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}\big(\mathrm{Loc}_{G^{\vee}}^{dR}(\Sigma)\big)\;\simeq\;D\mathrm{\text{-}\mathbf{mod}}\big(\mathrm{Bun}_{G}(\Sigma)\big),

where G∨G^{\vee} is the Langlands dual group and BunG​(Σ)\mathrm{Bun}_{G}(\Sigma) is the moduli stack of GG-bundles on Σ\Sigma. This is an equivalence between “spectral” data (sheaves on the space of Langlands-dual local systems) and “automorphic” data (DD-modules on the space of bundles).

Our Koszul reconstruction conjecture (Conjecture 7.4) asserts that

(65) ℬCS≃𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧),\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}\;\simeq\;\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}\big(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}\big),

identifying the category of Chern–Simons boundary conditions with ind-coherent sheaves on the character stack. This has a natural Langlands-dual reading. The category of boundary conditions of Chern–Simons theory with gauge group GG should be related, via the Langlands correspondence, to DD-modules on BunG∨​(Σ)\mathrm{Bun}_{G^{\vee}}(\Sigma). In this way, our program can be viewed as a quantized, 3-dimensional analogue of geometric Langlands. The 2-dimensional Langlands duality (between local systems and DD-modules on a surface) is lifted to a 3-dimensional duality (between Chern–Simons boundary conditions and a yet-to-be-defined “automorphic” category on 3-manifolds). This perspective is closely related to the “Betti geometric Langlands” program of Ben-Zvi and Nadler [14], which works with the Betti (topological) version of the character stack rather than the de Rham (algebraic) version, and to the foundational work of Gaitsgory and Lurie [38]. For the loop space perspective on character stacks, see [15]; the quantum geometric Langlands conjecture, which provides a direct quantized analogue of the classical correspondence, is formulated in [36]; and the role of singular support in the geometric Langlands conjecture is developed in [1]. Developing these connections further is an exciting direction for future work.

10.5. Summary of conjectures

For the reader’s convenience, we collect the seven conjectures formulated in this paper and describe their logical interdependencies.

  1. C1.

    Conjecture 6.5 (𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-equivalence): The BV-BFV category of boundary conditions is equivalent to the quantum group representation category as 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-algebras:

    ℬCS(k)≃𝔼2𝐑𝐞𝐩¯q​(G).\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}^{(k)}\simeq_{\mathbb{E}_{2}}\overline{\mathrm{\mathbf{Rep}}}_{q}(G).
  2. C2.

    Conjecture 6.8 (Main Conjecture): The non-perturbative BV-BFV and RT constructions agree as (3​-​2​-​1)(3\text{-}2\text{-}1)-extended TQFTs valued in 2​-​𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭2\text{-}\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}:

    ZBV,np(k)≃ZRT(k):𝐁𝐨𝐫𝐝3ext→2​-​𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭.Z_{\mathrm{BV,np}}^{(k)}\simeq Z_{\mathrm{RT}}^{(k)}:\mathrm{\mathbf{Bord}}_{3}^{\mathrm{ext}}\to 2\text{-}\mathrm{\mathbf{Vect}}.
  3. C3.

    Conjecture 6.11 (Quantization agreement): BV-BFV quantization of Chern–Simons theory on the cylinder D2×[0,1]D^{2}\times[0,1] agrees with shifted deformation quantization of the 11-shifted symplectic stack [G/G][G/G] at the 𝔼1\mathbb{E}_{1}-level (monoidal categories).

  4. C4.

    Conjecture 6.14 (Quantized Lagrangian correspondences): The Lagrangian morphism

    LocG​(M)→LocG​(∂M)\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)\to\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(\partial M)

    quantizes to the BV-BFV partition function.

  5. C5.

    Conjecture 6.16 (Cellular compatibility): The cellular BV-BFV partition function on a triangulation agrees with the factorization homology colimit.

  6. C6.

    Conjecture 7.4 (Koszul reconstruction): The category of Chern–Simons boundary conditions is equivalent to ind-coherent sheaves on the formal completion of the character stack:

    ℬCS≃𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧).\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{CS}}\;\simeq\;\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}\big(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}\big).
  7. C7.

    Conjecture 8.3 (Resurgence–Koszul correspondence): The Stokes automorphisms of the resurgent perturbative series are identified with the Koszul duality transition maps in 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐂𝐨𝐡​(LocG​(M)∧)\mathrm{\mathbf{IndCoh}}(\mathrm{Loc}_{G}(M)^{\wedge}).

These seven conjectures form a coherent web of mutually reinforcing statements. The logical structure is as follows: C3 (quantization agreement) implies C1 (𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-equivalence), which combined with factorization homology and the AKZ theorem gives the surface-level identifications of C2. However, the full Main Conjecture C2 (including cobordism maps) additionally requires C4 (quantized Lagrangian correspondences), which is logically independent of C1. C4 (quantized Lagrangian correspondences) is required to extend C2 from surface-level data to cobordism maps; this is a substantial independent conjecture, not a formal consequence of C1. C5 (cellular compatibility) provides concrete computational access to the factorization homology side. C6 (Koszul reconstruction) gives the global algebraic framework in which all the perturbative data is assembled. C7 (resurgence–Koszul) connects the algebraic approach of C6 to the analytic approach via Borel resummation and Stokes analysis, showing that the two routes to the non-perturbative answer (the algebraic (Koszul duality) and the analytic (resurgence)) encode the same information.

The synthesis proposed in this paper views the Reshetikhin–Turaev invariants and BV-BFV perturbative quantization as two facets of a single geometric object. Namely, the derived character stack LocG\mathrm{Loc}_{G}, equipped with its shifted symplectic structure. Factorization homology provides the machine that translates between the local algebraic data (the 𝔼2\mathbb{E}_{2}-category at a point, the perturbative BV-BFV expansion around a flat connection) and the global topological invariant (the RT partition function, the non-perturbative Chern–Simons path integral). Koszul duality provides the algebraic mechanism for the passage from perturbative to non-perturbative. If this program can be carried out, it would establish that the fundamental gap between perturbative and non-perturbative quantum field theory (at least in the topological setting of Chern–Simons theory) is not primarily an analytic problem requiring the resummation of divergent series, but an algebraic one. In particular, the passage from local to global in the sheaf theory on derived moduli spaces. The evidence assembled in this paper, while far from constituting a proof, suggests that this perspective is worth pursuing seriously, and we hope that the precise conjectures formulated here will serve as a useful roadmap for future work.

Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors. Generated by L A T E xml .

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.
